title: Anatomical Terminology

title: Respiratory Anatomy
content: # Respiratory Anatomy ## Topics 1. Respiratory Airways 2. Pulmonary vasculature 3. Muscles of respiration

title: Deep Fascia
content: # Deep Fascia ## Background - Dense inelaseic connective tissue - separates subcutnaoeus tissue from underlying structures (e.g. muscles, etc.) - Connects to the intermuscular septa - this separates compartments of muscle groups (e.g. flexor, extensor compartments of the arm) - this also ensures the tendons are restrained and can perform its appropriate actions on the joints - thicker in palm and soles - protects underlying structures - connects tightly to skin for improved grip via fibrous septa of the subcutaneous layer - known as the *palmar aponeurosis and plantar aponeuroses*

title: Dermis histology
content: # Dermis histology ## Tissue - Connective tissue - Collagen, elastic fibres, - Epithelial/endothelial tissue - blood vessels, lymph nodes, - Nervous tissue - nerves, receptors ## Features - contains downgrowths of epithelium - Hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands - Thicker on back, posterior upper limb, anterior lower limb

title: Epithelial histology
content: # Epithelial histology ## Tissue - Stratified squamous epithelium ## Features - Avascular - Thicker on palms and soles, glaborous (no hair) and ridges for grip ## Layers - Stratum basale (innermost) - high mitotic activity - renews every 15-30 days - Stratum corneum - dead keratinocytes - forms a protective layer - reduces transpiration ## Derivatives of epidermis - Hair - sweat glands - sebaceous glands - nails

title: Skin
content: # Skin ## Background - A continuous waterproof external layer - acts as the first layer of the innate immune system - The largest organ in the body ## Function 1. Protection 2. Sensation - sensory nerve receptors 3. Thermoregulation 4. Vitamin D synthesis ## Components 1. Epidermis - Contains ducts for which glands can release content into 2. Dermis - contains glands (technically pouchings of the epidermis) - sebaceous - sweat - contains hair follicles (technically pouchings of the epidermis) - contains vessels - contains perve receptors

title: Bones Histology
content: # Bone Histology ## Background - Living tissue with blood, nerve and lymphatic supply ## Features - Can heal, remodel and respond to pressure changes - Made of two types of bone tissue - Cortical bone - Cancellous bone ## Cells of bone - Osteoclasts - Osteoblasts - Osteocytes --- # Cortical bone histology ## Features - Roughly cylindrical - shaft of long bones - light weight and resistant to bending, but not brittle --- # Cancellous bone histology *Also called trabecular or spongy bone* ## Features - Arranged along lines of stress - absorbs impact and shocks

title: Brainstem
content: # Brainstem ## Rule of 4s 1. 4 cranial nerves in medulla, 4 in pons, 4 above pons 2. 4 structure in midline beginning with M (Motor, Medial lemniscus, Medial longitudinal fasciculus, motor nucleus) 3. 4 structures to the side beginning with S (Spinocerebellar, spinothalamic, sensory ncueli of 5th CN, sympathetic tract) 4. 4 motor nuclei in midline are the factors of 12 (except 1 and 2) ## Blood supply of the brainstem 1. Medial brainstem - from paramedian branches 2. lateral brainstem - long circumferential branches Derived from - Superior cerebellar artery (SCA) - Anterior inferior cerebellar

title: Cerebellum
content: # Cerebellum ## Dysfunction caused by a hemispheric lesion - Ipsilateral limb ataxia - Past pointing - Dysmetria - Intention tremor - Dysdiachokinesia - Nystagmus ## Dysfunction caused by a Vermis lesion - Truncal ataxia - Nystagmus (if flucculonodular lobe affected)

title: Corticospinal tract
content: # Corticospinal tract 1. Primary motor cortex 2. internal capsule 3. crus cerebri 4. basis pontis 5. pyramids 6. Decussation inferior to pyramids 7. anterior horn of spinal cord 8. synapse in the anterior horn to exit through ventral rootlets

title: Dorsal column tract
content: # Dorsal column tract 1. Sensory nerve via DRG into dorsal column 1. dorsal column separated in the gracile (medial, lower limbs) and cuneate (lateral, upper limbs) columns 2. Decussation at level of lower medulla 3. travels up Medial Lemniscus 4. up pons, midbrain, synapse at thalamus (lateral nuclei) 5. travels to primary sensory cortex

title: Eye movements
content: When looking RIGHT: - L eye: Medial rectus - R eye: Lateral rectus When looking LEFT: - L eye: Lateral rectus - R eye: Medial rectus When looking UP and RIGHT - L eye: inferior oblique - R eye: Superior rectus When looking DOWN and RIGHT - L eye: Superior oblique - R eye: Inferior rectus Function of the superior oblique: Depression (especially when adducted) and intortion

title: Internal capsule
content: # Internal capsule Functions - Anterior limb - - Posterior limb and Genu - corticospinal tract and some sensory fibres Supplied by - lenticulostriate arteries from M1

title: Spinal cord and nerve roots
href: BCS/Anatomy and Histology/Neuroanatomy/Spinal cord and nerve roots.html
content: ## Anatomy - 31 spinal cord segments consisting of dorsal (sensory) and ventral (motor) roots - each segment corresponds to a vertebra except C8 which has no corresponding vertebra - The spinal nerve roots exit at the segment BENEATH the spinal vertebra - At the cervical level, the nerve roots are named as above the vertebra - at the thoracic level (Starting t1), nerve roots are named as below the vertebra

title: Spinal cord vasculature
content: # Spinal cord vasculature - Anterior and posterior circulation system - Spinal arteries supplied by - vertebral arteries - Intercostal branches of aorta in mid-thoracic region - Great radicular artery (of Adamkiewicz) in lower thoracic/lumbar region - Anterior circulation - anterior spinal artery (from vertebral arteries) supplies the anterior 2/3rds of spinal cord - has fewer but larger radicular arteries = more prone to ischemia - Posterior circulation - paired posterior spinal arteries (at posterior columns) serve posterior 1/3rd of spinal cord - flow from multiple branches

title: Spinothalamic tract
content: # Spinothalamic tract 1. Peripheral sensors → cell body at the DRG 2. synapse at the dorsal horn 3. decussation at LEVEL of nerve via anterior commissure 1. decussation occurs over 2-3 segments 4. spinothalamic tract via medulla, pons, midbrain 5. synapse at thalamus - lateral nuclei 6. via internal capsule and corona radiata to primary sensory cortex

title: Necrosis
content: # Necrosis ## Pathology 1. Cell swelling - This process results in membrane damage - This causes a $Ca^{2+}$ influx into the cells, which draws water in - this results in the cell to swell to the point where it 'explodes 2. Vacuolisation 3. Lysis - Cells will tend to spill contents into surrounding tissue - Leaked cellular contents will induces an inflammatory response

title: Anti-pyrexics in children
content: # Anti-pyrexics in children ### Paracetamol - Contraindications - Infants < 3 months (without prior consultation) - Dosage - NO LOADING DOSE - 10-15mg/kg per dose (max 1g per day) PO q4-6h - PK/PD - Activity in 30-60 minutes, peak effect in 3-4h - Duration 4-6h ### Ibuprofen - Contraindications - Infant <3 months - serious infection risk - Indications - Children ≥6 months of age - Dosage - 10-60mg/kg per dose (max dose 600-2400mg) PO q6h - PK/PD - Activity in 3-4h, lasts 6-8h - Adverse effects - Gastritis, GIT bleeding - AKI following ibuprofen

title: Fibrinolytics
content: ## Fibrinolytics ## Background - On activation of coagulation cascade - Endogenous plasminogen activators are also activated (E.g. tPA, urokinase, kalligrein, neutrophil elastase) - Simultaneously release of lipoprotein(a) inhibits tPA during clotting/coagulation - Plasminogen is deposited on fibrin strands within a thrombus (however not active) - Plasminogen activators (serine proteases) diffuse into thrombus and will cleave plasminogen (a plasma zymogen) to release plasmin within the thrombus - Plasmin (a trypsin-like protease) will digest fibrin, fibrinogen, Factors II, V, VIII and other pr

title: Thyroid replacement therapy
content: # Thyroid replacement therapy ## Thyroxine Key points - initial starting dose of levothyroxine should be lower in elderly patients and those with ischaemic heart disease. The BNF recommends that for patients with cardiac disease, severe hypothyroidism or patients over 50 years the initial starting dose should be 25mcg od with dose slowly titrated. Other patients should be started on a dose of 50-100mcg od - following a change in thyroxine dose thyroid function tests should be checked after 8-12 weeks - the therapeutic goal is 'normalisation' of the thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) level. title: Pharmacokinetic basics
content: # Pharmacokinetic basics ## Drug movement 1. Permeation 1. Aqueous diffusion - governed by Fick's law 2. Lipid diffusion - governed by Fick's law 3. Carrier transport - NOT governed by Fick's law 4. Endocytosis - allows for permeation of very large protein molecules into cells 2. Fick's Law of Diffusion 3. Water and lipid solubility 4. Henderson-Hasselbach equation $$ log \left( \frac{protonised}{unprotonised}\right) = pK_a - pH $$ ## Absorption ## Distribution - Distribution models - multicompartmental distribution - 2-compartment distribution 1. drug enters the first compartment (blood) 2. d

title: Adenosine
content: # Adenosine ## Mechanism 1. Suppresses AV nodal conduction 2. Suppresses automaticity 3. Dilates coronary arteries ## Indications 1. title: Ivabradine
content: # Ivabradine ## Mechanism - Blockade of the funny current - the channel for pacemaker automaticity in SA and AV nodes - Decreases NORMAL automaticity -> slows sinus rate - MOA: - selective inhibition of funny channels, prolonging slow depolarisation (phase 4 of pacemaker cells) - reduces SA nodal firing → negative chronotope, no inotropy effect → reduces O2 requirement ## Indications - HF in patients whos HR remains elevated despite beta blockers - Stable angina in patients intolerant or with contraindications to beta blockers - Inappropriate sinus tachycardia - chronic stable angina in pt who

title: Digoxin
content: # Digoxin ## Uses - Typical uses 1. AF and A Flutter with rapid ventricular response 2. HFrEF - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Cardiac glycoside - Inhibition of the NKA (cardio-specific) → raises intracellular Na+ → lowers gradient of Na-Ca exchanger in cardiac membrane → Increases intracellular (cytosolic) Ca2+ → Strengthens myocardial contractility - Elevated cytosolic Ca2+ leads to - delayed after-depolarisations (seen in digoxin toxicity) - Increases vagal tone in heart - Decreases sinus rate - Increases AV node refractory period - Decreases AV nodal conduction velocity - other effec

title: Contraction of Cardiac Muscle
content: # Contraction of Cardiac Muscle ## Autonomic innervation ### Parasympathetic innervation - Source: Vagal and spinal ganglionic nerves - Effect: Heart rate reduction (negative chronotrope) - Resting HR is governed by parasympathetic mechanism - PNS activity dominates over SNS activity at rest ### Sympathetic innervation - Source: Post-ganglionic noradrenergic - Effect: Positive chronotrope, Positive inotrope - Vascular redistribution to shunt blood to vital organs

title: Mean arterial pressure (MAP)
content: # Mean arterial pressure (MAP) ## Equation $$ MAP = (CO \times SVR) + CVP$$ Components - CO (Cardiac Output) determined by - Heart Rate - Stroke Volume - filling pressure (i.e. if empty, cannot have stroke volume) - cardiac contractility - SVP (Systemic Vascular Resistance) - Vascular tone - arterioles impart the most SVR (arteriolar SA significantly greater than other vessels) - metabolic state (local mediators) - autonomic nervous system - CVP (Central Venous Pressure) - marker of RA pressure (roughly associated with filling status)

title: Homeostasis
content: # Homeostasis ## Background Heomstoastasis is the *maintenance of nearly constant conditions in the internal environment* (Walter Cannon). ## Examples of homeostasis 1. Your body controls the Na+ ion concentration within your blood to a variation of a few *millimoles* per litre 2. Capillaries are located extremely close to cells (few are > 50micrometres away from a capillary) - this close apposition allows rapid transport of nutrients and removal of waste products from cells 3. Regulation of O2 and CO2 in ECF 4. Regulation of Arterial BP ## Examples of breakdown in homeostasis 1. Diseases whic

title: Vascular compliance
content: # Vascular compliance ## Background - Vascular compliance is the ability for a blood vessel wall to expand and contract passively with pressure changes - It is given by the following formula $$C = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$$ - Where: - $\Delta V$ is the change in volume - $\Delta P$ is the change in transmural pressure (pressure inside minus pressure outside) - The gradient of the volume-pressure curve is the compliance ## Factors increasing vascular compliance - Lower pressures and volumes - venous compliance is 10-20x greater than arterial compliance at lower pressures - vascular smooth musc

title: ALS2 notes
content: # ALS2 notes --- ## Chapter 1 --- ## Chapter 2 --- ## Chapter 3 --- ## Chapter 4: Cardiac causes of cardiac arrest - Acute coronary syndrome - STE on ECG: STEMI(STEACS) vs NSTEACS (NSTEMI + UA) - NSTEACS differentiate on Troponin: Raised (NSTEMI) or cisnstently negative (UA, other) - NSTEACS risk increased with ST depression, dynamic ECG changes, unstable rhythm/haemodynamics, DM, high GRACE score - Unstable angina: 1+ of 1. Angina on exertion, occuring with increasing frequency over a few days, provoed by progressively less exertion 'crescendo angina' 2. episodes of angina-like pain recurrent

title: BASIC - Acute Respiratory Failure
content: ## Definition - Acute Respiratory Failure - Pulmonary system not meeting metabolic demands of body 1. Hypoxaemic respiratory failure - PaO2 < 60mmHg when on RA 2. Hypercapnic respiratory failure - PaCO2 >= 50mmHg ## Basic respiratory physiology 1. Function of lung - O2 in and CO2 out 2. Oxygen in dependencies: - Alveolar O2 partial pressure (PAO2) - Alveolar pressure is the sum of all the pressures within the alveoli, comprising of O2, CO2, H2O and N2 - increasing alveolar pressure will increase all constituent partial pressures - increasing alveolar O2 will proportionally decrease PP of all o

title: Shock
content: # Shock Common causes and examples of shock include: 1. Hypovolemic: Absolute or relative lack of circulating blood volume. Causes include haemorrhage of any cause or source, loss of fluid (GI, GU, Skin) or “third space loss” (sepsis, burns, pancreatitis, intestinal obstruction) 2. Cardiogenic: Myocardial (ischemia, infarct or infective/inflammatory – myocarditis) or endocardial (Valvular lesion – MR/AR) pathology. Pericardial (constrictive pericarditis) (Often classified as obstructive) 3. Distributive: Pathological vasodilatation – Sepsis, Pancreatitis, burns, anaphylaxis, neurogenic shock.

title: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (APO)
content: # Acute Cardiogenic Pulmonary Oedema ## Background - Acute dyspnea associated with elevated left-sided filling pressures, with or without pulmonary edema. - Most commonly due to LV systolic or diastolic dysfunction. - Flash pulmonary edema refers to a severe form of heart failure with rapid accumulation of fluid in the lungs. - Originates from both (1) an increased alveolar fluid and (2) extreme SNS activation ## Diagnosis - Differential includes pulmonary embolism, asthma, and pneumonia, all of which can cause rapid respiratory distress. - Diagnostic tests include ECG, CXR, ABG, B-type natriu

title: Asystole
content: # Asystole ### Acute management - Epinephrine (Adrenaline) - Atropine - do NOT cardiovert **Ensure it is not a very slow bradycardi**

title: Atrial Fibrillation
content: # Atrial Fibrillation ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Atrioventricular Re-entrant Tachycardia (AVRT)
content: # Atrioventricular Re-entrant Tachycardia (AVRT) ### Signs/Symptoms - Palpitations, shortness of breath, angina, syncope, lightheadedness ### ECG Findings - A retrograde P wave is often seen after a normal QRS A reexcitation delta wave is characteristically seen in WPW ### Treatment - Except for WPW, same as that for AVNRT WPW listed separately below

title: Bifascicular block
content: # Bifascicular block - combination of Right bundle branch block with left anterior or posterior hemiblock

title: Bradyarrhythmias
content: # Bradyarrhythmias ## Background **The general principle of bradyarrythmia therapy is:** - if the cause is identifiable (e.g. drugs - (digoxin, beta blockers, verapamil, diltiazem, amiodarone)), stop the drugs! - if is doesn't threaten your life, don't do anything - it's fine (asymptomatic bradyarrhythmias do not need treatment) - if it causes an AF or AFib (e.g. in sick sinus syndrome) → anticoagulate to prevent Cardioembolic thrombi 1. If bradycardia originates ABOVE the AV node → give **atropine** 2. If atropine doesn't work → use pacemaker → Bridge with: - isoprenaline OR - Adrenaline (pre

title: Epidemiology of CAD
content: # Epidemiology of CAD ## CAD is the single leading cause of death in Australia - CAD accounts for: - 30% of deaths in Australia - single leading cause of Death in AUS - also the most costly disease group ($8 billion in 2011) - 10% of all Australian hospitalisations - 25% of all GP consultations - CVD burden - 201 DALY per 1000 people due to CVD - 31% of burden due to modifiable risk factors ## Absolute CVD risk assessment is needed to determine management strategy - Seven countries study - CHD death rates highest in: - East Finland - Northern Europe - US railroad - CHD death rates lowest in -

title: Familial Hypercholesterolaemia
content: # Familial Hypercholesterolaemia ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis --- ### Case: A 35-year-old man presents to your clinic for a general checkup and cholesterol screening. He denies having medical problems and takes no medications on a regular basis. He works as a computer programmer, exercises regularly at a gym, and does not smoke or use illicit drugs. He drinks two to three beers on the weekend. His father suffered his first heart attack at age 42 and eventually died of complications of heart disease at age 49

title: First degree AV block
content: # First-Degree AV Block - PR interval is prolonged (>0.20 second) **OR 200ms**, normally 120-200ms - A QRS complex follows each P wave. - Delay is usually in the AV node. - Benign condition that **does not require treatment.**

title: Heart failure
content: # Heart failure ## Background - CHF is a clinical syndrome resulting from the heart’s inability to meet the body’s circulatory demands under normal physiologic conditions. It is the final common pathway for a wide variety of cardiac diseases (see also Clinical Pearl 1-7). - Causes - Most commonly caused by ischemia (IHD) → typically causes systolic dysfunction (low EF) - myocardium CANNOT CONTRACT, but can relax → this is a systolic dysfunction (HFrEF picture) - Hypertension → typically causes diastolic CHF (high EF) - myocardium can contract heart, but CANNOT RELAX because the muscles are too

title: Marantic Endocarditis
content: # Marantic Endocarditis ## Background - Associated with debilitating illnesses such as metastatic cancer (found in up to 20% of cancer patients). - Sterile deposits of fibrin and platelets form along the closure line of cardiac valve leaflets. ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Although the use of heparin may be appropriate, no studies have confirmed its efficacy. ## Prognosis - Vegetations can embolize to the brain or periphery.

title: Non-ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes (NSTEACS)
href: IM/Cardiology/Non-ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndromes (NSTEACS).html
content: ## Background **TIMI (Thrombolysis in MI) score mnemonic: AMERICA - Used in UA or NSTEMI** - Age (>65) - Markers (elevated) - ECG changes (ST-depression in initial ECG) - Risk factors (3+ CHD) - Ischaemic chest pain (2+ Events in last 24h) - Coronary stenosis (>50%) - Aspirin (in last 7 days) TIMI determines the aggressiveness of therapy: - ≥3 - Early invasive strategy - coronary angiography + revascularisation w/ PCI or CABG - <3 - stress testing +/- invasive testing **Risk factors include:** Hypercholesterolemia, Hypertension, Diabetes Mellitus, Cigarette smoking, Positive family history, Ob

title: Orthostatic Hypotension
content: # Orthostatic Hypotension ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - Medications review - some medications may cause/exacerbate orthostatic hypotension - Diuretics (E.g. loop, thiazides) - MOA - ECF volume depletion - Adrenergic antagonists - alpha-1 adrenergic blockers (e.g. tamsulosin, terazosin) - vasidilation via direct effects on vascular smooth muscle - beta adrenergic blockers (E.g. propanolol) - reduces CO and renin release, can reduce vascular peripheral resistance - Alpha-2-adrenergic agonists (e.g. clonidine) - vasodilation via central

title: Primary Cardiac Tumours
content: # Primary Cardiac Tumours Primary tumors of the heart are rare (typically less than 0.1% of the general population). Metastases from other primary tumors are more common (75% of cardiac neoplasms). Sites of these primary tumors include the lung, breast, skin, kidney, lymphomas, and Kaposi sarcoma in patients with AIDS.

title: Prinzmetal Angina
content: # Prinzmetal Angina ## Background - Involves transient coronary vasospasm that usually is accompanied by a fixed atherosclerotic lesion but can also occur in normal coronary arteries. - Episodes of angina occur at rest and are associated with ventricular arrhythmias, some of which may be life threatening. The angina classically occurs at night. - Hallmark is transient ST-segment elevation (not depression) on ECG during chest pain, which represents transmural ischemia. - Taken from history, if the patient was **recently using cocaine**, they are at high risk for having Prinzmetal angina ## Path

title: Prosthetic valve thrombosis
content: # Prosthetic valve thrombosis ## Background - Etiology - Occurs if insufficient anticoagulatory therapy after valve replacement - Usually affects mechanic valves - Rare if anticoagulation is adequate - Presence of a cardiac defect raises the risk of recurrent endocarditis ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Signs of acute heart failure - Left heart failure - dyspnoea, cough - Right heart failure - oedema, jugular distension - Deterioration of general condition, cardiac arrhythmias, cerebral emboli (Stroke) ## Diagnostic criteria - TOE ## Investigations ## Management - Anticoagulation, fibrinol

title: Pulseless electrical activity
content: ## Background Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) occurs when organised or semi-organised electrical activity of the heart persists but the product of systemic vascular resistance and the increase in systemic arterial flow generated by the ejection of the left ventricular stroke volume is not sufficient to produce a clinically detectable pulse - In the ALS algorithm, PEA was formerly known as EMD (electromechanical dissociation) - In reality, pulseless electrical activity encompasses a very heterogeneous variety of severe circulatory shock states ranging in severity from pseudo-cardiac arrest

title: ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction
content: # ST Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction ## Background - MI is due to necrosis of myocardium as a result of an interruption of blood supply (after a thrombotic occlusion of a coronary artery previously narrowed by atherosclerosis). - Most cases are due to acute coronary thrombosis: Atheromatous plaque ruptures into the vessel lumen, and thrombus forms on top of this lesion, which causes occlusion of the vessel. - MI is associated with a 30% mortality rate; half of the deaths are prehospital. Most deaths are due to a new onset arrhythmia - Most patients with MI have history of angina, risk

title: Second degree AV block
content: # Second-Degree AV Block (Includes Mobitz Types I and II) ## Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) - Characterized by progressive prolongation of PR interval until a P wave fails to conduct - Site of block is usually within the AV node Cause: - Drugs (digoxin, beta blockers, CCB), increased vagal tone, infarct - Benign condition that **does not require treatment OR atropine if necessary** ## Mobitz type II - P wave fails to conduct suddenly, without a preceding PR interval prolongation; therefore, the QRS drops suddenly - Often progresses to third-degree AV block - Site of block is within the His–Purkinj

title: Sick sinus syndrome
content: # Sick sinus syndrome ## General characteristics - Sinus node dysfunction characterized by a persistent spontaneous sinus bradycardia. - Patients usually elderly. - heterogenous disorder, leads to SVT and bradyarrhythmias ## Clinical symptoms: - Symptoms include dizziness, confusion, syncope, fatigue, and CHF. - recurrent bradycardia/tachycardia can result in AF → Thromboembolism → TIA, Stroke, dyspnoea, etc. ## Diagnosis: - ECG ## Treatment: - Pacemaker implantation may be required. - Anticoagulate if AFib/AFlutter to prevent cardioembolic thromboembolism

title: Sinus bradycardia
content: # Sinus bradycardia ## General characteristics Sinus rate <60 bpm: Clinically significant when rate is persistently <45 bpm Causes include - ischemia - increased vagal tone - antiarrhythmic drugs; (beta blockers, CCBs) - may be a normal finding in trained athletes ## Clinical presentation Can be asymptomatic; patients may complain of fatigue, inability to exercise, angina, or syncope ## Diagnosis - ECG - sinus rhythm + HR < 60bpm ## Treatment - Atropine can elevate the sinus rate by blocking vagal stimulation to the sinoatrial node. - use if HR >40bpm - A cardiac pacemaker may be required if b

title: Sinus tachycardia
content: # Sinus tachycardia ### General characteristics - **Normal physiological response** - fear, pain, exercise, anxiety - secondary response - hyperthyroidism - volume contraction → poor perfusion → tachycardia to prevent hypoxia - PE - heart failure ### Clinical signs: - palpitations - shortness of breath ### Diagnosis: - ECG is sinus with HR > 100bpm ### Treatment: - treat underlying cause - Inappropriate sinus tachycardia: Sinus tachycardia with no known cause - typically no treatment unless symptomatic (as per eTG) - Beta blockers - Atenolol, Metoprolol - Non-DHP CCB - Verapamil - SEVERE Sx: I

title: Tachycardias
content: # Tachyarrhythmias Three basic mechanisms 1. Increased automaticity resulting from rapid phase 4 depolarisations 2. Delayed repolarisation causes spotaneous depolarisation in phase 3 / 4 of action potential, triggered by a preceding action potential 3. Re-entrant circuit depolarisation - parallel electrical circuits of different conduction velocities Source of depolarisation 1. Narrow-complex QRS - ventricular depolarisation is occuring over specialised ventricular tissue and the arrhythmia therefore arises from at or above the AV node (i.e. supraventricular) 2. Wide-complex QRS - ventricular

title: Torsades de Pointes (TdP)
content: # Torsades de Pointes (TdP) ### Background - Associated with long QT syndrome, proarrhythmic response to medications, hypokalemia, congenital deafness, and alcoholism - Polymorphic VT with a constantly shifting QRS axis - Self-limiting, but can turn into a VF - The cause of the prolonged QT interval may be congenital or acquired, the latter usually secondary to drugs or bradycardia. ### Signs/symptoms - Can present with sudden cardiac death; typically associated with palpitations, dizziness, and syncope ### ECG Findings - Polymorphous QRS; VT with rates between 150 and 250 bpm ### Treatment -

title: Ventricular Fibrillation
content: # Ventricular Fibrillation ### **General Characteristics** - Multiple foci in the ventricles fire rapidly, leading to a chaotic quivering of the ventricles and no cardiac output. - Most episodes of VFib begin with VT (except in the setting of acute ischemia/infarction). - Recurrence - If VFib is not associated with acute MI, recurrence rate is high (up to 30% within the first year). These patients require chronic therapy: - Either prophylactic antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone) or implantation of an ICD. - If VFib develops within 48 hours of an acute MI, long-term prognosis is favorable and t

title: Atrophie Blanche
content: # Atrophie Blanche ## Description - Angular scar arising on lower leg or foot - occurs after skin injury when blood supply poor or healing delayed - Commony seen in - middle aged women - associated with venous insufficiency - Hallmark of livedoid vasculopathy - may follow ulceration due to - cutaneous small vessel vasculitis - diabetic vascular disease - LL wound (E.g. cryotherapy, curettage, cautery) in treatment of skin cancer Atrophie blanche is due to occlusion of small blood vessels in the middle and deep dermis, which prevents normal healing. Blood vessel occlusion may be due to: - Micro

title: Acromegaly
content: # Acromegaly ## Background 1. Acromegaly is broadening of the skeleton, which results from excess secretion of pituitary GH after epiphyseal closure (if before epiphyseal closure, gigantism results). 2. It is almost always caused by a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma (represents 10% of pituitary adenomas). 3. Cardiovascular disease (cardiomyopathy) is the most common cause of death in acromegaly ## Clinical features 1. Growth promotion - Soft tissue and skeleton overgrowth - Coarsening of facial features - Abnormally large hand and foot size (ask about increasing glove/ring size) - Organomegaly

title: Adrenal crisis
content: # Adrenal crisis ## Background - Acute and severely symptomatic stage of adrenal insufficiency - Can be precipitated by stress (e.g. trauma, infection, surgery) ## Clinical features - Severe hypotension - Cardiovascular collapse - Abdominal pain (can mimic acute abdomen) - Acute renal failure - Death ## Management - Fix underlying cause - IV Hydrocortisone - IV Fluids (alternating 0.9% NaCl and 5% dextrose) ## Prognosis - Fatal if untreated

title: Androgen producing adrenal tumours
content: # Androgen producing adrenal tumours Women generate androstenedione (AS)/dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)/testosterone (T) in the adrenals and the ovaries. Dehydroepiandrosteone-sulfate (DHEA-S) is only produced in the adrenals, thus elevated DHEA-S is the most specific for an adrenal androgen producing tumor • Presentation: signs of virilization (pubertal hair, menstrual suppression, cliteromegaly), negative pregnancy test, and no abnormalities of the uterus/ovaries • Dx: adrenal tumor on CT scan with presentation • Tx: excision

title: Chronic Lymphocytic Thyroiditis
content: C. Chronic Lymphocytic Thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis, lymphocytic thyroiditis) 1. Most common cause of autoimmune thyroid disorder; more common in women. 2. Causes a. Genetic component—family history is common. b. Antithyroid antibodies are present in the majority of patients. 3. Clinical manifestations (see also Clinical Pearl 4-8) a. Goiter is the most common feature. b. Slow decline in thyroid function is common. Hypothyroidism is present in 20% of cases when first diagnosed but often occurs later in disease. 4. Diagnosis a. Thyroid function studies are normal (unless hypothyroidism is

title: Craniopharyngioma
content: # Craniopharyngioma ## Background - Tumors of the suprasellar region arising from embryologic remnants of Rathke pouch. - These tumors comprise 20% to 25% of all pituitary mass lesions (pituitary adenoma is most common). - They result in visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopsia) due to compression of the optic chiasm and may also cause headaches, papilledema, and changes in mentation. - They are diagnosed by brain MRI. - They may cause hyperprolactinemia, diabetes insipidus, or panhypopituitarism. - Treatment is surgical excision (total or partial resection) with or without radiation thera

title: Cushings Syndrome
content: ### Background: - A condition that refers to the manifestations of hypercortisolism - results in hyperplasia of the adrenal cortex - specifically the fasiculata - There are several types - iatrogenic Cushing's - patients taking steroids is the most common cause of Cushing's syndrome - pituitary adenoma (Cushing's disease) - most common pathogenic cause (70%) - majority of adenomas are benign - cause bilateral hyperplasia of the adrenal glands - associated increase in conversion of norepinephrine to epinephrine in medulla - enzyme involved is phenylethanolamine n-methyltransferase - adrenal Cus

title: Diabetes Insipidus (DI)
content: # Diabetes Insipidus ## Background 1. Two forms 1. Central DI is the most common form—due to low ADH secretion by posterior pituitary. 2. Nephrogenic DI—ADH secretion is normal but tubules cannot respond to ADH. 2. Causes 1. Central DI 1. Idiopathic—50% of all cases 2. Trauma—surgery, head trauma 3. Other destructive processes involving the hypothalamus, including tumors, sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, syphilis, Hand–Schüller–Christian disease, eosinophilic granuloma, and encephalitis 2. Nephrogenic DI—the most common cause in adults is chronic lithium use. Other causes include hypercalcemia, pyel

title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
content: # Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) ## Background - DKA is an acute, life-threatening medical emergency that can occur in both type I and type II diabetic patients (more common in type I). 1. Common emergency affecting primarily type 1 diabetics 2. Due to absolute insulin deficiency or insulin resistance with relative insulin deficiency 3. Presence of serum ketones, metabolic acidosis, with high anion gap and profound hyperglycaemia 4. Incidence higher in teenage years 5. Pathophysiology 1. Lack of insulin -> hyperglycaemia 2. hyperglycaemia -> increased osmolarity of blood 1. fluid returns to intra

title: Fibrous Thyroiditis (Riedel thyroiditis)
content: D. Fibrous Thyroiditis (Riedel thyroiditis) 1. Fibrous tissue replaces thyroid tissue, leading to a firm thyroid. 2. Surgery may be necessary if complications occur. 3. Patient may be hypothyroid as well, in which case thyroid hormone should be prescribed.

title: Fine needle aspiration (FNA)
content: Patient should first be evaluated with ultrasound and TSH level. A needle is inserted into the nodule, and cells are aspirated and then examined under a microscope. False-positive and false-negative rates approach 5%. This is the only test that can reliably differentiate between benign and malignant nodules. Ultrasound differentiates between solid and cystic nodules, but either may be malignant. On the thyroid scan, “cold” nodules are more likely to be malignant than “hot,” but this is not reliable. FNA findings: Probable cancer (15%): Most of these are really cancers. Surgery is indicated. In

title: Gestational Diabetes
content: # Gestational Diabetes ## Background - Glucose intolerance first recognised in pregnancy ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - requires tighter glycaemic targets than other diabetes populations ## Prognosis

title: Glucagonoma
content: # Glucagonoma A glucagon-producing tumor located in the pancreas. Clinical manifestations include necrotizing migratory erythema (usually below the waist), glossitis, stomatitis, DM (mild), and hyperglycemia (with low amino acid levels and high glucagon levels). Treatment is surgical resection.

title: Hyperglycaemic Hyperosmolar syndrome
content: # Hyperglycaemic Hyperosmolar (Nonketotic) Syndrome 1. General characteristics (see also Table 4-6) a. A state of severe hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, and dehydration is typically seen in elderly type II diabetic patients. b. Pathogenesis Low insulin levels lead to hyperglycemia. Severe hyperglycemia causes an osmotic diuresis, leading to dehydration. Ketogenesis is minimal because a small amount of insulin is released to blunt counterregulatory hormone release (glucagon), and thus ketones are not produced in excess as they are in DKA. Ketosis and acidosis are typically absent or minimal. Se

title: Hyperprolactinaemia
content: # Hyperprolactinaemia ## Background 1. Prolactinoma - Most common cause of hyperprolactinemia - Most common type of pituitary adenoma (up to 40%) 2. Medications (e.g., psychiatric medications, H2 blockers, metoclopramide, verapamil, estrogen) 3. Pregnancy 4. Renal failure 5. Suprasellar mass lesions (can compress hypothalamus or pituitary stalk) 6. Hypothyroidism 7. Idiopathic ## Clinical features 1. Men - Hypogonadism, decreased libido, infertility, impotence - High levels prolactin → GnRH inhibition → decreased LH/FSH secretion → decreased estrogen and testosterone production - Galactorrhea

title: Hypoglycaemia
content: # Hypoglycaemia ### A. General Characteristics 1. Most common endocrine emergency 2. Definition: Plasma glucose ≤ 3mM (approx) 3. The primary organ at risk in hypoglycemia is the brain—The brain uses glucose as its main energy source except when using ketone bodies during fasting 4. Unlike other tissues, the brain cannot use free fatty acids as an energy source. 5. Hypoglycemia is really due to an imbalance between glucagon and insulin. 6. If there is no correlation between the symptoms and low glucose levels (e.g., patient has symptoms when glucose levels are normal), an underlying disorder o

title: Hypoparathyroidism
content: # Hypoparathyroidism A. Causes 1. Removal of glands during head and neck surgery account for the majority of cases —thyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy, radical surgery for head and neck malignancies. 2. Nonsurgical hypoparathyroidism is rare. B. Clinical Features 1. Cardiac arrhythmias 2. Rickets and osteomalacia 3. Increased neuromuscular irritability due to hypocalcemia a. Numbness/tingling—circumoral, fingers, toes b. Tetany Hyperactive deep tendon reflexes Chvostek sign—Tapping the facial nerve elicits contraction of facial muscles. Trousseau sign—Inflating the BP cuff to a pressure higher t

title: Hypopituitarism
content: # Hypopituitarism ## Background 1. All or some of the hormones released from the anterior pituitary may be absent. 2. Loss of hormones is unpredictable, but LH, FSH, and GH are usually lost before TSH and ACTH. 3. Clinical manifestations depend on which hormones are lost. ## Pathophysiology and aetiology 1. Hypothalamic or pituitary tumor is the most common cause. 2. Other causes: radiation therapy, Sheehan syndrome, infiltrative processes (e.g., sarcoidosis, hemochromatosis), head trauma, cavernous sinus thrombosis, surgery. ## Clinical features 1. Reduced GH: growth failure (decreased muscle

title: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)
content: # Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN) ## Background - Inherited condition with propensity to develop multiple endocrine tumours - Autosomal dominant inheritance, incomplete penetrance ## MEN I (Wermer syndrome) - PPP - Primary Hyperparathyroidism (hypercalcemia, high urine calcium, high PTH) - Entero**pancreatic** tumors (most commonly gastrinoma causing Zollinger-Ellison syndrome; VIPoma, glucagonoma, and insulinoma all possible) - Pituitary adenomas (most commonly prolactin secreting) ## MEN IIa (Sipple syndrome) - MPH - MOA: RET-protooncogene mutation - Medullary Thyroid Cancer (parafollicul

title: Myxedema Coma
content: # Myxedema Coma ## Background - Rare condition due to severe hypothyroidism - May develop after years of severe untreated hypothyroidism - Precipitated by - Trauma, infection, cold exposure and narcotics - Medical emergency with high mortality rate (50-75%) with treatment ## Clinical features - Depressed state of consciousness, profound hypothermia and respiratory dspression ## Management 1. Supportive therapy - Maintain BP and respiration 2. Thyroid replacement - IV Thyroxine - Hydrocortisone

title: Pheochromocytoma
content: # Phaeochromacytoma ## General Characteristics - Pheochromocytomas are rare tumors that produce, store, and secrete catecholamines. - Ninety percent found in adrenal medulla (10% extra-adrenal). - Curable if diagnosed and treated, but may be fatal if undiagnosed. - Arise from the chromaffin cells of the adrenal medulla or from the sympathetic ganglia if extra-adrenal. ## Pathology - 10% familial, associated with [Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)](Multiple%20Endocrine%20Neoplasia%20(MEN).md)-II, [Neurofibromatosis](../Genetics/Neurofibromatosis.md) and von Hippel-Lindau syndrome ## Clinical F

title: Pituitary Adenoma
content: # Pituitary Adenomas ## Background 1. Pituitary adenomas account for about 10% of all intracranial neoplasms. 2. Almost all pituitary tumors are benign. They may grow in any direction causing “parasellar” signs and symptoms. 3. Size: microadenoma (diameter ≤10 mm); macroadenoma (diameter >10 mm). ## Clinical features 1. Hormonal effects occur due to hypersecretion of one or more of the following hormones: - Prolactin—see the following section on hyperprolactinemia - GH—results in acromegaly (or gigantism if epiphyseal closure has not occurred; seen in the pediatric population) - Adrenocorticot

title: Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn syndrome)
content: # Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome) A. General Characteristics 1. Excessive production of aldosterone by the adrenal glands independent of any regulation by the renin–angiotensin system (see also Clinical Pearl 4-12) 2. Excess mineralocorticoids increase the activity of the Na+/K+ pumps in the cortical collecting tubules. a. Sodium retention causes ECF volume expansion and HTN. b. Potassium loss results in hypokalemia. 3. Excess aldosterone also increases the secretion of hydrogen ions into the lumen of the medullary collecting tubules resulting in metabolic alkalosis. B. Causes 1.

title: Primary hyperparathyroidism
content: # Primary hyperparathyroidism A. General Characteristics 1. One or more glands produce inappropriately high amounts of PTH relative to the serum calcium level. 2. Most common cause of hypercalcemia in the outpatient setting. B. Causes 1. Adenoma (80% of cases)—majority involve only one gland 2. Hyperplasia (15% to 20% of cases)—all four glands usually affected 3. Carcinoma (<1% of cases) C. Clinical Features 1. “Stones” a. Nephrolithiasis b. Nephrocalcinosis 2. “Bones” a. Bone aches and pains b. Osteitis fibrosa cystica (“brown tumors”)—predisposes patient to pathologic fractures (Figure 4-6)

title: Pseudohypoparathyroidism
content: # Pseudohypoparathyroidism ## Background - End-organ resistance to the action of PTH ## Diagnosis - Laboratory findings - Hypocalcaemia - Hyperphosphatemia - High PTH - Low urinary cAMP

title: Secondary adrenal insufficiency
content: 2. Secondary adrenal insufficiency a. Patients on long-term steroid therapy—This is the most common cause of secondary adrenal insufficiency today. When these patients develop a serious illness or undergo trauma, they cannot release an appropriate amount of cortisol because of chronic suppression of CRH and ACTH by the exogenous steroids. Therefore, symptoms of adrenal insufficiency result. b. Hypopituitarism (rare)—due to a variety of insults. 3. Tertiary adrenal insufficiency—hypothalamic disease.

title: Somatostatinoma
content: # Somatostatinoma A rare, malignant pancreatic tumor; metastases usually are present by diagnosis. Poor prognosis Classic triad of gallstones, diabetes, and steatorrhea

title: Subacute (Viral) Thyroiditis (Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis) (De Quervain's thyroiditis)
href: IM/Endocrinology/Subacute (Viral) Thyroiditis (Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis) (De Quervain's thyroiditis).html
content: # Subacute (Viral) Thyroiditis (Subacute granulomatous thyroiditis) (De Quervain's thyroiditis) ## Background ## Pathology - Causes - usually follows a viral illness - associated with HLA-B35 - presents with hyperthyroidism ## Clinical features - Prodrome - lasts few weeks - fever, flu-like illness - phase 1 (lasts 3-6 weeks): hyperthyroidism, painful goitre, raised ESR - hyperthyroid from transient leakage of thyroid hormone - phase 2 (1-3 weeks): euthyroid - phase 3 (weeks - months): hypothyroidism - thyroid hormone depleted over time - phase 4: thyroid structure and function goes back to no

title: Subacute Lymphocytic Thyroiditis (painless thyroiditis)
content: B. Subacute Lymphocytic Thyroiditis (painless thyroiditis, silent thyroiditis) 1. A transient thyrotoxic phase of 2 to 5 months may be followed by a hypothyroid phase. The hypothyroid phase is usually self-limited and may be the only manifestation of this disease if the hyperthyroid phase is brief. 2. Low radioactive iodine uptake—differentiates it from Graves disease during thyrotoxic phase. 3. Similar to subacute (viral) thyroiditis, only without the pain or tenderness of the thyroid gland.

title: Syndrome of Inappropriate Secretion of Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH)
href: IM/Endocrinology/Syndrome of Inappropriate Secretion of Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH).html
content: # Syndrome of Inappropriate Secretion of Antidiuretic Hormone (SIADH) ## Background 1. Pathophysiology 1. Excess ADH is secreted from the posterior pituitary or an ectopic source. Elevated levels lead to water retention and excretion of concentrated urine. This has two major effects: hyponatremia and volume expansion. 2. Despite volume expansion, edema is not seen in syndrome of inappropriate secretion of antidiuretic hormone (SIADH). This is because natriuresis (excretion of excessive sodium in urine) occurs despite hyponatremia. 3. Reasons for natriuresis 1. Volume expansion causes an increa

title: Thyroid cancer
content: # Thyroid cancer ## Background - Risk factors - Head and neck radiation (during childhood) - Gardner syndrome and Cowden syndrome for papillary cancer - MEN type II for medullary cancer ## Pathology - In order of reducing likelihood - Please Fuck My Ass (PFMA) 1. Papillary carcinoma - 70-80% of thyroid cancer - least aggressive - slow growth and slow spreading with excellent prognosis. - RF - head/neck irradiation - Spread - lymphatics → cervical lymph node mets; rarely distant mets - Positive iodine uptake scan 2. Follicular carcinoma - 15% thyroid cancer - very positive iodine uptake scan -

title: Thyroid storm
content: # Thyroid storm ## Background - Rare, life-threatening complication of thyrotoxicosis - an acute exacerbation of hyperthyroidism - Precipitated by - infection, DKA, stress (e.g. trauma, surgery, illness, childbirth) - High mortality rate - 20% enter a coma or die ## Clinical features - Fever - Tachycardia - Agitation, psychosis, confusion - GIT symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea) ## Management - Supportive therapy - IVF, cooling blankets, glucose - Antithyroid agents - PTU q2hrly - Iodine - for inhibition of thyroid hormone release - Beta blockers (HR control) - Dexamethasone - impairs peri

title: Thyroiditis
content: ## Thyroiditis ## Thyroid-Associated Ophthalmopathy (TAO) - Autoimmune attack on the periorbital connective tissue and extraocular muscles - Clinical features - Lid retraction ('thyroid stare') - Proptosis - Eyelid oedema - lagopthalmos (inability to close eyelid completely) - Diplopia - Patients are - Hypothyroid - Hyperthyroid (e.g. Graves disease) - Euthyroid - Management - Treatment of thyroid dysfunction has minimal effect on course of TAO - Typically self-limited but surgery may be required if disease severe - Oral steroids may also be helpful

title: Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (Gastrinoma)
content: # Zollinger-Ellison Syndrome (ZES, Gastrinoma) A pancreatic islet cell tumor that secretes high gastrin, which leads to profound gastric acid hypersecretion, resulting in ulcers. Up to 60% are malignant; 20% associated with MEN I (80% are sporadic); 90% located in the “gastrinoma triangle” (formed by the following points: cystic duct superiorly, junction of second and third portions of the duodenum inferiorly, and neck of pancreas medially). Possible complications: GI hemorrhage, GI perforation, gastric outlet obstruction/stricture, and metastatic disease (liver is the most common site). Clini

title: Coeliac disease
content: # Coeliac disease ## Background - autoimmune condition caused by gluten sensitivity - epidemiology - ~ 1% affected ## Pathology - sensitivity to gluten - repeat exposure → villous atrophy → malabsorption - strongly associated with HLA-DQ2 (95% of patients) and HLA-DQ8 (80%) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240213004738.png) - Complete atrophy of the villi with flat mucosa and marked crypt hyperplasia. Intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Dense mixed inflammatory infiltrate in the lamina propria. ## Clinical features - Associations - dermatitis herpetiformis (a vesicular, pruritic skin eruption)

title: Crohn disease
content: # Crohn disease ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Induce remission - Glucocorticoids - oral, IV, topical - 5ASA (e.g. mesalazine) second line - Add on - AZA, 6MP - TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) needs to be done prior as it can contraindicate these two - MTX - if AZA and 6MP contraindicated - IFX - if failed conventional therapy, fistulating Crohn's - Maintaining remission - AZA/6MP to maintain remission - MTX second line - Surgery - around 80% of patients with Crohn's disease will eventually have surgery - stricturing

title: Liver cirrhosis

title: Dyspepsia
content: # Dyspepsia ## Background - Acute, chronic or recurrent **pain or discomfort** in the upper abdomen - Prevalence 10-20% ## Causes of dyspepsia 1. Food or drug intolerance 2. Functional dyspepsia 3. Luminal GI tract dysfunction 4. Heliobacter pylori infection 5. Pancreatic disease 6. Biliary tract disease 7. Other causes 1. Diabetes mellitus 2. Thyroid disease 3. CKD 4. Myocardial ischemia ## Investigations - Labs - H. pylori testing - urea breath test, fecal antigen test - Age > 60 - FBC, EUC, LFT, CMP, TFT - Gastroscopy - for gastric or oesophageal malignancy - Age > 60 with new onset dyspeps

title: Acute Cholecystitis
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Acute Cholecystitis.html
content: ### General Characteristics 1. Obstruction of the cystic duct (not infection) induces **acute inflammation of the gallbladder wall**. 2. Chronic cholecystitis may develop with recurrent bouts of acute cholecystitis. 3. Ten percent of patients with gallstones develop acute cholecystitis. ### Pathophysiology - Obstructuve cholelithiasis (~90%) → passage of gallstone into cystic duct → cystic duct obstruction - (STERILE) → inflammatory response triggered → gallbladder distention and inflammation - Secondary bacterial infection may be present (E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterobacter, Enterococcus most c

title: Biliary Dyskinesia
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Biliary Dyskinesia.html
content: ### Background - Motor dysfunction of the sphincter of Oddi which leads to recurrent episodes of biliary colic without any evidence of gallstones on diagnostic studies such as ultrasound, CT scan, and ERCP. ### Diagnosis - Diagnosis is made by CCK-HIDA scan. Once the gallbladder is filled with labeled radionuclide, give cholecystokinin (CCK) intravenously; then determine the ejection fraction of the gallbladder. If the ejection fraction is low, dyskinesia is likely. - Tc-99m labelled HIDA injected IV → excreted into biliary tract - After gallbladder filling, CCK is infused IV to stimulate gall

title: Choledochal Cyst
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Choledochal Cyst.html
content: ### Background - Cystic dilatations of biliary tree involving either the extra- or intrahepatic ducts, or both; more common in women (4:1). - Saccular or fusiform dilations of biliary tree, or as dilated masses which communicate with the biliary tree - Commonly seen in patients of asian descent ### Clinical features - Clinical features: epigastric pain, jaundice, fever, and RUQ mass. ### Complications - cholangiocarcinoma → most feared complication—risk is about 20% over 20 years - hepatic abscess - recurrent cholangitis/pancreatitis, rupture - biliary obstruction → cholestatic jaundice - cirr

title: Choledocholithiasis and Cholangitis
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Choledocholithiasis and Cholangitis.html
content: ## Background - 15% of patients with cholelithiasis also have choledocholithiasis - percentage increases with age, up to 50% - juxtapupillary duodenal diverticulum = increased risk of cholelithiasis - Bile duct stones form - in gallbladder (in most) - in bile duct (post-cholecystectomy) ## Clinical features - Biliary pain - obstructed bile duct and backwards pressure - Jaundice - Acute cholangitis - endoscopic emergency - Charcot triad: RUQ pain, fever/chills, jaundice - Reynolds pentad: AMS, hypotension, RUQ pain, fevers/chills/ jaundice - Hepatomegaly - in calculous biliary obstruction - ass

title: Gallbladder Empyema (Suppurative Cholecystitis)
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Gallbladder Empyema (Suppurative Cholecystitis).html
content: ### Background - distended pus-filled gallbladder ### Clinical features - similar to uncomplicated acute cholecystitis ### Diagnosis - Imaging - CT with IV contrast → gallbladder distention with hyperechoic or hyperintense material within lumen ### Treatment - Empiric antibiotic therapy for biliary infection - Emergency source control procedure - Low surgical risk → Laparoscopic cholecystectomy - High surgical risk → image-guided percutaneous drainage of empyema followed by interval laparascopic cholecystectomy

title: Gallbladder Perforation
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Gallbladder Perforation.html
content: ### Background - break in continuity of gallbladder wall, usually secondary to ischemic necrosis ### Clinical features - Variable, typically progress rapidly - may be indistinguishable from uncomplicated acute cholecystitis - Possible palpable RUQ mass +/- generalised peritonitis ### Diagnosis - Imaging - U/S or CT w/ contrast → focal defect in gallbladder wall, possible extraluminal stone ### Treatment - Emergent lap choley + empirical ABx for biliary infection

title: Gangrenous Cholecystitis
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Gangrenous Cholecystitis.html
content: ## Background - ischemic necrosis of the gallbladder - commonly a complication of acute cholecystitis ## Clinical features - Difficult to distinguish from uncomplicated acute cholecystitis ## Diagnosis - Imaging - Ultrasound: Acute cholecystitis + echogenic membranes floating within gallbladder lumen - CT w/ IV contrast: nonenhancement of the gallbladder wall ## Treatment - Emergency laparoscopic cholecystectomy with empiric antibiotic therapy for biliary infection

title: Hemobilia
content: ## Background - Blood draining into the duodenum via the common bile duct (CBD). - The source of bleeding can be anywhere along the biliary tract, the liver, or the ampullary region. - Causes - trauma (most common) - papillary thyroid carcinoma (intrabiliary metastasis) - surgery (e.g., cholecystectomy, CBD exploration) - tumors, and infection. - spontaneous bleeding during anticoagulation ## Clinical features - Triad: GI bleeding (melena, hematemesis), jaundice, and RUQ pain. - "pain, bleed, jaundice" - similar to Charcot's triad! ## Diagnosis - **Arteriogram is diagnostic.** - Upper GI endos

title: Primary Biliary Cholangitis
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Primary Biliary Cholangitis.html
content: ### General Characteristics 1. PBC is a chronic and progressive cholestatic liver disease characterized by destruction of **ONLY** **intrahepatic** bile ducts with portal inflammation and scarring - Most common cause of **vanishing bile duct syndrome (progressive ductopenia)** 2. It is a slowly progressive disease with a variable course. It may progress to cirrhosis and end-stage liver failure. 3. It is an autoimmune disease that is often associated with other autoimmune disorders. 4. It is most common in middle-aged women. (F>M 9:1, 30-65yo), may be predisposed by a positive family history ##

title: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC).html
content: ### General Characteristics 1. A chronic idiopathic progressive disease of intrahepatic and/or extrahepatic bile ducts characterized by thickening of bile duct walls and narrowing of their lumens; leads to cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and liver failure 2. There is a strong association with **Ulcerative colitis** (less so with Crohn disease). UC is present in 50% to 70% of patients with PSC; often the UC may dominate the clinical picture. (Note: The course of PSC is unaffected by a colectomy done for UC.) [Ulcerative Colitis](../Inflammatory%20Bowel%20Disease%20e1750a6964df41869d83308756d010

title: Hereditary Haemochromatosis

content: # Non-variceal acute upper GI bleed ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240219015337.png) ## Management - Transfusion target - Villanueva 2013 NEJM - transfuse if Hb < 70; if vascualr disease, aim Hb > 80-90 - no difference in survival with liberal target; increased risk of further bleed and pulmonary oedema - PPI - give for 72h (post-endoscopy) if received endoscopic therapy - switch to BD PO or IV - no difference for BD or infusion - Cosnider IV maxolon or erythromycin - always test for H. pylori if ulc

title: Intraductal Papillary Mucinous Neoplasm (IPMN)
content: # Intraductal Papillary Mucinous Neoplasm (IPMN) ## Background - Nil gender predisposition - 60-80 year old ## Classifications 1. Main duct Main duct IPMN: • >10 mm diameter at least 50% chance of cancer • Resect all involved pancreas when possible • May require a total pancreatectomy • 5-10mm (worrisome but not absolute criteria) • At ERCP: Patulous ampulla with mucous flow 1. Side branch (branch-type) Often multiple small cysts : Head of pancreas common – Frequency increases with age – Typically picked up incidentally – Often can see a communication with a side duct of the pancreatic duct –

title: Mucinous cystic neoplasm
content: - 99% women, 40-50F Usually Solitary cystic lesion in tail of the pancreas – Almost always women, occurs in middle age – Imaging features of single cyst or few septations with a solid component and sometimes calcifications – Pathologically has ovarian type stroma with columnar mucosal lining – Up to 1/3 harbour malignancy – Size, Mural Nodules, Solid Component, Increased Age – Resect all MCN when possible – Recurrence after complete resection is rare. Malignant potential

title: Pancreatic Neuroendocrine tumours (P-NET)
content: – 70-80% are non-functioning – Present as incidental mass lesions or occasionally with metastases – 20-30% functioning – Presentation usually due to overproduction of a hormone – Insulinoma (presenting with hypoglycaemia) is most common of these – Imaging: – CT scan. Strong arterial phase blush • Occur anywhere within the pancreas and can be multifocal • Look for metastases (liver and lymph nodes) – Octeotide imaging: • Octreotide scan mostly replaced by DOTATATE PET scanning • Resect non functioning >2cm • Resect functioning • Palliate with Long-acting Octreotide analogues. • Role for Lutetiu

title: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma
content: This is an aggressive tumour with poor outcomes – Risk factors: Smoking, alcohol, chronic pancreatitis, family history – Most are uncurable at diagnosis – Around 20% are or become operable – Of these… only around 25% will achieve long-term (5 year) survival DDx : – Pancreatic Neuroendocrine Tumour (P-NET) – Autoimmune pancreatitis – Rare solid lesions/metastases – Not usually resectable (80%) – Most have metastasised at time of diagnosis – Poor long-term survival even if resected – 20-30% long term survival – Surgery required for cure: Only for non- metastatic – Chemotherapy is mainstay of ad

title: Pancreatic pseudocyst
content: ## Background - Follows acute pancreatitis episode - unlike a cyst, the lining has scar/granulation tissue rather than epithelial lining ## Management - Large symptomatic pseudocysts → Endoscopic drainage - cyst-gastrostomy - pancreatic duct stenting - Large pseudocysts not amenable to endoscopic management - cyst gastrostomy - cyst-Roux en Y-Jejunostomy ## Prognosis and complications - 5cm and >6 weeks after pancreatitis episode → unlikely to resorb spontaneously

title: Serous Cystic Neoplasm
content: - 75% F, 60-70F Usually solitary in the body or tail of the pancreas – Usually in women of older age – Grow slowly – Can become Very large – Association with Von-Hippel-Lindau Syndrome – Morphologically: Numerous cysts surrounding a spongey central ‘scar’ – Rarely symptomatic, Rarely Malignant – Management: Resect the large or symptomatic ones, watch the rest

title: Solid Pseudopapillary Neoplasm (Franz Tumour)
content: Low grade malignancy – Epithelial cells forming solid and pseudopapillary structures with haemorrhagic or necrotic cystic areas – Occur anywhere in pancreas. Young women 20-30 years old most common – Management is Surgical resection – Long term survival is usua Locally aggressive

title: Acute Hepatitis D
content: # Acute Hepatitis D ## Background ## Pathology - ssRNA - incomplete RNA virus, requiring hepatitis B surface antigen to complete replication and transmission - can only occur as a co-infection with hepatitis B - parenteral transmission - requires bodily fluid exchange ## Clinical features - Superinfection: Classically presents with acutely decompensated hepatitis B - high risk of fulminant hepatitis, chronic hepatitis and cirrhosis ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - Hepatitis D RNA ## Management - Interferon ## Prognosis

title: Frailty
content: ## Background/definition - loss of physiological reserve and dysregulation across multiple systems - results in poor health outcomes - Prevalence - 5-17% ## Elements (3+ of below) 1. Weakness (grip strength) 2. slow gait speed 3. decreased physical activity 4. weight loss 5. exhaustion/low energy ## Interventions 1. Exercise - strength, resistance training - aim to improve walking speed and function 1. optimise nutrition - increase protein intake 1. Once frailty is established - supportive, multifactorial and targeted treatment

title: Urothelial cancer

title: Antifungal agents
content: # Antifungals - Fluconazole - candida albicans (vaginitis, thrush), cryptococcus - not for krusei, glabrata or auris - does not cover aspergillus - do not use in neutropenic fever - do not know what fungus is in neutropenic fever - almost no adverse effects - Icanocandins - caspofungin, mycofungin, anudulafungin - covers ALL candida Onychomycosis / Tenia fungin - eficonazole and tavabarole - only topical agents against onychomycosis - terbinafine is better for nail and hair involvement - better efficacy AND fever adverse effects Aspergillus - voriconazole - better efficacy and fewer adverse ef

title: Antiviral agents
content: # Antivirals - Herpesvirus - Most accurate test: HSV PCR - Best step - treatment - acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir - equal efficacy in HSV and VZV - use PO formulation (not topical) → antivirals do not work by going on top (they need systemic) - CMV - retinal involvement (CMV retinitis) - management - valganciclovir, ganciclovir, foscarnet - equal in efficacy - also covers HSV and VZV - valganciclovir - best long-term therapy for CMV retinitis - L-isomer of gangiclovir → can be given orally - adverse effects - valganciclovir and ganciclovir - neutropenia and BM suppression (especially WCC

content: # Septic bursitis ## Background ## Pathology - Caused by - direct inoculation - contiguous infection - less commonly - hematogenous source - Microbiology - >80% due to Staphylococcus aureus ## Clinical features - Predominantly involves prepatellar, olecranon and trochanteric bursa ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - Clinical presentation - Bursa aspirate for - fluid analysis - gram stain - culture ## Management - Antibiotics (2-3 weeks) with daily bursa aspirates until sterile fluid obtained ## Prognosis

title: HACEK organisms
content: # HACEK organisms A series of organisms which cause infective endocarditis, but tend hard to grow/culture - even on negative blood cultures, suspect that these organisms are present: - Haemophilus (most commonly haemophilis parainfluenzae) - Aggregatibacter (previously actinobacillus) - Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans is an etiologic agent of periodontal disease - Cardiobacterium (e.g. cardiobacterium hominis) - Eikenella (e.g. Eikenella corrodens) - Kingella (e.g. Kingella kingae) These organisms have certain properties: - all commensals of the human oropharynx, can travel due to tooth

content: # Animal Bites ## Background - Most wounds (~ 80%) do not become infected - Cat bites more likely infected due to deeper puncture wounds - Microbiology - Mixed aerobes and anaerobes including staphylococci, streptococci, bacteriodes, porphyromonas, fusibacterum and pasturella - canine - capnocytophaga canimorsus - can cause severe infections in asplenic patients ## Assessment - Wound extent and dimension - Signs of inflammation and infection - oedema, erythema, pain, necrosis, lymphangitis, pus - Local neurovascular involvement - Tetanus and rabies assessment ## Investigations - Culture of dee

href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Diabetic foot infection.html
content: # Diabetic foot infections ## Background - Occur post-traumatic in people with Diabetes and vasculopathy, neuropathy, suboptimal glucose contorl and immunologic deficit - Other risk factors include - Ulcers for > 1 month - Recurrent ulcers - Low extremity amputatoin - Kidney function impairment - Walking barefoot - Positive probe-to-bone test in wound ## Diagnosis - **Diagnostic definition: Pus or two+ inflammatory signs (calor, tumor, rubor, dolor)** - Foot assessment - arterial ischemia, venous insufficiency, neuropathy, biomechanical abnormalities (E.g. carcot arthropathy, hammer toe) - cri

content: # Human bites ## Background - Cause: Intentional biting of others, self-inflicted wounds (e.g. fingernail biting, clenched-fist injruies) - Microbiology - Aerobic (Staphylococci, streptococci, Eikenella corrodens) and anaerobic (peptostreptococcus, fusobacterium, prevotella) ## Management - Short course prophylactic antibiotics (3-5 days) - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate - recomended for perforating infections - empiric MRSA coverage for patients with increased risk - Surgical evaluation - Hand involvement - Tetanus prophylaxis - Evalulation for Hepatitis B and C, HIV and other body fluid-transmissib

title: Acute Flaccid Myelitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Neurological/Acute Flaccid Myelitis.html
content: - Clinical features - acute onset limb weakness - preceded by respiratory illness or fever within previous 4 weeks - Background - most commonly in pediatric population, occasionally in adults - Pathology - neuroinvasive enteroviruses - EV-A71 - EV-D68 - 2-yearly outbreaks (late summer, early fall) since 2014 - Pathology - rapid progression - may be complicated by respiratory failure - Management - hospitalisation for close monitoring - nil evidence for any therapies to improve outcomes

title: Arbovirus Encephalitis
content: - Background - West nile virus - eastern and western equine encephalitis - St louis encephalitis - Powassan virus - La Crosse virus - Clinical features - meningitis - acute flaccid paralysis (similar to poliomyelitis) - neuropathy - retinopathy - Risk factors for poor outcome - Older patients - transplantation or immunosuppression - Pathology - thalamus and basal ganglia affected - Clinical features - facial or arm tremors - parkinsonism - myoclonus - Imaging - MRI - hypodense lesions or enhavements in thalamus, basal ganglia and midbrain - CSF serology - WNV IgM positive - Management - suppor

title: Autoimmune encephalitis
content: - Autoimmune neurological diseases include - encephalitis - cerebellitis - dystonia - status epilepticus - cranial neuropathies - myoclonus - Pathology - Anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate (anti-NMDA) receptor encephalitis most common - subacute onset - Associated with - paraneoplastic syndromes (young women with ovarian teratomas) - sex cord stromal tumours - small cell lung cancer - can occur without tumours - Triggered by - HSV and VZV encephalitis - Clinical features - Young women (<356 years) - post- viral like illness - behavioural changes - headaches - fevers - altered mental status - seizures -

title: Brain Abscess
content: - M > F - Causative agents - anaerobes - arobic - microaerophilic streptococci - S. aureus - Enterobacteriaceae - Immunosuppressed patients (e.g. HIV/AIDS) higher risk - Management - Empiric therapy - depends on predisposing condition - ABx therapy for 4-8 weeks with followup cranial imaging to ensure resolution of infection - Investigations - Aspiration preferred for definitive diagnosis - surgical/sterotactic drainage performed in abscess > 2.5cm

title: Cranial Abscess
content: - Aetiology - underlying osteomyelitis complicating paranasal sinusitis (Pott puffy tumour) - otitis media - post-neurosurgical intervention - head trauma - bacterial meningitis (rare) - Pathology - - slow growing - subacute/chronic symptoms - headache - localised bone pain - focal neurological signs - Pathology of subdural empyema - rapidly progressive infection - high mortality - neurosurgical emergency - Investigations - CSF - neurophilic pleocytosis - high protein levels - negative gram stain and cultures - Culture CSF during surgical drainage of abscess

title: Healthcare associated Meningitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Neurological/Healthcare associated Meningitis.html
content: # Healthcare associated Meningitis ## Background ## Pathology - Occur after - head trauma - neurosurgical procedures (e.g. craniotomy, LP) - Secondary to - DEvice infection (s.g. CSF shunt, drain, intrathecal pump, deep brain stimulator) - Common agents - Staphylococcus - Gram negative (enteric) bacteria - 50% have negative cultures ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria - CSF - normal or abnormal cell count, glucose, protein levels do NOT confirm/rule out infection - Assays - b-D-glucan - Galactomannan ## Investigations ## Management - Empirica ABx - device removal (if present) ## Progno

title: Herpes Simplex Encephalitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Neurological/Herpes Simplex Encephalitis.html
content: - Pathology - HSV1 most common cause of sporadic encephalitis - Risk factors - older age - abnormal GCS - delay in starting antiviral therapy - Clinical features - Altered mental status - fever - seizures - focal neurology - unilateral temporal lobe oedema - hemorrhage - enhancement on imaging - Investigations - CSF - lymphocytic pleocytosis - elevated protein - normal glucose - HSV PCR of CSF - Sn 98, Sp 94 - repeat PCR within 1 week if highly suspect - EEG - degree of cerebral dysfunction - Management - HSV1 - IV acyclovir 14-21 days HHV6 - Clinical features - severe limbic encephalitis - al

title: Noninfectious meningitis
content: # Noninfectious meningitis ## Background ## Pathology - MEdications - NSAIDs - antibiotics - IV Ig - Other diseases - Leukemia - Lymphoma - Metastatic carcinoma - SLE - Behcet disease - Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome (Uveomeningoencephalitis) - Neurosarcoidosis - Chemical meningitis secondary to - Intrathecal injections - Intracranial haemorrhage - Neurosurgical procedures - Spinal anaesthesia ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - CSF cytology - atypical/immature cells - severe hypoglycorrhachia (<10mg/dL) ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Tuberculous meningitis
content: # Tuberculous meningitis ## Background ## Clinical features - basilar meningitis with cranial neuropathies (esp of CN VI - abducens nerve) - mental status changes - SIADH ## Diagnosis 1. History (esp of TB exposure) 2. CXR 3. Tuberculin skin test positive 4. Inteferon-gamma release assay 5. CSF examination 1. lymphocytic pleocytosis (leukocyte 100-500/uL) 2. elevated protein levels 3. hypoglycorrhachia 4. CSF acid-fast bacilli smear - insensitive 5. CSF culture - positive in only 38-88% 1. Sensitivity increases with serially performed for >=3 days 6. NAAT ## Management - Anti tuberculosis ther

title: Varicella Zoster Virus Encephalitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Neurological/Varicella Zoster Virus Encephalitis.html
content: - Background - underidagnosed - treatable cause of encephalitis in adults - Clinical features - vasculopathy - stroke - encephalitis - meningitis - radiculopathy - myelitis - +/- vesicular rash - Investigations - PCR CSF for VZV - Management - IV acyclovir 10-14 days

title: Viral Meningitis
content: # Viral meningitis ## Background - Commonly due to enteroviruses - Herpesvirus present year-round including HSV type 1 and HSV type 2, VZV, CMV, EBV, HHV6 - HSV2 is most common with recurrence possible (called Mollaret meningitis) - VZV higher risk in older people or immunocompromise - West Nile Virus, St Louis Encephalitis, California encephalitis - seasonal mosquito-borne - HIV meningitis can occur with aseptic meningitis and mononucleosis-like syndrome - Less common causes - mumps, lymphocytic choriomeningitis, parainfluenza, adenovirus, influenza A and B, measles, rubella, poliovirus, rota

content: # Otitis Externa ## Background - note this is different from malignant otitis externa (a osteomyelitis with pseudomonas eating through skull) - swimming washes out the cerumen (keeps the external auditory canal acidic) - similar to washing out the lactobacillus in the vagina → OE is the 'vaginismus of the head' - Foreign bodies, cotton swabs → macerate EAC → makes bacteria easier to stick ## Clinical features - itchy - drainage from auditory canals - painful when tragus is tugged ## Investigations - no specific investigations - otoscopy - difficult to visualise tympanic membrane - tends to dri

content: ## Background - in diabetics out of control - different from mucormycosis → can be seen in top of palate, whereas MOE is a CT-scan based diagnosis - caused by Pseudomonas organism ## Investigation - Radiological (CT) is NOT the most accurate test - Biopsy is the MOST ACCURATE test ## Management - Surgical debridement - Best option is to use quinolone (best for gram negative bone infection due to accumulation) - Other options - cefepime/ceftazidime - piptaz - aztreonam - carbapenems

title: Kidney function Evalulation
content: # Kidney Function Evalulation ## Roles of the Kidney - Waste removal → Dialysis - Fluid balance → Dialysis - Acid/base balance → Dialysis - RBC and EPO - Electrolytes → Dialysis - Blood pressure → can be affected by dialysis - Bone mineralisation, calcium, phosphate, vitamin D → can be affected by dialysis ## Biochemical markers ### Serum Creatinine - Creatinine is freely filtered by glomerulus and secreted into urine by the proximal tubule - tubular secretion contribution as proportion of total creatinine increases as GFR declines - when urinary creatinine clearance used to estimate GFR, secr

title: Serum osmolality
content: # Serum Osmolality ## Formula $$2 \times [Na^+] + \frac{Glucose(mg/dL)}{18} + \frac{BUN (mg/dL)}{2.8} + \frac{Ethanol (mg/dL)}{3.7}$$ ## Physiology - Range 275 - 295 mOsm/kgH2O - Osmolar gap (between measured and calculated) should be < 10mOsm/kgH2O - greate value suggests LMW alcohol - The body defends volume status over tonicity - Volume depletion → ADH release → Hyponatremia - ADH release occurs regardless of osmolality

href: IM/Neurology/Epilepsy and seizures/Focal seizures with non-motor onset.html
content: # Focal seizures with non-motor onset ## Background These seizures may present as: - Changes on somatic sensation (e.g. paresthesias) - Visual changes (Flashing lights, formed hallucinations) - Disequilibrium (falling or vertigo) - Autonomic symtpoms (flushing, sweating, piloerection) - Emotional symptoms (tearing) These internal events not observable by others are also called *auras* ## Aetiology 1. Temporal or frontal cortex - hearing (hearing loud or complex sounds), olfaction (e.g. smelling odors), emotional symptoms (fear, deja vu), epigastric sensations

title: Arterial dissection
content: # Arterial dissection ## Background - Risk factors - Hypertension - Migraine - Polycystic Kidney Disease - Connective tissue disorders ## Clinical features - Acute pain in head and heck - Occur spontaneously or following trauma - More common in younger people - Carotid artery dissection - Orbital pain - Partial Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis only) - Ipsilateral cerebral or retinal ischemia - Vertebral artery dissection - Occipital or neck pain - Posterior fossa symptoms - dysarthria, dysphagia, ataxia, hemifield visual loss ## Investigations - Non-invasive vessel imaging - MR angiography -
