title: Brown-Sequard syndrome
href: IM/Neurology/Other neurological issues/Cord lesion syndromes/Brown-Sequard syndrome.html
content: # Brown-Sequard syndrome ## Clinical features - ipsilateral UMN weakness below level of lesion - ipsilateral LMN weakness and complete sensory loss at level of lesion - ipsilateral impairment of vibration and proprioception below level of lesion - contralateral pain and temperature sensation from 1-2 segments below lesion - bladder control NOT affected - due to bilateral sensory control ## Pathophysiology - trans-sectional lesion to a level of the spinal cord

title: Central cord syndrome
href: IM/Neurology/Other neurological issues/Cord lesion syndromes/Central cord syndrome.html
content: ## Background - Can be caused by - syringomyelia ## Clinical features ## Pathophysiology - Central canal-located injury - anterior commissure affected - spinothalamic tract decussates at level (and 2-3 above) at anterior commisure - therefore loss of pain and temperature at level of central cord injury bilaterally (but not below) - if expands, can place pressure on anterior horn at the level - anterior horn contains motor output AT LEVEL of injury - can cause segmental LMN weakness at level of lesion - further expansion can put pressure on lateral tracts - corticospinal tracts - this will resu

title: Posterior cord syndrome
href: IM/Neurology/Other neurological issues/Cord lesion syndromes/Posterior cord syndrome.html
content: # Posterior cord syndrome ## Background - Rarely ischemic (due to bilateral blood supply) - mainly caused by demyelination, nutritional deficiency and genetic issues ## Clinical features - loss of dorsal column function

title: Foster Kennedy Syndrome
content: # Foster Kennedy Syndrome ## Clinical features 1. Papilloedema 2. Visual loss and optic atrophy 3. Intracranial oedema 4. Personality changes ## Pathophysiology - Space occupying lesion (typically meningioma) results in pressure against the frontal lobe

title: Gerstmann Syndrome
content: # Gerstmann Syndrome ## Clinical features 1. Acalculia 2. Agraphia 3. L/R disorientation 4. Finger agnosia ## Pathophysiology - Lesion to the dominant Angular Gyrus (Parietal lobe)

title: Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome
content: # Osmotic Demyelination Syndrome (Central Pontine Myelinolysis) ## Background Central Pontine Myelinolysis is a demyelinating disorder of the pontine neurons - this occurs following rapid correction of hyponatremia resulting in an osmotic demyelination. This tends to occur 2-6 days following rapid Na+ elevation. ## Pathophysiology Chronic hyponatremia results in osmotic gradient pushing fluid into the cerebral neurons - over time: - intracellular osmolytes will be pushed extracellularly - osmolar gradient is neutralised - fluid will shift back extracellularly resulting in nil osmolar gradient

title: Posttraumatic Headache
content: # Posttraumatic Headache ## Background - Develops post traumatic event - e.g. injury to head - e.g. infection (viral menigitis, flu, parasite) - e.g. carotid dissection, SAH, intracranial surgery ## Clinical features - dizziness, vertigo - memory impairment - lasts for months or years after event - normal neurological exam ## Diagnosis - CT/MRI findings normal

title: Visual fields
content: # Visual fields ## Nerve pathways 1. Light is received at the retina - Temporal retina receives the contralateral visual field and the nasal retina receives the ipsilateral visual field 2. Retina projects through optic nerve 3. L/R visual field decussation at the optic chiasma 1. R optic chiasma contains all L) visual field information and vice versa 4. Optic chiasma will synapse at the Lateral Geniculate Nucleus in thalamus 5. LGN will sent projections through the parietal and temporal lobes to the primary visual cortex 1. parietal projections carry lower visual field information 2. temporal

title: Acute Stroke Care
content: # Acute stroke care ## Background - Neuroprotection - evidence based - Every minutes an LVO is untreated - 1.9 million neurons lost - Every 15 minutes to reduce thrombolysis time - increased odds of leaving hospital (4%), going home (3%), reduced risk of inpatient death (4%), brain haemorrhage (4%) Thrombolysis - rTPA (alteplase) given IV up to 4.5 post stroke LSW time - limited evidence for 6h window - ECR effective for strokes up to 24h - ICA, M1, M2, A1, A2, P1 and basilar arteries Stroke code - 20g IVF in cubi foss - 'Acute stroke' order on EMR - Bloods incl Coags, NOAC levels - CT ASAP -

title: Alteplase
content: # Alteplase ## Pharmacodynamics - Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) ## Indication - Patients presenting with **no evidence of haemorrhagic or large infarct** on noncon CT brain - Must be applied within 4.5h from onset of stroke - Recovery of stroke OR 1.15-1.75 - within 3 hours - 23.1 vs 32.9% improvement for placebo vs tPA respectively ## Dosage - 0.9mg/kg alteplase - 10% as bolus, 90% over 1 hour - ENCHANTED study (NEJM 2016) - Q: use of 0.6mg/kg vs 0.9mg/kg alteplase - shown to be NOT non-inferior at lower dose, howwever less tPA associated ICH ## Risks - Intracranial haemorrhage risk incr

title: Ischemic stroke recanalisation
content: # Ischemic stroke recanalisation ## Considerations - Should only recanalise if there is a large penumbra and a relatively small section of ischemic tissue - Identification ona stroke series imaging (CT Perfusion) - Cerebral blood flow (CBF) < 30% volume is considered **dead tissue** - Tmax > 6.0s (i.e. time to peak dye concentration >6s) is penumbra region - TMax increases as tissue becomes more ischemic - indicates slowed perfusion | Site of occlusion | 2h success | |---|---| | Carotid terminus | 6-12%| |MCA M1 | 21-30% | |MCA M2 | 37-44%| |Basilar | 30%| ## Time windows summary - tPA time wi

title: Lacunar infarct
content: # Lacunar infarct ## Background - Small lesions (typically < 1.5cm diameter) - Occur in distrubtion of short penetrating arterioles in basal ganglia, pons, cerebellum, internal capsule, thalamus and sometimes deep cerebral white matter - Associations - Poorly controlled hypertension - Diabetes ## Clinical features - Progression may occur over 24-36h prior to stabilising 1. Contralateral Pure motor hemiparesis 1. 33-50% of lacunar strokes 2. affects the Basis Pontis (posterior limb of internal capsule, or anterior pons) 2. Ipsilateral Ataxic hemiparesis 1. Affected posterior limb of internal ca

title: Mechanical thrombectomy (Endovascular clot retrieval)
href: IM/Neurology/Vascular Neurological Disorders/Mechanical thrombectomy (Endovascular clot retrieval).html
content: # ECR ## Indications - CTB - nil excessive infarct - CTA - major vessel occlusion (ICA, M1, M2 (if appropriate), Basilar) - Evidence for basilar thrombosis minimal (BASICS study) but practically still done - CTP - large penumbra, small core - tPA eligible (AND ECG indicated) → tPA should be given - DIRECT-MT, SKIP, DEVT - Good premorbid function (age is not an indication ## Markers for good outcome in ECR - Age - NIHHS score - SBP - Hx of ischemic stroke - Pre-morbid function - location of thrombosis - collaterals - size of infarct (ASPECT score) - time to treatment (JAMA 2019)

title: Stroke secondary prevention
content: # Stroke secondary prevention ## Vascular protection 1. Hypertension 1. single most modifiable risk factor for ischemic AND haemorrhagic stroke 2. Aim 120-130/80-90mmHg 1. Consider adding small dose ACEi if BP already low 2. avoid using betta blockers as first line unless IHD present 3. Initiate antihypertensive therapy 24-48h after stroke 2. Dyslipidaemia 1. for each 1mmol/L reduction in LDL, 21% relative risk reduction in stroke 2. SPARCL study - atorvastatin 80mg > placebo (but increased risk of ICH) 3. Aim for targets 1. Aim LDL target < 1.8mmol/L 3. Insulin resistance 4. Antiplatelet ther

title: Otitis Media
content: ## Background - possible causes: - caused by swelling of Eustachian tubes → blocks egress of secretions - caused by - pneumococcus, nontypeable haemophilus (NOT HiB - i.e. not vaccine preventable), Moraxella ## Clinical features - redness - bulging - immobile tympanic membrane - decreased hearing - fever ## Diagnosis - no radiological test available - most accurate physical finding - loss of mobility of tympanic membrane - most accurate test - tympanocentesis - however not necessary as you can clinically diagnose Otitis Media - aspirate tympanic membrane for culture ## Treatment - Amoxicillin

Safety ## Introduction to patient and family - Tell patient and family the structure of how the consultation will work - Speak together to understand the presentation - Speak to child 1 on 1 to understand social issues, etc. - Discuss confidentiality ## PRO-FORMA FOR HEADSSSS SCREEN ### Confidentiality - Hurting yourself, hurting others, someone else hurting you, committing a crime ### Home 1. who do you life with (anyone else in household?) 2. How do you get along with

title: Inhalation burns
content: # Inhalation burns ### General features - 20% in burn patients - 60% in burns patients with central facial burns - massive ↑ mortality ### Clinical features - events: enclosed space, explosion, LOC, toxic gases, did you breathe fire, voice changes - burns in face, especially around mouth/nose - singed nasal hair - **carbonaceous sputum** - nasal deposits - soot, charring, mucosal erythema/oedema/blistering/necrosis - voice changes/hoarseness - respiratory distress: hypoxaemia, raised A-a gradient, stridor, wheeze, tachypnoea, dyspnoea - **high CO levels – might look ‘cherry red’** - **CN: bitt

title: Adrenal Insufficiency (Paediatrics)
content: # Adrenal Insufficiency Congenital adrenal hyperplasia, in which there is deficiency of the enzyme 21-hydroxylase, presents with ambiguous genitalia in the female. If this is not recognized (as in the male), it may lead to unexplained vomiting, dehydration and collapse early in the second week of life. If the adrenal insufficiency is of the salt-losing type, the diagnosis is further suspected by finding low levels of sodium and increased levels of potassium in the serum, and is confirmed by appropriate hormonal studies.

title: Appendicitis (Paediatrics)
content: ### General features - Most common cause of abdominal pain in children - Age – **median 8-9yrs**: pain before vomiting and fever is uncommon – beware preschool child and fit teenager - 50% <5 will present with advanced – perforated/mass - Acute onset abdo pain - Infection/faecolith → Transmural inflammation →perforation ### Clinical features - Intermittent periumbilical pain (appendicular midgut colic) - 24h → migrates to RIF - 36-48h (peritonieal inflammation) - + Fevers (not high grade) - + anorexia - Pain changing over 12 hours of onset - initially no-few vomiting - >48h - risk of perforati

title: Duodenal Atresia
content: # Duodenal atresia - Abnormalities include: stenosis, web, atresia, annular pancreas (grows around the duodenum  extrinsic compression) - Obliteration of lumen – failure to recanalize  neonatal bilious vomiting - Double bubble – prematurity, CHD, trisomy 21 – insult occurs **earlier in gestation** Intestinal Atresia - Vascular insult late in gestation – **no associated congenital conditions** - Types: narrow lumen, Fibrous band , mesenteric defect (no join) + bowel spiralling round an artery (apple peel deformity – associated with larger defect – short bowel syndrome), multiple atresias Meco

title: Hernias (Paediatrics)
content: # Hernias ### Types of hernias in children - **Umbilical hernia** – don’t **fix until 4 or 5** – **heal on their own** - **Neck is important – >2cm fix (push back in) – esp. if black** - Contents: omentum – possibly bowel, rarely non-reducible - **Paraumbilical: swelling arises from above → smile sign – do not resolve spontaneously** - Contents: epigastric fat, rarely non-reducible – but **more likely to cause pain and discomfort** - Inguinal hernia – direct = lifestyle, indirect – through the canal - **Erythema over the labia – ovarian torsion** - Contents: **bowel, ovary, fallopian tube, bla

title: Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia
content: # Incarcerated inguinal hernia ### General characteristics - 9x M:F - Any age; incidence close to 5%, reported in 30% of male infants weighing ≤1000g ### Clinical features - Acutely swollen, tender erythematous testes (incarcaterated) - Painless inguinal swelling in most cases - May retract with active, cold, frightened or agitated infant - Coughing, long periods of standing - Firm, globular and tender swelling, sometimes associated with vomiting and abdominal distension as clinical clues - Herniated loop of intestine may become partiall obstructed leading to severe pain - Bowel can get trappe

title: Intussusception
content: # Intussusception In intussusception, the distal ileum (the intussusceptum) telescopes into adjoining distal bowel (the intussuscipiens), resulting in intestinal obstruction. It can occur at any age but is most likely in the infant between 3 and 18 months who suddenly develops screaming attacks of pain with vomiting. During each episode of pain the infant becomes pale and may draw up the legs. The spasms of pain tend to last for 2–3 minutes and occur at intervals of about 10–20 minutes, although after a while the pain becomes more persistent. Vomiting is an early symptom. The passage of a few

title: Malrotation and Volvulus (Paediatrics)
content: # Malrotation/Volvulus (Paediatrics) ### General features - 1% of population may have intestinal malrotation, diagnosed in 1st year of life (7-90%) ### Pathogenesis - Midgut: DJ junction to mid-TV colon - supplied by SMA (running in root of mesentery) - In gestation - Midgut elongates into umbilical sac, returns to intra-abdomina posiiton (10wk GA) → Mesenteric root rotates CCW during retraction (→ colon crosses abdominal cavity ventrally, Cecum moves from left to right lower quadrant, duodenum crosses dorsally, and becomes partly retroperitoneal) - Incomplete rotation → rdsal fixation of mese

title: Meckel's diverticulum (Paediatrics)
content: # Meckel’s diverticulum (Paediatrics) ### General features - Remnant of **omphalomesenteric duct** – **sits off antimesenteric border of mid-distal ileum** - **Rule of 2’s:** - 2% popn, - 2 inches long, - 2 feet from ileocaecal valve, - PC: 2y, - 2x mucosa (intestinal and ectopic – secretes acid 75%), - 2x M: F ### Clinical features - Painless rectal bleeding (40-60%) – less commonly intussusception, volvulus, perforation - due to deep ileal ulcers adjacent to diverticulum from acid secretion by heterotopic gastric tissue - can cause shock and anaemia - unlikely to be occult bleeding - Obstruc

title: Meconium ileus equivalent (Fecal Impaction)
content: # Meconium ileus equivalent (Fecal impaction) Children with cystic fibrosis frequently experience episodes of abdominal pain from faecal impaction (called ‘meconium ileus equivalent’), a well-known manifestation of this disease. The symptoms resolve following a bowel washout. ![Plain X-ray of the abdomen demonstrating gross faecal overload in a child with severe constipation causing abdominal pain. The child also had regular soiling](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402144242.png)

title: Mesenteric Adenitis
content: # Mesenteric Adenitis Mesenteric adenitis is the most difficult disorder to distinguish from acute appendicitis. In general, localization of pain and tenderness is variable and less specific, and the temperature may be higher. Guarding is rarely present in mesenteric lymphadenitis.

title: Necrotising Enterocolitis (NEC)
content: # Necrotising enterocolitis (NEC): - neonatal bowel: **multifactorial exaggerated inflammatory response** due to an initiating event (e.g. feed) in the context of an immature intestine and immune system - 1:1000 live births, **90% preterm – gestational age main prognostic predictor** - Pathophysiology – **epithelial barrier disturbed, bacterial translocation** - Severity  **modified Bell’s Classification system** - Stage 1: apnoea, bradycardia, temp instability, occult blood, mild abdo distension - distended, shiny abdomen - Progresses to shock, metabolic acidosis – **perforation especially i

title: Pancreatitis (Paediatric)
content: # Pancreatitis In pancreatitis, vomiting is prominent and epigastric tenderness with guarding may be marked. These children often look ill and obtunded. Pancreatitis is most commonly due to viral illnesses or may follow a blunt injury to the abdomen, (e.g. a handlebar injury), and several weeks later may produce a pancreatic pseudocyst. The diagnosis is suggested by increased levels of plasma or urinary amylase or plasma lipase, and is confirmed by ultrasonography initially, followed by magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) if indicated clinically. The management of acute pancreatitis involves corr

title: Pyloric stenosis
content: # Pyloric Stenosis This is one of the most dramatic causes of vomiting in infancy. Typically, the onset is sudden, commencing between the second and sixth week of life. Males are affected five times more often than females and there is a definite familial incidence. Before the onset of vomiting, these infants feed well and are thriving. The vomiting is forceful and rapidly becomes projectile. The infant loses weight and becomes dehydrated. Despite vomiting, these infants remain hungry and are keen to feed even immediately after vomiting. The vomitus is not bile-stained but may contain altered

title: Short bowel syndrome
content: # Short bowel syndrome SHORT BOWEL SYNDROME » General Considerations Short bowel syndrome (SBS) is defined as a condition resulting from reduced intestinal absorptive surface that leads to alteration in intestinal function that compromises normal growth, fluid/electrolyte balance, or hydration status. The vast majority of pediatric patients with SBS have undergone neonatal surgical resection of intestine. title: Down Syndrome
content: # Down Syndrome **Life expectancy: Women - 57.8 years, Men - 61.1 years** - Most **children die of CHD** – but towards end of life **Alzheimer’s disease.** - Beware **diagnostic overshadowing:** attributing **symptoms to mental impairment** **Congenital malformations** - CHD in 50-60% - **AVSD, VSD, PDA, TOF** - Echocardiogram in neonatal period vital - GIT Malformations in 10% - **duodenal atresia, Hirschsprung’s Disease** **Ocular/vision disorders:** - **High prevalence – up to 60%** - strabismus (squint) - refractive errors (50%) - cataracts (15%) - keratoconus - blepharoconjunctivitis - Ch

title: Fragile X Syndrome
content: ## Fragile X - ID and delayed speech and language development. Males are usually more severely affected than females. Additional features: anxiety; attention deficit disorder (ADD); ASD affect communication and social interaction; and seizures. - Physical features: **long and narrow face**; **large ears**; a prominent jaw and forehead; **unusually flexible fingers**; **flat feet**; and in males, enlarged testicles (macroorchidism) \ First line testing: **X-linked dominant** - **Triplet repeat mutation in FMR1 gene at Xq28**: full mutation>200 CGG repeats - **FMR1 DNA testing**: **all DD/ID of

title: Intellectual disability
content: # ID: limitations in both intelligence and adaptive skills, presenting <18 - Developmental delay (DD) **< 5 years** - **ID > 5 years** - **NOT mental retardation** - Formal diagnosis **IQ<70** - Affects **1-3% of population** - mild IQ 55– 70 – 85% - moderate IQ 40– 55 - severe IQ 25– 40 - profound IQ <25 - Mild ID 30% **more common in males than females** - Mild ID **more often familial than severe ID** - Mild ID more prevalent in developing countries and low socioeconomic status Most common genetic causes of ID Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome, karyotype since 1960’s) – 1 in 700 - Most **common sin

title: Phenylketonuria
content: # Phenylketonuria **AR** inherited disorder ↑ phenylalanine (excreted as **phenylpyruvate**)– **PAH (liver catalyses conversion of phenylalanine to tyrosine) severely reduced or absent.** - **Very common in Turkey, 1/50 NSW is a PKU carrier.** - **Risk offspring will have PKU is 1/100 if one parent is a known carrier.** It is **found in all proteins and in some artificial sweeteners**. If **PKU is not treated**, phenylalanine can **build up to harmful levels competing with tyrosine and tryptophan for entry to brain (precursors of impt transmitters) ** ID etc. ↑phenylalanine during catabolic i

title: Turner's Syndrome
content: # Turner’s Syndrome Females: 45XO Signs and symptoms: **short stature** (apparent by 5), premature ovarian failure, "webbed" neck - a low or indistinct hairline in the back of the head - swelling (lymphedema) of the hands and feet - **broad chest and widely spaced nipples** - arms that **turn out slightly at the elbow** - **congenital heart defects** or heart murmur – **coarctation, truncal** - **scoliosis** (curving of the spine) or other skeletal abnormalities - kidney problems - horseshoe - an underactive thyroid gland - ↑risk of diabetes, especially if older or overweight - osteoporosis du

title: Velocardiofacial syndrome
content: # Velocardiofacial syndrome - Very variable phenotype – **requires genetic dx – with FISH** - Congenital heart disease ~70% VSD most common - Many psychiatric disorders – 25x psychosis, ↑ADHD

title: Lymphoma (Paediatrics)
content: # Lymphoma - General features - most common cause of a malignant pediatric neck mass - rare < 5y - Pathogenesis - Diffuse malignant tumour of lymphoid tissue - Clinical features - Cervical adnopathy more common in Hodgkin's lymphoma - Investigations - Biopsy - Hodgkin's lymphoma - presence of Reed-Sternberg cells - Management - Depends on cell type

title: Allergic rhinitis
content: ## **Allergic rhinitis** Allergic rhinitis (AR), or rhinoconjunctivits, is the commonest allergic disease in older children, affecting 15% of young teenagers. - **** Poorly controlled AR is associated with a significant –ve impact on quality of life, school attendance and performance, and sleep disturbance. - **** AR is a risk factor for asthma development. - **** AR and asthma often coexist, with up to 75% of older asthmatics reporting rhinitis symptoms. - **** AR impairs asthma control, and therefore, management of AR should be optimized in asthmatics. ### **Symptoms** - **** Nasal: rhinorrh

title: Drug allergies
content: ## **Drug allergy** Drug allergy is an immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to a medicinal product. In children, prevalence of a self-reported drug allergy is between 3% and 7%. Up to 95% of children with a plausible history of drug allergy are, in fact, able to tolerate the drug on re-challenge. Drug allergies should be investigated as they can lead to lifetime use of less effective and more costly treatments: - **** Children with chronic conditions requiring regular antibiotics, e.g. CF, are at higher risk of sensitization and allergy. - **** Atopy is not a risk factor for a drug allerg

title: Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome (FPIES)
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/Immunology and microbiology/Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome (FPIES).html
content: ## **Food protein-induced enterocolitis syndrome (FPIES)** FPIES is a rare non-IgE-mediated food allergy occurring classically in infants and young children. - **** Acute FPIES occurs a couple of hours post-food ingestion and is characterized by repeated vomiting, lethargy, pallor, and hypothermia. - **** 15% are at risk of hypovolaemic shock. - **** Sepsis is a possible differential, and it is not uncommon for these children to be treated repeatedly for sepsis before diagnosis. - **** Acute management is focused on aggressive fluid rehydration. - **** Common allergens are cow’s milk, wheat, r

title: Hereditary angioedema
content: ## **Hereditary angio-oedema** Due to mostly hereditary C1-esterase inhibitor deficiency: - **** AD, so family history may be present. - **** Attacks begin in later childhood and can be precipitated by trauma, infections, hormonal changes, and emotional stress. It does not cause urticaria. - **** Typically, C4 and C2 levels are low between attacks and undetectable during an attack; C1-inhibitor levels can also be low. - **** Treat major attacks with purified/recombinant inhibitor and supportive care as needed. Prophylaxis can be with modified androgens (e.g. danazol) or anti-fibrinolytics (tra

title: Mastocytosis
content: ## **Mastocytosis** Includes a range of disorders characterized by accumulation of mast cells: - **** Cutaneous mastocytosis: urticaria pigmentosa—itchy brown macules, with urticaria developing on scratching these (Darier’s sign). - **** Systemic mastocytosis: involves the gut, bone, and bone marrow and can present with abdominal symptoms, flushing, palpitation, headaches, SOB, and malaise. Differential diagnosis includes phaeochromocytoma and carcinoid. - **** Can be associated with haematological disorders: leukaemia, lymphoma, myelodysplasia. ### **Diagnosis** - **** Mast cell tryptase. - *

title: Venom allergies
content: ## **Venom allergy** Allergy to bee and wasp venom is a common cause of anaphylaxis and may result in death. In general, children have less severe reactions than adults. - **** All children with a systemic reaction to venom should be referred to an allergy specialist. - **** Large local reactions are common and do not indicate venom allergy, but rather the local histamine response to venom inoculation. - **** Family members of bee-keepers have sensitization rates of 30–60% to bee venom. - **** Venom allergy is not commoner in atopic individuals. - **** In suspected cases, sIgE to both venoms s

title: Potter sequence
content: # Potter sequence A collection of fetal abnormalities caused by oligohydramnios. The classical triad of Potter sequence is craniofacial abnormalities, clubbed feet, and pulmonary hypoplasia. The prognosis of patients born with Potter sequence depends on the root cause of oligohydramnios but the condition is often deadly due to pulmonary hypoplasia.

title: Depression
content: # Non-pharmacological treatment of depression Mild depression - psychotherapy alone No improvement in 6-8 weeks - consider pharmacotherapy (SSRI, e.g. Fluoxetine, setraline, escitalopram) If psychotherapy alone is declined, pharmacotherapy can be started Moderate to severe depression - psychotherapy AND pharmacotherapy preferred - increased likelihood of improvement Psychotherapy options 1. CBT 2. Interpersonal therapy for adolescents (IPT-A) 3. Family therapy, Dynamic therapy, Group therapy, Supportive therapy all shown to be ineffective

title: Nonpharmacological options for pain management
href: IM/Pain medicine/Nonpharmacological options for pain management.html
content: # Nonpharmacological options for pain management ## Options 1. Distraction techniques - *benefit for patients in ED and postoperative settings* - Music therapy 2. Structured physical therapy program for chronic pain - *high quality evidence that PT improves pain and function* - Continuation > 12 weeks based on clinical assessment and documented improvement 1. Cognitive behavioural techniques - CBT, mindfulness, biofeedback - *associated with reduced pain and improved overall function and mood* - CBT to treat depression may lead to substantial improvements in chronic pain and depressive symptom

title: Osteogenesis Imperfecta

title: Osteopetrosis

title: Urate lowering therapy
content: # Urate lowering therapy ## Indications - Frequent acute arthritis (>+ episodes/year) - tophaceous deposits - CKD (stage 2 or worse) ## Approach - Treat to target - aim for < 5mg/dL - lower uric acid levels does not benefit an acute gout flare ## Xanthine Oxidase Inhibitors - Drugs - Allopurinol - Febuxostat - MOA - blockade of final enzymatic production of uric acid → blocks plasma uric acid levels - Adverse events - precipitation of gouty attack - need to receive prophylactic colchicine dose - toxic epidermal necrolysis occurs in hypersensitivity (2% of cases) - STOP allopurinol IMMEDIATELY

title: Tumour Lysis Syndrome

title: Bladder cancer
content: ## Epidemiology - M:F = 3:1 - median age 73-74, older patient - 10% will present metastatic → generally incurable, 4% of all cancer deaths - Risk factors - Age - Tobacco Hx - Azo dyes - Infections - schistosomiasis results in SCC - arsenic, cyclophosphamide cancer - Race - whites - male - FHx - personal hx of bladder cancer ## Types of bladder cancer - urotherlial/TCC (90%) - Squamous (6-8%) - schistosomiasis - chronic catheterisation can result in nonschistosomiasis causes (spinal cord patients) - adenocarcinoma (2%) - rare - urachal vs. nonurachal (chronically irritated TCC) - small cell (<1

title: Febrile neutropenia
content: Febrile neutropenia - Definitions - Fever - PO temperature > 38.3 OR 38.0 for >1h - neutropenia - ANC < 500/uL OR ANC < 1000 with predicted nadir of <500 in next 48h - Risk for occult infection and mortality increases as ANC falls below 1000 - greatest risk with ANC < 500 - mortality rate for solid tumours less than haem malignancies - solid tumour febrile neutropenia vs. leukemia/lymphoma neutropenia better - growth factors (GCSF) MODESTLY reduces neutropenia and hospitalisation (<1 day difference), with no improvement on mortality - no benefit to be using GCSF in uncomplicated febrile neutro

title: Gastrointestinal cancers and chemotherapy

title: Pancreatic cancer
content: # Pancreatic cancer ## Epidemiology - survival after pancreatectomy not significantly improved over non-pancreatectomy patients - risk factors - Hereditary - Hereditary chronic pancreatitis - Peutz-Jeghers syndrome - Ataxia-telangiectasia - BRCA2 - Chronic pancreatitis - mixed data - Diabetes (RR = 2) - Smoking - Obesity - History of partial gastrectomy ## Treatments - Local disease → surgery + adjuvant therapy - not affecting surrounding vessels - SMA, SMV, coeliac - localised to pancreas only - Adjuvant therapy - FOLFIRINOX achieving 30-40% 5-year survival post pancreatectomy - curative inte

title: Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Syndrome
content: Superior vena cava syndrome - overwhelming majority 2ndary to malignancy - external compression from mediastinal mass - most common mediastinal cancers - lung cancer (NSCLC, SCLC), lymphoma - thrombosis 2ndary to catheters (CVCs) can cause SVC syndrome - especially in chemotherapy patients - Physical SSx - facial oedema and plethora - JV distension - venous distension of superficial veins on chest - SSx - dyspnoea - gradual process; not the initial complaint - cough - hoarseness of voice - Management - Tissue diagnosis is CRITICAL for diagnosis - prognosis dependent on underlying cause - treat

title: Behcets disease

One-third of cases are idiopathic Three types 1. I (25%) 1. monoclonal (IgG, IgM) 2. asosciated with MM, Waldenstroms 2. title: The Kidney
content: # The kidney ## Description ### The kidney - The kidney is approx 12 x 6 x 3cm and ~120g - The kidney has a capsule which gives it a glistening appearance - Medially the hilum contains the renal artery and vein, and the renal pelvis (which becomes the ureter) - the renal pelvis emerges posterior to the vessels ### Perinephric fat - the perinephric fat lies outside the renal capsule and is more solid at body temperature than general body fat - this fills in the space and positions the kidney in the paravertebral gutter - after severe weight loss, loss of perinephric fat results in nephroptosis

title: Subcutaneous tissue
content: # Subcutaneous tissue ## Background - Technically not part of the skin - Lies BELOW the [Skin](Skin.md) and above the Deep fascia - Otherwise known as *superficial fascia, hypodermis, tela subcutanea* ## Components 1. Fat - acts as a shock absorber and protective cushion - insulates deep tissue - determines the thickness of the subcutnaeous tissue - not present in tissue of eyelids, ear, scrotum, penis or clitoris 1. Connecting bones via hyaline cartilage 2. Also doesn't necessarily enable movement 3. Examples 1. Between epiphysis and metaphysis (i.e. the physis/growth plate) 2. Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P) and Dehydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) - Dehydroxyacetone phosphate (DHAP) can convert to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate 6. 2x Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (G3P) 7. 2x 1,3-bisphosphylglycerate 8. 2x 3-phosphylglycerate 9. 2x 2-phosphylglycerate 10. 2x phosphylenolpyruvate 11. 2x pyruvate ## Figure ![](../Phys

title: Glycolysis
content: # Glycolysis ## Background Glycolysis is the breakdown of glucose to produce ATP $$ \text{Glucose} + 2NAD^+ + 2ADP + 2P_i \rightarrow 2\text{Pyruvate} + 2NADH + 2ATP + 2H_2O + 2H^+$$ $$\Delta G_1^{'o} = -146kJ/mol$$ In this process: - Glucose (6 carbons) into 2 pyruvates (2x 3-carbon molecules) - Utilise 2 ATP (investment phase) to produce 4 ATP -> NET production of 2 ATP - Reduction of $NAD^+$ to $NADH$ - gains a $H^+$ and an $e^-$ - NADH can be oxidised in the [Electron Transport Chain](Electron%20Transport%20Chain.md) to produce ATPs later - The final carbon compound is pyruvate - this cont

title: Iron metabolism
content: # Iron metabolism ## Iron intake ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221103114119.png) - Dietary iron (Fe3+) is reduced to Fe2+ in the gut - Fe2+ is transported by DMT1 (divalent metal transporter 1) found in the enterocyte (cells of the GIT) INTO the enterocyte - Iron is absorbed in the duodenum and proximal jejunal segments of the gut - Fe2+ is exported OUT of the enterocyte via the basolateral membrane via ferroportin, or it is stored as ferritin - ???binding to transferrin - Fe2+ can be transported out of the enterocyte, oxidised to Fe3+ and bind transferrin for mobilisation in bloodstream ## Iron

title: Branchial Apparatus

title: Cardiac development
content: ## Overview - Heart development - 5 dilatations along primitive heart tube forms into adult heart structures - dextral looping of the primitive heart aligns the heart chambers and structures - septum and valves develop which allow for separation of the venous and arterial circulatory pathways - heart begins beating during week 4 - can be detected by transvaginal ultrasound by week 6 ## Embryonic structures ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221103104837.png) - Truncus arteriosus - neural crest cells migrate from hindbrain to aorticopulmonary (AP) trunk - cells invade truncal and bulbar ridges - the r

title: Fetal circulation and erythropoiesis
content: ## Overview - Umbilical vein - returns 80% saturated blood from the placenta to the fetus - Umbilical arteries - carry mainly deoxygenated blood back to the placenta for oxygenation - Erythropoeisis - begins at week 3 ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221103105408.png) ## Circulatory Shunts - Ductus venosus - oxygenated blood returning from the placenta via the umbilical vein is shunted away from the liver via the ductus venosus to the IVC - becomes ligamentum venosum upon closure at birth - Foramen ovale - due to the increased pulmonary resistance, oxygenated blood entering the right atrium is dive

title: Renal embryology
content: # Renal Embryology ## Background There are three excretory organs related to the kidney during development: 1. Pronephros - excretory tutbules arranged segmentally, emptying into the mesoonephric duct 2. Mesonephros - excretory tutbules arranged segmentally, emptying into the mesoonephric duct 3. Metanephros - mass of tubules with no segmental arrangement, draining into a new duct (the ureter) ## Development of the excretory organs 1. title: Teratogens
content: **Introduction** - Overview - teratogens are agents that can cause abnormal fetal development and birth defects - effects vary depending on timing of fetal exposure to the teratogen - weeks 1-3 of gestation - "all-or-none" effects - the embryo either dies or survives without any complications - weeks 3-8 of gestation - embryonic period - most susceptible time period due to organogenesis - week 8 of gestation until birth - growth and function of organs are affected ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221103105302.png) ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221103105309.png)

title: Umbilical cord and remnants

title: Organisation of immunity
content: ## Organisation The immune system is organised on two levels 1. Systemic level - bloodstream, thymus, bone, spleen 1. defends INTERNALLY 2. thymus, bone marrow are **central organs of immunity** 1. all cells related to immunity originate from bone marrow 2. t-cells mature in thymus, b-cells mature in bone marrow 3. thymus governs the whole immune system 3. spleen, lymph nodes, lymphoid elements, appendix, lymphatics, skin, liver are **secondary/peripheral organs of immunity** 4. mucosae-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) organises some essential secondary organs of immunity 2. Skin and mucosal

title: Fracture healing
content: # Fracture healing ## Background Fracture healing occurs in two different ways 1. Direct (primary) healing - Direct healing - bone WITHOUT callous formation - good cortical apposition, absolute stability, no movement/gaps between fragments - normal osteoclastic-mediated remodelling is directed across fracture interface - osteoclastic cutting-cones cut across fracture line - osteoblasts lay down lamellar bone across fracture 1. Indirect (secondary) healing - most common form - transition from one tissue to another with callus formation - Process of healing (*union*) occurs by: - Fracture hemato

title: Venous thrombus
content: # Venous thrombus ## Background - occurs in areas of decreased or mechanically altered blood flow - pockets adjacent to valves in deep veins - potential locations of venous stasis and hypoxia - noted in postmortem studies where venous thrombi are found in these sinuses - MOA - blood flow slows → O2 tension declines with increase in hematocrit - hypercoagulable microenvironment may downregulate antithrombotic proteins that are preferentially expressed on venous valves (incl. thrombomodulin, endothelial protein C receptor) - hypoxia drives expression of procoagulants (P-selectin - adhesin attrac

title: Wound healing
content: # Wound Healing ## Wounds - interruption in the continuity of epithelium - classified as: - surgical - due to surgery - traumatic - laceration or tissue loss - neoplastic - tumour ulceration when outgrowing blood supply - infective - e.g. eruption of an abscess ## PHases of wound healing - Three phases of wound healing 1. Inflammatory - clot, exudate, PMN within 24-74 hours of injury - control of bleeding 2. Proliferative - angiogenesis and granulation and epithelialisation (grow/heal) starts from 4 days and lasts 1-2 weeks 3. remodelling - wound heals to increase strength (scarring or shaping

content: # Diuretics ![](../figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402140400.png) 1. Glomerulus - *Mannitol* - PD: Freely filtered at glomerulus, poor resorption at tubules - remains in lumen and acts as an osmotic agent - Effect: - Reduced brain volume, intracranial pressure (osmotic drawing of water from tissue) - Clinical uses - Haemolysis - Rhabdomyolysis - Reducing intra-ocular and intra-cranial pressures - Side effects - Sodium wasting/hypokalaemia - Pulmonary oedmea - headache, nausea, vomiting 2. title: Insulin
content: # Insulin a. Method of administration. Self-administered by SC injection in abdomen, buttocks, arm, and leg. Given intravenously for emergency ketoacidosis. b. Regimens Most type I diabetic patients require 0.5 to 1.0 unit/kg per day to achieve acceptable glycemic control. Start with a conservative dose (0.2 units/kg) and adjust the regimen according to the patient’s glucose levels. Many different regimens exist, and every patient has unique needs (see Table 4-5). c. title: Cardiac Action Potential
content: # Cardiac Action Potential ## Fast response action potential - Atria, Ventricles, His-purkinjie system 1. Phase 0: Rapid depolarisation, overshoots to slightly more positive - Inward Na+ 2. Phase 1: Brief repolarisation 3. Phase 2: Plateau of voltage 4. Phase 3: Repolarisation back to resting potential - Outward K+ ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220808192025.png) ## Slow response action potential - See [Pacemaker cardiomyocytes](Pacemaker%20cardiomyocytes.md) - Seen in SA and AV nodes - No Phase 1 and 2 in cycle

title: Central Venous Pressure
content: # Central Venous Pressure ## Background - Central venous pressure (CVP) is the pressure in the **thoracic vena cava near the right atrium** - as the right atrium at rest is passively filled by the vena cava, it is essentially equal to the right atrial pressure - central venous pressure deterimines atrial filling - as the majority of ventricular filling (~70%) is passively filled via the atrium, this will also determine ventricular filling - degree of ventricular filling is also known as the preload - therefore CVP is a deterinant of the right ventricular preload - The change in CVP is defined

title: Frank-Starling Mechanism
content: # Frank-Starling Mechanism ## Background - venous return into the heart affects the **strength** and thus its **stroke volume** - increasing venous return increases the **filling pressure** (also known as the End Diastolic Pressure; when referring to the systemic circulation, we describe the Left Ventricular End Diastolic Pressure, or LVEDP). - This response has a mechanism based on optimising sarcomere length - with more preload (LVEDP), the sarcomeres are stretched more optimally, allowing for stronger contractile strength - this is ultimately a local mechanism with nil extrinsic neurohumora

title: Cellular energy production
content: # Cellular energy production ## Background Energy utilised by the cells (ATP) can be produced from a multiple pathways. ## Cellular energy production using GLUCOSE 1. Dietary carbohydrates are consumed and broken into hexoses (glucose, galactose, fructose) - of which the most important are the D-isomers 2. Glucose circulates in the blood and can enter cells 3. Intracellular glucose is phosphorylated into glucose-6-phosphate by **hexokinase**. - hepatocytes have **glucokinase** which also can phosphorylate glucose and can also be upregulated by insulin (and decreased in starvation and diabetes)

title: Cellular Respiration
content: # Cellular Respiration ## Background Cellular respiration undergoes the general formula: $$ C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 \rightarrow 6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{energy (ATP and heat)}$$ The steps of cellular respiration are: 1. [Glycolysis](../../Biochemistry/Glycolysis.md) - The breakup of glucose (gluco = sugar, lysis = breakdown) in the absence of oxygen (i.e. anaerobic) - This process breaks down glucose (6 carbon molecule) into a 3 carbon molecule (pyruvate) - This process: - utilises 2 ATP to generate 4 ATP (i.e. a net 2 ATP gain) - Converts 10 NAD+ to NADH (which is then used to drive the [Electron Tr

Shape change - platelets turn from smooth discs to spine spheres with protruding pseudopodia - this increases its reach and contact with other platelets 3. title: B-cell mediated (Humoral) immunity
content: # Humoral immunity ## Background - Mediated by proteins called **[Antibodies](Antibodies.md)** which are produced by [B cell (B lymphocyte)](../../Histology/B%20cell%20(B%20lymphocyte).md)s - Antibodies are found - Circulating in blood - Extracellular tissue fluids - Mucosal organ lumen (e.g. GIT, respiratory tract) - The roles of antibodies include - Neutralising invading tissue cells and inducing phagocytosis - *note that whilst extracellular microbes can be effectively neutralised, intracellular microbes aren't as easily affected by antibodies* - Neutralising toxins - Can recognise proteins

title: Estimating Glomerular Filtration Rate
content: # Estimating GFR ## Background ## Serum creatinine - Creatinine freely filtered by glomerulus and secreted into ultrafiltrate at proximal tubule - PCT secretion can be reduced by some drugs - Cimetidine, Cobicistat, Dolutegravir, Bictegravir, Trimethoprim - Non-linear relationship between serum creatinine and GRE - As GFR declines, the proportion of tubular secretion of total creatinine excretion will increase - serum creatinine will OVERESTIMATE true GFR - With loss of kidney function (anuria), SCr can increase by 1mg/dL (88.4umol/L) per day - Creatinine - Metabolite of creatine, prsent in sk

title: Fluid components
content: # Fluid components ## Background Fluid in the body are 'organised' into two main components 1. Intracellular fluid (ICF) - Accounts for ~ 40% of body weight 2. Extracellular fluid (ECF) - This forms a 'sea' bathing cells and allowing nutrient transfer - this provides cells with O2 and nutrients, and take up the metabolic waste products of cells - compositionally resembles sea-water with Na+ and Cl- in relatively higher concentrations - This is further divided into 1. Blood plasma - Accounts for ~ 5% of body weight 2. Interstitial fluid - excessive interstitial fluid can result in [Oedema](../.

title: Renal Perfusion Autoregulation
content: # Renal autoregulation ## Background Perfusion autoregulation is related to how drugs can affect renal fluid management. In order to change the intraglomerular pressure, considerations include 1. Pressure of blood flow INTO the kidneys (afferent arteriole tension) 1. Mesangial cell autoregulation 2. Reduced blood flow → local prostaglandin production → Vasodilation of afferent arterioles - NSAIDs can stop this from working 2. Pressure of blood flow OUT of the kidneys (efferent arteriole tension) 1. Mesangial cell autoregulation 2. Reduced blood flow and Juxtaglomerular apparatus → Renin produc

title: Virchow's triad
content: # Virchow's triad ## Background - First described in 1856 - Three factors in the formation of thrombosis - Venous stasis - most consequential of the three factors, but requires the other two to be present to greatly increase risk of clot formation - Vascular injury - Hypercoagulability - applies clinically to - surgery or trauma - malignancy - prolonged immobility - proegnancy - congestive heart failure - varicose veins - obesity - advanced aging - DVT history ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221113152003.png) ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020221113152015.png) ## Relevant links

content: # Acute Pericarditis ## **General Characteristics** - Inflammation of the pericardial sac—may be an isolated finding or part of an underlying disorder or generalized disease - Causes **(CARDIAC RINDS)** - Collagen vascular diseases (e.g., SLE, scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis, sarcoidosis) ([Connective Tissue Diseases](https://www.notion.so/Connective-Tissue-Diseases-16b664eebf2b4b27913051986c5b053f)) - Aortic dissection ([Aortic dissection](https://www.notion.so/Aortic-dissection-5a37aec1c75247c8a9e54a895eba96d0)), Amyloidosis - Radiation - Drug-induced lupus syndrome (procainamide, hydralaz

title: Aortic Regurgitation
content: # Aortic Regurgitation ## Background - Acute - Infective endocarditis - Trauma - Aortic dissection - Iatrogenic as during a failed replacement surgery - Chronic - Valvular causes - Congenital [Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV)](https://www.notion.so/Bicuspid-Aortic-Valve-BAV-939a4d42194c43e598fe4f34cf865b75) - [Infective endocarditis](https://www.notion.so/Infective-endocarditis-d579dc4572844501865d2263183fc7a4) , [Rheumatic heart disease](https://www.notion.so/Rheumatic-heart-disease-3697812c777f4f29bc72ec07270a1a21) (will not typically occur without MR and AS) - Myxomatous ([Atrial Myxoma](https:/

title: Aortic Stenosis
content: # Aortic Stenosis ## Background - Causes - Calcification of a congenitally abnormal [Bicuspid Aortic Valve (BAV)](https://www.notion.so/Bicuspid-Aortic-Valve-BAV-939a4d42194c43e598fe4f34cf865b75) - most common cause in older people - Calcification of tricuspid aortic valve in elderly (degenerative) - [Rheumatic heart disease](https://www.notion.so/Rheumatic-heart-disease-3697812c777f4f29bc72ec07270a1a21) - Commisural fusion → converts tricuspid into pseudo-bicuspid valves → makes leaflets more susceptible to trauma → fibrosis, calcification → valvular narrowing → LV outflow obstruction - Assoc

title: Atrial Flutter
content: # Atrial Flutter ### **General Characteristics** - Pathophysiology - One irritable automaticity focus in the atria fires at about 250 to 350 bpm **(typically very close to 300 bpm)**, giving rise to regular atrial contractions. - Atrial rate between 250 and 350, around **300 bpm**. The long refractory period in the AV node allows only one out of every two or three flutter waves to conduct to the ventricles. - Limited conduction means that the ventricles can only contract at 75-175bpm - Causes - Heart disease: Heart failure (most common association), rheumatic heart disease, CAD - COPD, other h

title: Atrial Myxoma
content: # Atrial Myxoma ### General characteristics - An atrial myxoma is a benign gelatinous growth, usually pedunculated and arising from the interatrial septum of the heart in the region of the fossa ovalis. It is the most common primary cardiac neoplasm. - The majority of myxomas are sporadic, but autosomal-dominant transmission has been noted. - Although benign, atrial myxomas can embolize, leading to metastatic disease, or can cause relative valvular dysfunction. ### Clinical features - Prototypically present with fatigue, fever, syncope, palpitations, malaise, and a lowpitched diastolic murmur

title: Bicuspid Aortic Valve
content: # Bicuspid Aortic Valve ## Background - Approximately 1–2% of the population is born with a congenitally abnormal aortic valve, making it one of the most common types of CHD. - Patients born with a bicuspid aortic valve have structural abnormalities of the ascending aorta predisposing them to aneurysm and dissection. - It is estimated that bicuspid valve disease accounts for more morbidity and mortality than all other congenital cardiac lesions combined. ## Pathology - Evidence now points to a congenital pattern of inheritance, and the fundamental embryology is being elucidated in animal model

title: Cardiac neoplasms
content: # Cardiac Neoplasms Primary tumors of the heart are rare (typically less than 0.1% of the general population). Metastases from other primary tumors are more common (75% of cardiac neoplasms). Sites of these primary tumors include the lung, breast, skin, kidney, lymphomas, and Kaposi sarcoma in patients with AIDS. ## Topics 1. [Atrial Myxoma](Atrial%20Myxoma.md) 2. [Primary Cardiac Tumours](Primary%20Cardiac%20Tumours.md)

title: Cardiac Tamponade
content: # Cardiac tamponade ### **General Characteristics** - Defined as accumulation of pericardial fluid. It is the rate of fluid accumulation that is important, not the amount. - Two hundred milliliters of fluid that develops rapidly (i.e., blood secondary to trauma) can cause cardiac - Two liters of fluid may accumulate slowly before cardiac tamponade occurs. When fluid accumulates slowly, the pericardium has the opportunity to stretch and adapt to the increased volume (i.e., related to a malignancy). - Pathophysiology - Pericardial effusion that mechanically impairs diastolic filling of the - Cha

title: Cardiorespiratory arrest
content: # Cardiorespiratory arrest ## Background - ~ 20% of inpatient cardiac arrests will survive to go home - 80% of arrests demonstrate deteriorating a few hours before the arrest ## Assessment of cardiorespiratory arrest ### Airway - complete obstruction - partial obstruction - precedes complete obstruction - can cause cerebral or pulmonary oedema, exhaustion, apnoea, hypoxic brain injury, cardiac arrest - CNS depression -> loss of airway patency and protcetive reflexes - Head injury ,intracerebral disease, hypercarbia, metabolic disorder depressant effect (e.g. diabetes mellitus), drugs (alcohol,

title: Constrictive Pericarditis
content: # Constrictive Pericarditis ### General Characteristics - Fibrous scarring of the pericardium leads to rigidity and thickening of the pericardium, with obliteration of the pericardial cavity. - Pathophysiology - A fibrotic, rigid pericardium restricts the diastolic filling of the heart. - Ventricular filling is unimpeded during early diastole because intracardiac volume has not yet reached the limit defined by the stiff pericardium. - When intracardiac volume reaches the limit set by the noncompliant pericardium, ventricular filling is halted abruptly. (In contrast, ventricular filling is impe

title: Hypertension in children
content: # Hypertension in children ## Antecedents of adult disease **Hypertension** Measurement of BP - Cuff size – bladder size, not overall cuff – if in doubt use larger - **Width >40% of MUAC** - **Length 80-100% MUAC** - Position of child - Sitting **legs uncrossed**, cuff at heart level - Younger children – may be lying down with **arm supported at level of heart** - Hypertension: **>95th centile** - Consider **24 hour ambulatory BP monitor** (child > 5- 6 yrs) ## Causes of Hypertension in Childhood ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220402171114.png) Ix - UEC, FBC (glucose/lipids if overweight

title: Hypertension
content: # Hypertension ## Background - BP is a continuous variable with a relationship to CVD outcomes - Stroke, MI, HF, CKD - Increased morality with SBP > 115mmHg - Epidemiology - increasing HTN in low-middle countries and around the world ## Primary hypertension ## Secondary hypertension - Secondary hypertension - underlying and potentially reversible causes - accounts for 5-10% of elevated BP - In children and young adults (<35 years) - renal parenchymal disease and CoA - Adults >65 - Atherosclerotic renal artery stenosis, CKD, hypothyroidism - Other causes - hyperaldoesteronism, OSA, phaeochromo

title: Libman-Sacks Endocarditis (Nonbacterial Verrucous Endocarditis)
content: # Libman-Sacks Endocarditis ## Background - nonbacterial verrucous thrombi on mitral or aortic valve can be present on either surface of the valve → caused by SLE induced autoimmune destruction of the valves - Typically involves the aortic valves in individuals with SLE - Characterized by the formation of small warty vegetations on both sides of valve leaflets and may present with regurgitant murmurs - Rarely gives rise to infective endocarditis, but can be a source of systematic embolization - Considered pathognomonic of SLE-associated valve disease, noninfective valve vegetations are almost

title: Long QT syndromes
content: ## Long QT Syndromes Causes: - [Hypokalemia](../Nephrology/Electrolyte%20disturbances/Hypokalemia.md) - Hypokalaemia causes apparent QTc prolongation in the limb leads (due to T-U fusion) with prominent U waves in the precordial leads. - [Hypomagnesaemia](../Nephrology/Electrolyte%20disturbances/Hypomagnesaemia.md) - [Hypocalcaemia](../Nephrology/Electrolyte%20disturbances/Hypocalcaemia.md) - Hypocalcaemia typically prolongs the ST segment, leaving the T wave unchanged. - Hypothermia - Severe hypothermia can cause marked QTc prolongation, often in association with bradyarrhythmias (especially

title: Mitral Regurgitation
content: # Mitral Regurgitation ## Background - Causes - Acute - Endocarditis (most often Staphylococcus aureus) - Papillary muscle rupture (from infarction) or dysfunction (from ischemia) - Chordae tendineae rupture - Chronic - Mitral valve prolapse (MVP) - Rheumatic fever - Marfan syndrome - Cardiomyopathy causing dilation of mitral annulus - **Etiology (Primary/secondary)** - Primary MR - Degenerative [mitral valve](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/vvCWAb8n0/19/article/Up0bKS#Z5804f1ad30d669a2fa653b583a64120d) disease ([mitral valve prolapse](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/vvCWAb8n0/19/art

title: Mitral Stenosis
content: # Mitral Stenosis ## Background - Almost all cases are due to rheumatic heart disease. (Patient may not recall a history of rheumatic fever.) - Also occurs in pregnant women (due to 50% increase in plasma volume) - Pathophysiology - Immune-mediated damage to the mitral valve (due to rheumatic fever) caused by cross- reactivity between the streptococcal antigen and the valve tissue leads to scarring and narrowing of the mitral valve orifice. - Mitral stenosis results in elevated left atrial and pulmonary venous pressure leading to pulmonary congestion. - Anything that increases flow across the

title: Mitral Valve Prolapse (Barlow Syndrome)
content: # Mitral Valve Prolapse ## Background - MVP is defined as the presence of excessive or redundant mitral leaflet tissue due to myxomatous degeneration of mitral valve leaflets and/or chordae tendineae. The redundant leaflet(s) prolapse toward the LA in systole, which results in the auscultated click and murmur. - MVP is common in patients with genetic connective tissue disorders, such as Marfan syndrome, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers–Danlos syndrome. - MVP is a common cause of MR in developed countries. - Arrhythmias and sudden death are very rare. ## Pathology - The most common underlyin

title: Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia
content: # Multifocal Atrial Tachycardia ### Background - **Risk factors** - Usually occurs in patients with severe pulmonary disease (e.g., COPD) - Looks similar to the wandering atrial pacemaker (except wandering atrial pacemaker has a HR of 60-100bpm) ### **Diagnosis** - ECG findings: Variable P-wave morphology and variable PR and RR intervals. A**t least three different P-wave morphologies are required to make an accurate diagnosis** - The diagnosis of wandering atrial pacemaker is identical except that the heart rate is between 60 and 100 bpm (i.e., not tachycardic). - Can also be diagnosed by use

title: Pacemaker
content: # Cardiac Pacemaker ## Background - Device that delivers direct electrical stimulation to the heart when the heart's natural pacemaker is unable to do so - There are three types - Permanent implantable system for long-term treatment - Temporary systems - either transcutaneous (with electrode pads over chest) or transvenous - both use an external pulse generator that patient can secure to the waist with straps - On ECG, cardiac pacing is noted by the presence of a "spike". - Indications - Sinus node dysfunction is the most common indication - Symptomatic heart block - [Mobitz type II](Second%20

title: Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia
content: # Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia ### **General Characteristics** **_Regular tachycardia without P-waves, fibrillatory waves or flutter waves_** - Pathophysiology (most often due to reentry) - AV nodal reentrant tachycardia - Two pathways (one fast and the other slow) within the AV node, so the reentrant circuit is within the AV node - Most common cause of supraventricular tachyarrhythmia (SVT) - Initiated or terminated by PACs - ECG: Narrow QRS complexes with no discernible P waves (P waves are buried within the QRS complex). This is because the circuit is short and conduction is rapi

title: Pericardial Effusion
content: # Pericardial Effusion General Characteristics** - Defined as any cause of acute pericarditis (see above) that can lead to exudation of fluid into the pericardial space - Can occur in association with ascites and pleural effusion in salt and water retention states such as CHF, cirrhosis, and nephrotic syndrome - Is often asymptomatic and suspected based on the symptoms of the underlying condition - May be acute or chronic. Acute effusions have higher risk of progressing to tamponade - Aetiology - [Hemopericardium](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/vvCWAb8n0/3/article/uh0pff#Z850dc71b17efabd

title: Premature Atrial Complexes
content: # Premature Atrial Complexes ## Background 1. This early beat arises within the atria, firing on its own. 2. Causes include adrenergic excess, drugs, alcohol, tobacco, electrolyte imbalances, ischemia, hypoxia, and infection. 3. Premature atrial complexes (PACs) are found in more than 50% of normal adults who undergo Holter monitoring and are of no significance in a normal heart, but may be a precursor of ischemia in a diseased heart. 4. May cause palpitations or give rise to PSVTs. 5. These are common and of minimal concern - treat symptomatically with beta blockers or non-DHP CCBs - The abno

title: Premature Ventricular Complexes
content: # Premature Ventricular Complexes ### Background 1. This early beat fires on its own from a focus in the ventricle and then spreads to the other ventricle 2. PVCs can occur in patients with or without structural heart disease. Causes include hypoxia, electrolyte abnormalities, stimulants, caffeine, medications, and structural heart disease. 3. Since conduction is not through normal conduction pathways, but rather through ventricular muscle, it is slower than normal, causing a **wide QRS.** 4. PVCs appear in more than 50% of men who undergo 24-hour Holter monitoring. 5. Presence of PVCs in pati

title: Rheumatic heart disease
content: # Rheumatic heart disease ## Background - Rheumatic heart disease occurs as a complication of **streptococcal pharyngitis** (group A streptococcus). - **Acute rheumatic fever** is an immunologically mediated systemic process that may progress to rheumatic heart disease. - **Rheumatic heart disease describes the chronic valvular abnormalities** secondary to acute rheumatic fever. - The most common valvular abnormality is mitral stenosis, but patients may have aortic or tricuspid involvement as well. - The incidence of rheumatic heart disease has fallen drastically in industrialized countries an

title: Stable Angina
content: # Stable Angina ## Background - Stable angina pectoris is due to fixed atherosclerotic lesions that narrow the major coronary arteries. Coronary ischemia is due to an imbalance between blood supply and oxygen demand, leading to inadequate perfusion. Stable angina occurs when oxygen demand exceeds available blood supply. - **Major risk factors** - Diabetes mellitus (DM)—worst risk factor - Hyperlipidemia—elevated low-density lipoprotein (LDL) - Hypertension (HTN)—most common risk factor - Cigarette smoking - Age (men >45 years; women >55 years) - Family history of premature coronary artery dise

title: Third degree (complete) AV block
content: # Third-Degree (Complete) AV Block - Absence of conduction of atrial impulses to the ventricles; no correspondence between P waves and QRS complexes - A ventricular pacemaker (escape rhythm) maintains a ventricular rate of 25 to 40 bpm - Characterized by AV dissociation - Pacemaker implantation is necessary. - Causes - possible complication of an Inferior wall MI - no electrical communication b/w atria and ventricles - Signs - bradycardia - hypotension - cannon A-waves - contraction of the atrium against closed tricuspid valve - Diagnosis - ECG; no relation between P waves and QRS complexes -

title: Tricuspid regurgitation
content: # Tricuspid regurgitation ## Background 1. Tricuspid regurgitation (TR) results from a failure of the tricuspid valve to close completely during systole, causing regurgitation of blood into the RA. It is estimated that up to 70% of normal adults have mild, physiologic TR as seen on high-resolution echocardiography. A much smaller percentage of people are actually symptomatic. 2. Causes—up to 90% of cases occur in people with anatomically normal valve leaflets and chords. - TR is usually secondary to RV dilation. Any cause of RV dilation can result in enlargement of the tricuspid orifice. - Lef

title: Trifascicular block
content: # Trifascicular block - Definition: features of bifascicular block as above + 1st-degree heart block - Common cause of falls in the elderly ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220730084327.png)

title: Ventricular Tachycardia
content: # Ventricular Tachycardia ## Background - rapid and repetitive firing of three or more PVCs in a row, at a rate of between 100 and 250 bpm - AV dissociation is present - Originates below the bundle of His - Causes - Substrate related (90%) - CAD with prior MI is the most common cause - Active ischemia, hypotension - Cardiomyopathies, especially those with infiltration into myocardium such as sarcoidosis - Ventricular scar tissue - Idiopathic (no structural heart disease) (10%) - Congenital defects - Long QT syndrome (results in torsades de pointes) - Drug toxicity - *tend to present with frequ

title: Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome
content: # Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) Syndrome ### **General Characteristics** - An accessory conduction pathway from atria to ventricles through the **bundle of Kent** causes premature ventricular excitation because it lacks the delay seen in the AV node. - May lead to a paroxysmal tachycardia, which can be produced by two possible mechanisms: - Orthodromic reciprocating tachycardia (orthodromic AVRT) - The impulse travels through the AV node (anterograde limb) and depolarizes the ventricles. Then it travels back through the accessory pathway (the retrograde limb) and depolarizes the atria again, cre

title: Erythema nodosum
content: # Erythema nodosum ## Background - erysipelas and cellulitis mimic ## Pathology - inflammation of subcutaneous fat, typically over shins - Associations - infections - streptococci, tuberculosis, brucellosis - Systemic disease - sarcoidosis, IBD, Behcet's - Malignancy/lymphoma - Drugs - COCP - Penicillins, sulphonamides - Pregnancy ## Clinical features - single or multiple tender nodules on extensor surface of lower extremities - slightly raised red lesions, obviously inflamed, across both shins - oval/round in shape - no ulcers ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240217201411.png) ![](../../f

title: Intertrigo
content: # Intertrigo ## Background - Dermatitis of adjacent skin folds (axillary, inframammary, abdominal, inguinal) - Risk factors - Obesity - Friction - Occlusion - Immune response inference (E.g. diabetes) ## Clinical features - Erythematous, macerated plaques - Secondary candida infection - possible multiple small red papules and pustules around main rash ('satellite pustules') ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220721183242.png) ## Management - Moisture reduction and maceration - drying agents (e.g. drying powder) - Prevention of secondary yeast infection - if candida suspected, concomitant tre

title: Venous stasis dermatitis
content: # Venous stasis dermatitis ## Background - associated with chronic venous dermatitis - weeping plaques with vesicles and crusting - can be secondarily infected with bacteria or candida ## Clinical features - erythematous - scaling and eczematous patches or plaques ## Management - as per [Chronic Venous Insufficiency](../../Surgery/Vascular%20surgery/Chronic%20Venous%20Insufficiency.md) - Sparing use of a topical steroid - Wound care - hydrocolloids and foam dressings - control ulcer trainage and prevent skin maceration ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220726153516.png) --- # Venous stasis

title: Addison disease
content: # Primary adrenal Insufficiency (Addison's disease) A. Causes 1. Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) (see also Clinical Pearl 4-13). a. Idiopathic (thought to be autoimmune disease) is the most common type in the industrialized world. b. Infectious diseases—these include tuberculosis (most common cause worldwide) and fungal infections. Causes also include cytomegalovirus, cryptococcus, toxoplasmosis, and pneumocystis. c. Iatrogenic—for example, a bilateral adrenalectomy. d. Metastatic disease—from lung or breast cancer. B. Clinical Features 1. Lack of cortisol a. GI symptoms—anorex

title: Clinical approach to Insulin
content: # Clinical approach to Insulin see [Insulin](../../BCS/Pharmacology/Classes/Insulin.md) ## Inpatient management - Aim BGL < 10 whilst avoiding hypoglycaemia - Basal bolus vs sliding scale - basal bolus has better control and patient outcomes ## Starting new insulin 1. Weight based - total insulin as percentage of weight - 0.3-0.5U/kg to start as a daily - 50% goes into basal insulin - 50% goes into bolus insulin - titrate insulin based on risk factors (e.g. hypoglycaemia) - better to be more conservative and increase insulin doses (Rather than decrease after a aspiration event!) ## Adjusting i

title: Insulinoma
content: # Insulinoma ### A. General Characteristics 1. Insulin-producing tumor arising from the β-cells of the pancreas 2. Associated with MEN I syndrome [Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)](Multiple%20Endocrine%20Neoplasia%20(MEN).md) 3. Usually benign (in up to 90% of the cases) ### B. Clinical Features - Fasting hypoglycemia, which leads to: 1. Sympathetic activation—diaphoresis, palpitations, tremors, high blood pressure, anxiety 2. Neuroglycopenic symptoms—headache, visual disturbances, confusion, seizures, coma - Presents with Whipple's triad ### C. Diagnosis 1. Screening test: Hypoglycaemic hyp

title: Thyroid nodules
content: ## Background - Cancer is found in 4% to 10% of nodules that are investigated. - Causes - Thyroid cancer - benign adenoma - multinodular condition - only one of these nodules may be palpable ## Examination - Detecting the thyroid nodule is the goal - not determining benign/malignancy - To be detectable on palpation, a nodule must be at least 1 cm in diameter. ## Prognosis - Malignancy is suggested by the following: - If the nodule is fixed in place and no movement occurs on swallowing - Unusually firm consistency or irregularity of the nodule - If the nodule is solitary - History of radiation

title: Type I Diabetes
content: # Type I Diabetes ## Background - This is characterized by a severe deficiency of insulin. → Patients require insulin to live. - complete insulin deficiency - The onset is typically in youth (before age 20), but can occur at any age. Not related to obesity. - around 50% are diagnosed in adulthood - Cause: autoimmune destruction of insulin-secreting pancreatic beta cells - Associated with other autoimmune disorders (>90% are HLA-DR3/4) - ~30% concordance in identical twins → polygenetic cause - Must treat with insulin → risk of ketoacidosis and weight loss - Latent autoimmune diabetes of adults

title: Type II Diabetes
content: # Type II Diabetes ## Background - Insulin levels are usually normal to high but may diminish over many years of having diabetes. - relative insulin deficiency → may eventually require insulin therapy - Insulin resistance due to obesity plays a major role → increasing prevalence, particularly in Asians, men, and elderly (most >40y/o) - Cause: Reduced insulin secretion and increased insulin resistance at tissue-level - ≥80% of all diabetes concordance in identical twins → likely genetic association - It often goes undiagnosed for many years; Typically starts off with an impaired glucose toleran

title: Chronic liver disease
content: # Chronic liver disease ## Background - final stage of CLD is [Liver cirrhosis](Decompensated%20cirrhosis/Liver%20cirrhosis.md) - Chronic liver disease is a progressive deterioration of liver functions. Liver functions include the production of clotting factors and other proteins, detoxification of harmful products of metabolism, and excretion of bile. This is a continuous process of inflammation, destruction, and regeneration of liver parenchyma leading to fibrosis and cirrhosis. Cirrhosis is a final stage of chronic liver disease that results in disruption of liver architecture, the formatio

title: Ascites
content: # Ascites ## Background Causes of ascites most commonly: - portal hypertension secondary to - [Liver cirrhosis](Liver%20cirrhosis.md) (80%) - [Alcoholic hepatitis](../Alcoholic%20hepatitis.md) - [Acute (fulminant) liver failure](../Acute%20(fulminant)%20liver%20failure.md) - Heart failure - Constrictive pericarditis - Hypoalbuminemia - Nephrotic syndrome - Protein-losing enteropathy - Severe malnutrition - ../../../..//IM/Gastroenterology/Decompensated%20cirrhosis/Ascites.md../../../..//IM/Gastroenterology/Decompensated%20cirrhosis/Ascites.md[Malignancy-related ascites](#Malignancy-related%20a

title: Diarrhoea
content: # Diarrhoea ## Background ## History and differentials - association with food / oily foods - cramping pains - concomintant vomiting - changes in colour/smell/consistency - blood/mucous in stool - unintentional weight loss ## Examination - General exam - Hands - Arms - track marks - Chest - gynaecomastia - spider naevi - Neck - raised JVP - Face - scleral icterus - wilson's disease - kaiser fleischer rings - oral ulcers - abdomen - obvious distension - palpate for liver and splenic margins, liver span - ?AAA - oedema - bowel sounds ## Investigations - Bloods - FBC - EUC CMP - LFT - Amylase, li

title: Acalculous Cholecystitis
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Acalculous Cholecystitis.html
content: ## Background - [Acute Cholecystitis](Acute%20Cholecystitis.md) but without obstructing stones - Seen in 10% of acute cholecystitis - Concerns - Progression to necrotising cholecystitis → lifethreatening and critically ill - Risk factors - Critically ill, severe underlying illness - Dehydration, ischemia, burns, severe trauma, post-operative - older age, prolonged TPN, DM, immunosuppression ## Clinical features - Same as [Acute Cholecystitis](Acute%20Cholecystitis.md) ## Diagnosis - Difficult due to concomitant illness - Laboratory studies similar to calculus cholecystitis - Imaging: - Abdomin

title: Biliary Stricture
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Biliary Stricture.html
content: ### Background - Most common cause is iatrogenic injury (e.g., prior biliary surgery such as cholecystectomy, liver transplantation). Other causes include recurring choledocholithiasis, chronic pancreatitis, and [Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC)](Primary%20Sclerosing%20Cholangitis%20(PSC).md) ### Clinical features - Clinical features are those of obstructive jaundice. ### Complications - Complications can be life threatening: secondary biliary cirrhosis, liver abscess, and ascending cholangitis. ### Treatment - Treatment involves endoscopic stenting (preferred) or surgical bypass if obstru

title: Cholecystoenteric fistula
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Cholecystoenteric fistula.html
content: ### Background - Abnormal communication between gallbladder lumen and lumen of adjacent bowel (usually stomach or duodenum) - Gallbladder perforation or **pressure necrosis** from large gallstone (possibly even neoplastic infiltration) - Patients are usually older ### Pathophysiology - Large gallstones → chronic gallbladder inflammation → cholecystoenteric fistula formation between gallbladder and duodenum → stone impaction at the ileocaecal junction (or at a Meckel's diverticulum) → Small bowel obstruction ### Clinical features - Potentially asymptomatic - Bile acid diarrhoea (due to excess b

title: Mirizzi Syndrome
href: IM/Gastroenterology/Gallbladder and Biliary Tract disorders/Mirizzi Syndrome.html
content: # Mirizzi Syndrome ## Background - Extrinsic compression of CBD (or any extrahepatic bile duct) by gallstone(s) in the cystic duct or gallbladder infundibulum - i.e. cholelithiasis mass effect on CBD - results in biliary stasis in CBD ## Clinical Features - Similar to [Choledocholithiasis and Cholangitis](Choledocholithiasis%20and%20Cholangitis.md) ## Investigations - Imaging (preferrably ERCP/MRCP) - Narrowing of common hepatic duct - Stone within cystic duct - Dilatation of intrahepatic biliary tree ## Management - ERCP guided CBD stent placement for biliary drainage - Open cholecystectomy p

title: Cough
content: ## Approach to cough ## Background We approach cough based on time of cough duration: 1. Acute cough (< 3 weeks) - Aetiologies include: Viral infection, URTI, Bronchitis, Allergic rhinosinusitis, Pneumonia, Medication adverse effect, [Acute Pulmonary oedema](../Respiratory/Acute%20Pulmonary%20oedema.md) - Approach - History, Examination, Vital signs - Chest XR → **Not indicated in acute cough unless altered vital signs present** - Medications review (e.g. [Angiotensin Converting Enzyme inhibitor (ACEi)](../../BCS/Pharmacology/Targets/Angiotensin%20Converting%20Enzyme/Angiotensin%20Converting%2
