title: Insomnia
content: # Insomnia ## Background - Complex health problem manifesting as - poor sleep quality - frustration with sleep quantity - difficulty initiating sleep - inability to return to sleep after awakening - Epidemiology - Prevlance increases with age - F > M affected - Causes - Medical disorders - cardiac, neurodegenerative, psychiastric - Medications - caffeine, alcohol, glucocorticoids, diuretics, antidepressants ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria - Chronic insomnia are symptoms which: 1. Cause substantial functiona distress or impairment 2. Occur > 3 nights/week for > 3 months

title: Low back pain
content: # Low back pain ## Background ## Pathology - 90% with nonspecific LBP have no determinable cause - Commonly caused by - spinal stenosis - disk herniation - compression fractures - less commonly caused by - cancer (vertebral metastases) - infection (diskitis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess) - visceral disease (nephrolithiasis, pyelonephritis, abdominal aortic aneurysms) ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria - Acute (<4 weeks) vs subacute (4-12 weeks) vs chronic (>12 weeks) ## Investigations - History - Fever and neurological signs - Physical examination - Thorough neurologic examination

title: Lower limb oedema
content: # Lower limb oedema ## Background - Common symptom in inpatient and outpatients ## Pathology - accumulation of interstitial fluid in dependent parts of body (sacrum, lower limbs) - Common causes of bilateral LL oedema - [Chronic Venous Insufficiency](../../Surgery/Vascular%20surgery/Chronic%20Venous%20Insufficiency.md) - Heart failure - including R) sided heart failure secondary to pulmonary disease - Cirrhosis - Nephrotic syndrome - Hypoalbuminaemia - Medication use - Common causes of unilateral LL oedema - mechanical obstruction to venous/lymphatic flow - Deep Vein Thrombosis - Cancer (see [

title: Medically unexplained symptoms
content: # Medically unexplained symptoms ## Background - Symptoms that cannot be attributed to specific medical cause - Epidemiology - Prevalence: 40-49% - F > M - more common in lower educational attainment and lower SES - Associated with significantly increased healthcare utilisation - > 250 billion USD annually ## Pathology - Likely related to central sensitisation - CNS hyperexcitability and reduced inhibityory responses → - pain (often without nociceptive stimuli) - sensory disturbances (E.g. allodynia, hyperalgesia) - altered autonomic, motor, cognitive function - precipitated by protromal event

title: Neck pain
content: # Neck pain ## Background - Prevalence 30% - 4th leading cause of disability in US - Most common causes - MSK - cervical strain, spondylosis, cervical facet osteoarthritis - whiplash injuries following rapid acceleration/deceleration - myofascial pain - localised tenderness or 'trigger points' on palpation - neurologic causes - radiculopathy - spinal nerve root compression resulting from degenerative spinal changes or disk herniation - neck pain + radiating arm pain, paresthesia, decreased deep tendon reflexes, diminished strength in affected extremity - pain occasionally limited to shoulder g

title: Refeeding Syndrome
content: # Refeeding Syndrome ## Background - Fluid and electrolyte occuring in malnourished patients who refeed (i.e. intake of food after not eating for a prior period) - **Hypophosphatemia** is hallmark - May affect sodium and fluid balance - Incidence - Severe hypophosphatemia: 0.5% - When using TPN with no phosphorus → 100% - When using TPN with phosphate → ~ 20% - High risk patients - Decreased intake - eating disorders (especially anorexia nervosa) - alcoholism - depression - fasting - vomiting - dysphagia - chemotherapy - prolonged NBM - post-operative - chronic malnutrition - Decreased absorpt

title: Syncope
content: # Syncope ## Background - Complete, transient loss of conscionsness and postural tone - Epidemiology - cumulative incidence - 3-6% over 10 year period - Prevalence - 40% of all adults have experienced once - 80% of these have had first episode before 30 years - 1-2% of all A+E visits ## Pathology - Global cerebral hypoperfusion due to either - decrease in cardiac output - decrease in systemic vascular resistance ## Classification - Neurally mediated (reflex) - most common - seen primarily in younger adults - Neurocardiogenic / vasodepressor reflex → vasldilation, bradycardia → systemic hypoten

title: Ulcer and wounds
content: # Ulcer and wounds ## Background - chronic wounds affect over 200,000 Australians at any time - Venous and arterial ulcers, DFIs and skin tears are most common - most wounds will heal in < 12 weeks ## Pathology Major wound type - [Venous ulcer](Venous%20ulcer.md) - [Arterial ulcer](Arterial%20ulcer.md) - [Mixed venous arterial ulcer](Mixed%20venous%20arterial%20ulcer.md) - [Lymphoedema ulcer](Lymphoedema%20ulcer.md) - [Diabetic (neuroischemic) foot ulcers](Diabetic%20(neuroischemic)%20foot%20ulcers.md) - [Skin tear](Skin%20tear.md) - (other) traumatic wounds - [Zoster rash](Zoster%20rash.md) (

title: Erythropoietin
content: # Erythropoetin ## Background Erythropoietin is a haematopoietic growth factor that stimulates the production of erythrocytes. Erythropoietin is secreted by the kidney in response to cellular hypoxia. The main uses of erythropoietin are to treat the anaemia associated with chronic kidney disease and that associated with cytotoxic therapy. ## Side-effects of erythropoietin - accelerated hypertension potentially leading to encephalopathy and seizures (blood pressure increases in 25% of patients) - bone aches - flu-like symptoms - skin rashes, urticaria - pure red cell aplasia* (due to antibodies

title: Essential Thrombocytosis
content: # Essential Thrombocytosis *Other names: ET, Essential Thrombocythaemia, Idiopathic thrombocytosis, Primary thrombocytosis, Haemorrhagic thrombocythaemia* ## Background Overproduction of platelets without a definable cause. ### Epidemiology - Incidence of 1-2/100, 000 - Female predominance ## Pathology Clonal haematopoietic stem cell disorder associated with JAK2 (V617F), MPL, CALR mutations - responsible for 90% of ET - Non-mutation ET might be hereditary - JAK2 V617F: 55% - CALR (type 1 and 2): 36% - MPL: 4% Elevation of platelet count → removal of HMW vWF multimers by platelet destruction →

title: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL)
content: # Chronic lymphocytic leukemia - Epidemiology - heterogenous - most common leukemia in western world - median age 65 (older) - BM, LN, spleen involvement at diagnosis - B-cell malignancy - CD19, CD20, CD5, CD23 - complications - AIHA - hypogammaglobulinaemia - secondary malignancy risk (defective immune surveillance) - Prognostic factors -IPI - TP53 (17p) del/mutation → strong determinant; worse in mutations - IGHV (unmutated) - B2 microglobulin > 3.5 - clinical stage - Binet B/C - Age > 65 - Who and when to treat - IPI score - low score → do not treat → 93% 5yr survival - intermediate (2-3) -

title: Lymphoma
content: ## Background - lymphoid cell malignancies in predominantly lymphoid tissue (nodes, spleen, marrow) - classification based on - morphology - immunophenotype - cytogenetics - molecular testing - clinical factors - Non-Hodgkin lymphoma - B-cell - precursor vs. mature - T and NK cells - precursor vs. mature - Hodgkin lymphoma - Classical - nodular sclerosis - mixed cellularity - lymphocyte rich - lymphocyte depleted - Nodular lymphocyte predominant Hodgkin lymphoma ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231025152659.png) - B-cell - precursor B-cell in BM - goes into circulation, encounters antigen

title: Multiple Myeloma (MM)
content: # Multiple myeloma ## Background - generalised osteopenia is commonly seen in MM - not lytic bone lesions - don't do CT with IV contrast even wth normal renal function as can precipitation renal failure - IgA paraprotiens have higher serum viscosity than IgG paraproteins - fever is worrysome features in MM ## Epidemiology - mAge 69, more common in AA/pacific isalnders - 1.4% of all cancers, males slightly more predominant than females - Always preceded by [Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS)](Monoclonal%20Gammopathy%20of%20Undetermined%20Significance%20(MGUS).md) - Risks

title: Renal cancer
content: # Renal cancer ## Background - Subtypes - [Renal cell carcinoma](Renal%20cell%20carcinoma.md) - Diagnosis tends to occur incidentally as a mass on imaging - Risk factors - Smoking - Male, increased age - Obesity, family history - Exposure to heavy metals and chemicals ## Clinical features - Classic triad: Flank pain, abdominal mass, hematuria - typically seen in 10-15% of patients - Sudden varicocoele (rare) - tumour invades IVC or L) renal vein -> blockade of L) testicular vein -> varicocoele ## Management - Radical or partial nephrectomy - removal of kidney, perinephric fat, Gerota's fascia,

title: Renal cell carcinoma
content: # Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) ## Background - Most common primary tumour of kidney - Arises from renal tubule cells - 1/3 of patients have metastatic disease at time of presentation - Histological subtypes - Clear cell (70%) - Papillary (15%) - Chromophobe (5%) - Metastatic locations - Commonly metastasizes to the lung - Metastatic tumours in thyroid are commonly RCC - Aetiology - MOST COMMONLY sporadic - FAMILIAL syndromes include - von Hippel-Lindau (AD, Chr3p) - Tuberous sclerosis (AD, Chr9) - Birt-Hogg-Dube (AD, Chr17) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220923001730.png)

title: Staphylococcus aureus
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by disease/Bacterial infections/Staphylococcus aureus.html
content: # Staphylococcus aureus --- # Staphlococcus aureus bacteraemia ## Background - significant morbidity and mortality, requires ID consult - ALWAYS assume gram positive cocci in clusters is A. aureus - complicated by metastatic infections - consideration must be given regarding treatment of these complications (E.g. cefazolin has poor CNS penetration) - endocarditis - septic arthritis - lung abscess - osteomyelitis - staphlylococcal pneumonia - cardiac implantable electronic device infections - can also result in staphylococcal [Toxic Shock Syndrome](../../ID%20by%20system/General%20skin%20and%20

title: Infectious arthritis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Bone and Joint/Infectious arthritis.html
content: # Infectious arthritis ## Background - infection of one or more joints caused by bacteria, viruses, fungi and parasites - Acute bacterial arthritis - native joint incidence: 2-10/100,000 per year (increased in [Rheumatoid arthritis](../../../Rheumatology/Rheumatoid%20arthritis.md) positive patients) - via: - hematogenous dissemination - direct joint inoculation - contiguous focus - Viral arthritis - sporadic infection or community outbreaks (small - e.g. parvovirus B19, explosive - e.g. chikungunya virus) - typically acute, polyarticular with other systemic viral symptoms - can result in prolo

title: Internal fixation associated infection
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Bone and Joint/Internal fixation associated infection.html
content: # Internal fixation associated infection ## Background ## Pathology - Microbiology - similar to [Periprosthetic joint infection (PJI)](Periprosthetic%20joint%20infection%20(PJI).md) - Staphylococci most important organisms - Open fracture → consider selection of beta-lactam resistant gram-negative bacilli such as *Enterbacter* spp. and nonfermenters - Pathogenesis - Exogenous route > hematogenous origin - Contiguous/adjacent origin is also likely ## Clinical features - Pain, inflammation - wound dehiscence - loosening of fixation devices - Acute early postoperative infection - Wound healing di

title: Osteomyelitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Bone and Joint/Osteomyelitis.html
content: # Osteomyelitis ## Background - Contaminated fracture → osteomyelitis in fractured bone from 3-25% of cases - Staphlococci and aerobic gram-negative bacilli are most common microorganisms - [[Diabetic foot ulcer](Diabetic%20foot%20ulcer.md) associated with osteomyelitis - typically contiguous to lower extremity ulcer - exposed bone/probe-to-bone test Sn 60%, Sp 91% - peripheral artery disease, diabetes → PMNs dysfunctional → cannot fight bacteria - in peripheral arterial disease, antibiotics cannot penetrate effectively due to reduced BF ## Pathology - Develops from - continuous spread from ad

title: Periprosthetic joint infection (PJI)
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Bone and Joint/Periprosthetic joint infection (PJI).html
content: # Periprosthetic joint infection (PJI) ## Background ## Pathology - Most common microorganisms - S. Aureus - Coagulase negative staphylococci - Streptococci - Periprosthetic shoulder infection - *propionibacterium acnes* - Microorganisms establish biofilm on surface of implant - Foreign devices covered by host proteins (e.g. fibronectin) → bacterial adherence - lower organism count threshold needed (10,000-fold lower) for implant associated infections - impaired granulocyte function ## Clinical features - Wound dehiscence, draining sinus, joint effusion, loosening of prosthesis - Acute exogeno

title: Infective Endocarditis
content: # Infective Endocarditis ## Background - Infective endocarditis is defined as an infection of the endocardial surface of the heart (usually involves the cusps of the valves) - Pathogenesis - Fibrin clot formation is a key step in the pathogenesis of subacute infective endocarditis. - First, endothelial damage occurs (in native valves this is typically due to turbulent blood flow), followed by adherence of platelets and fibrin to the damaged valve areas. - These initially sterile platelet-fibrin aggregates can become infected by bacteria circulating in the blood, forming vegetations. - recurren

title: Abscess
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Abscess.html
content: # Abscess ## Background - See [Furuncle](Furuncle.md) and [Carbuncle](Carbuncle.md) ## Clinical features - Erythematous, nodular, localised collections of pus within dermis and subcutaneous fat ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220726152625.png) ## Management - Incision and drainage - Obtain gram stain and culture - Mild infection without systemic symptoms - nil antibiotics necessary - Moderate infection with systemic signs - Empiric oral therapy against MRSA - Immunocompromised patient, hypotension, or systemic inflammatory response sindrome - Empirical treatment with parenteral ther

title: Carbuncle
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Carbuncle.html
content: # Carbuncle ## Background - Furuncle (boils) which extend subcutaneously to involve several furuncles ## Clinical features - Colescence of abscesses can result in systemic infective signs ## Management - As per [Abscess](Abscess.md)

title: Cellulitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Cellulitis.html
content: # Cellulitis ## Background and definitions - Infection involving deeper demis and subcutanoeus fat tissue - i.e. deeper than [Erysipelas](Erysipelas.md) ## Clinical features - less demarcated than erysipelas ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220726152612.png) ## Diagnosis - Blood culture - positive in 5% of patients - not routinely performed, however should be done in: (along with skin cultures) - immunocompromised - chronic lymphoedema - severe sepsis or possible necrotising component - unusual precipitating circumstances (e.g. immersion injury, animal bites) - Radiographic imaging M

title: Erysipelas
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Erysipelas.html
content: # Erysipelas ## Background and definitions - Infection of epidermis, upper dermis, and superficial lymphatics - Sites - Usually face or lower extremity ## Clinical features - Brightly erythematous - Tender - Distinct elevated borders - Associated fever, lymphangitis, regional lymphadenopathy ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220726152053.png)

title: Folliculitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Folliculitis.html
content: # Folliculitis ## Background - superficial inflammation or infection of a hair follicle - Risk factors - Occlusive dressings or clothing - Increased sweating - Obesity - Long term topical glucocorticoid use - Infectivity - noninfectious (HIV-associated eosinophilic folliculitis) - infectious - most frequently straphylococcus aureus - other common causes include gram negatives (Klebsiella, Enterobacter) from prolonged acne antibiotics, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (hot tub use) - Yeast infection - Malassezia - Viral folliculitis (e.g. Herpes simplex, Varicella zoster) - Demodex folliculitis (mite

title: Necrotising fasciitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Necrotising fasciitis.html
content: # Necrotising fasciitis ## Background - Necrotising soft tissue infection involving subdermal compartments (E.g. fascia, muscle) - uncommon but life threatening - Pathophysiology - infection spreads along superficial fascia - Aetiology - Monomicrobial - Classically S.pyogenes - Polymicrobial - mixed aerobic/anaerobic bacteria and associated toxin production - Risk factors - Previous skin trauma or infection - Diabetes - high risk perineum infection - [Fournier gangrene](Fournier%20gangrene.md) - IVDU - Malignancy - Immunosuppression - Liver disease ## Clinical features 1. Initial presentation

title: Skin and soft tissue infections
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Skin and soft tissue infections.html
content: # Skin and soft tissue infections ## Background 1. Risk factors 1. Breach of epidermis associated with trauma (e.g. IVDU, surgery, bites, lacerations, abrasions) 2. Non-traumatic (ulcers, tinea pedis) 3. Impaired lymphatic/venous drainage (chronic venous insufficiency, lymphoedema) 4. Immunocompromise (diabetes, neutropenia, cirrhosis, HIV, transplantation) 5. Peripheral Arterial Disease 6. Cellulitis history 1. Characterisation of infections 1. Anatomic involvement 2. Purulence 1. Nonpurulent - typically caused by beta-hemolytic streptococci (e.g. Streptococcus pyogenes) - [Erysipelas](Erysip

title: Toxic Shock Syndrome
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/General skin and soft tissue/Toxic Shock Syndrome.html
content: # Toxic Shock Syndrome ## Background - Pathophysiology - S.aureus and S.pyogenes infection → Release exotoxins → Body produces cytokines → Results in toxic shock syndrome - Aetiology - Staphylococcus aureus TSS - associated with tampon use, nasal packing, surgical wound, skin ulcer, burns, catheters, IVDU - Streptococcus pyogenes TSS - associated with skin/soft tissue infection (particularly [Necrotising fasciitis](Necrotising%20fasciitis.md)) - Higher mortality rate with bacteremia compared to staphylococcal ## Diagnosis ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220727035220.png) ## Manageme

title: Bacterial Meningitis
content: # Bacterial meningitis ## Background - Aetiology - Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseria menigitidis, Streptococcus pneumoniae - S. pneumoniae - most common cause of community acquired bacterial menigitis - RF: Alcoholism - N. menigitidis serogroup B - 40% of infections in USA - S. aureus - RF: IVDU, hemodialysis - Secondary to endocarditis - Bacterial endocarditis caused by S. pneumoniae and S. aureus → purulent menigitis - Look for hx of valvular disease, new regurgitant murmur, empbolic phenomena, endocarditis stigmata ## Clinical features - Acute meningeal signs (Fever, nuchal rigidity) - Alte

title: Encephalitis
content: # Encephalitis ## Background - Brain parenchymial inflammation ## Clinical definition - One major (altered consciousnesss > 24h) - 3 minor - fever, - new-onset seizure, - on-onset focal neurological findings, - CSF pleocytosis, - abnormal MRI, - electroencephalographic findings ## Pathology - vasuative agent unknown in 37-70% of infections - Most commonly caused by - HSV1 - HSV6 - VZV - WNV - autoimmune diseases ## Viral Encephalitis - [Herpes Simplex Encephalitis](Herpes%20Simplex%20Encephalitis.md) - [Varicella Zoster Virus Encephalitis](Varicella%20Zoster%20Virus%20Encephalitis.md) - [Arbov

title: Meningitis
content: # Meningitis ## Background ## Acute meningitis Aetiologies of meningitis include: 1. [Viral Meningitis](Viral%20Meningitis.md) 2. [Bacterial Meningitis](Bacterial%20Meningitis.md) 3. [Fungal Meningitis](Fungal%20Meningitis.md) ## Subacute and chronic meningitis ## Background - Definitions - Subacute = 5-30 days - Chronic = >30 days - Aetiology - Mycobacterium tuberculosis and fungi most common causes - [Tuberculous meningitis](Tuberculous%20meningitis.md) - [Fungal Meningitis](Fungal%20Meningitis.md) - [Neurobrucellosis](Neurobrucellosis.md) - [Parasitic meningitis](Parasitic%20meningitis.md)

title: Spinal Epidural Abscess
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by system/Neurological/Spinal Epidural Abscess.html
content: - PAthology - haematogenous dissemination - s. aureus in >50% infections - streptococcus and gram negative (e.g. E. coli) - Predisposing factors - endocarditis - IVDU - long-term IV catheter (hemodialysis, central lines) - UTI - post-neurosurgical intervention (e.g. psinal fusion, epidural catheter placement) or paraspinal injection - Symptoms - localised pain at site of infection - pain radiates down spine - Investigations - MRI - location/extent of abscess - baseline bloods - ESR, CRP - blood cultures PRIOR to antibiotics - Management - IV ABx for 6 weeks - surgical drainage indicated for ne

title: Acute Kidney Injury
content: # Acute kidney injury (AKI) ## Background ## Definition - A rapid decline in renal function over hours or days, with an increase in serum creatinine level - while the cutoff for rise in creatinine which qualifies as an “injury” is controversial, an increase of as little as 0.3 mg/dL may have clinical relevance, while traditional definitions describe a relative increase of 50% or an absolute increase of 0.5 to 1.0 mg/dL). - The creatinine may be normal despite a markedly reduced glomerular filtration rate (GFR) in the early stages of acute kidney injury (AKI) due to the time it takes for creati

title: Chronic Kidney Disease and End Stage Kidney Disease
href: IM/Nephrology/Chronic Kidney Disease and End Stage Kidney Disease.html
content: ## Chronic Kidney Disease ### A. General Characteristics 1. Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is defined as either decreased kidney function (GFR <60 mL/min) or kidney damage (structural or functional abnormalities) for at least 3 months, regardless of cause. 2. Causes a. Diabetes is the most common cause (30% of cases). b. HTN is responsible for 25% of cases. c. Chronic GN accounts for 15% of cases. d. Interstitial nephritis, polycystic kidney disease, obstructive uropathy (BPH) e. Any of the causes of AKI may lead to CKD if prolonged and/or if treatment is delayed. - Pyelonephritis 3. Pathophysio

title: Dialysis in Renal Failure
content: # Dialysis ## Background There are three main forms of dialysis: 1. [Haemodialysis (HD)](Haemodialysis%20(HD).md) 2. [Haemofiltration](Haemofiltration.md) 3. [Peritoneal dialysis (PD)](Peritoneal%20dialysis%20(PD).md) ### How to choose dialysis 1. Independent patients → allow maintained independence → peritoneal dialysis - If potential issues with peritoneum or bowel, or high risk infection → haemodialysis 2. If not independent → haemodialysis ### Indications for dialysis - Acidosis - severe metabolic acidosis - Electrolyte abnormalities - severe hyperkalemia or rapidly rising K+ level - Intox

title: Hypernatremia
content: # Hypernatremia ## Clinical manifestations - Neurological signs - Fatigue, Headache, Nausea/vomiting - Develops at Na 125-130mEq/L - Confusion, Seizures, Coma - at Na+ 120-130mEq/L - Symptoms/severity related to derangement severity and speed - Occurs due to reduced extracellular osmolality (low Na+) resulting in osmotic gradients causing intracellular fluid increase -> cerebral oedema - Note this is the same as in [Hyponatremia](Hyponatremia.md) ## Aetiology 1. Unreplaced water loss 1. Excessive GI/insensible losses - diarrhoea, sweating, etc. 2. Diabetes insipidus - inadequate ADH function 1

title: Hypokalemia
content: # Hypokalemia ## Clinical Manifestations - Typically no signs/symptoms until K+ < 3.0mEq/L - Muscular signs/symptoms - Weakness (legs > arms), cramps, ileus, rhabdomyolysis - Cardiac conduction - EKG changes (ST depression, TWI, Prominent U waves) - Arrhythmias (AF, VT, TdP) - at higher risk with QT prolonging drugs, Digoxin toxicity, Hypomagnesemia, Coronary ischemia ## Aetiology 1. Poor intake 1. Starvation 2. Anorexia 2. GIT losses 1. Vomiting/NG drainage 2. Diarrhoea 3. Laxative abuse - form of bullemia 4. Ureterosigmoidostomy - ureter into sigmoid after bladder removal -> increased Na+ de

title: Hyponatremia
content: # Hyponatremia ## Clinical manifestations - Neurological signs - Fatigue, Headache, Nausea/vomiting - Develops at Na 125-130mEq/L - Confusion, Seizures, Coma - at Na+ 120-130mEq/L - Symptoms/severity related to derangement severity and speed - Occurs due to reduced extracellular osmolality (low Na+) resulting in osmotic gradients causing intracellular fluid increase -> cerebral oedema ## Aetiology ![](../../../figures/Hyponatremia%203-37%20screenshot.png) ### By mechanism (Hypotonic hyponatremia, hypoosmolar) 1. Elevated ADH 1. Low Blood pressure (low effective circulating volume) - Volume dep

title: Haemodialysis (HD)
content: # Haemodialysis (HD) ## Background Arteriovenous fistulas are direct connections between arteries and veins. They may occur pathologically but are generally formed surgically to allow access for haemodialysis. They are now regarded as the preferred method of access for haemodialysis due to the lower rates of complications. The time taken for an arteriovenous fistula to develop is 6 to 8 weeks - maturation is needed to allow dialysis flow rate of 500mL/min - Blood from patient put through machine and runs through filter - dialysate flows in opposite direction to blood flow - semipermeable membr

title: Intradialytic hypotension
content: # Intradialytic hypotension ## Background - Hypotension during [Haemodialysis (HD)](Haemodialysis%20(HD).md) - Definition - presence of decrease SBP >= 20mmHg or MAP by 10mmHg - associated with clinical events and need for nursing interventions - Epidemiology - Affects 5-30% of dialysis treatments (depending on definition) - Risk factors - older age, longer dialysis vintage, diabetes, lower predialysis BP, lower albumin, female, higher BMI ## Pathology ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020221003141111.png) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020221003141127.png) ## Clinical features - Occasional

title: Kidney Transplantation in Renal Failure
content: # Kidney Transplantation in Renal Failure ## Background - Treatment of choice in end-stage renal failure ### Pre-transplant assessment 1. Virology status 1. CMV, Hep B, Hep C, HIV, EBV, VZV 2. Can cause issues if immunocompromised 2. TB status - isoniazid and pyridoxine prophylaxis given to high-risk and proven TB 3. ABO blood group, HLA typing for matching required 4. Pre-op K+ monitoring Pre-op anti-IL2R antibodies may also be given (e.g. basiliximab) to reduce rates of early rejection ## Contraindications - Active infection - Cancer - severe heart disease or comorbidity ### Post-transplanta

title: Nephrolithiasis
content: # Nephrolithiasis (Kidney stones) ## Background - Risk factors - FHx, PMHx - high protein diet - *breakdown of protein lowers urinary pH and increases uric acid excretion - increases risk of forming uric acid stones* - Male - low fluid intake, dehydration - recurrent UTIs - Diabetes, gout - RTA - electrolyte abnormalities - medications (e.g. acetazolamide, furosemide, allupurinol) - Subtypes - Oxalate - *the most common renal stone - leading cause is hypercalciuria* - Uric acid - Calcium phosphate - Struvite - caused by UTIs secondary to urease positive organisms (e.g. Proteus), can form stagh

title: Nephrotoxins
content: ## Nephrotoxins ### Definition - toxic agents which can cause [Acute Kidney Injury](Acute%20Kidney%20Injury.md), by direct tubular necrosis or interstitial nephritis ### Exogenous nephrotoxins 1. Analgesics (NSAIDs) 2. Antimicrobials (gentamicin, sulphonamides, tetracycline, vancomycin, amphoteracin, aciclovir) 3. radiocontrasts - can induce radiocontrast nephropathy - RF: DM, high dose contrast, hypovolaemia, nephrotoxic agents, pre-existing renal impariment - Prevention: - Stop nephrotoxic agents peri-procedure - pre-hydriate with IV 0.9% NaCl - Consider Acetylcysteine or IV NaHCO3 - Inform

title: Peritoneal dialysis (PD)
content: # Peritoneal dialysis (PD) ## Anatomy of the peritoneum - Peritoneum (containing PD fluid) - mesothelium - Interstitium - Endothelium - Blood ## Background and principles - dialysis is movement of substances across a semi-permeable membrane down a concentration gradient - peritoneal membrane is used as the semi-permeable membrane - dialysis fluids placed into the peritoneal cavity, leave it for a few hours, then drain hours later - Mechanisms of molecular transport in the peritoneal membrane 1. Diffusion - solutes move from high to low concentration 2. Convection/solute drag/ultrafiltration -

content: # Focal seizures with impaired awareness ## Background - A transient impairment to maintain normal contact with the environment - Unable to respond appropriately to verbal or visual commands, impaired recollection or awareness of the ictal phase ## Clinical features 1. title: Non-epileptic Seizures (Pseudoseizure)
content: # Non-epileptic Seizures (Pseudoseizure or Psychogenic seizures) ## Background - Non-epileptic behaviours resembling seizures - Caused by psychological distress → conversion reaction ## Clinical features 1. Non-epileptic behaviours - side-to-side turning of head - symmetric and large-amplitude shaking movements of the limbs - twitching of all four extremities without loss of consciousness - pelvic thrusting 2. Period of onset - typically last longer than epileptic seizures - wax and wane over minutes to hours ## Diagnosis and investigations 1. EEG - post-ictal EEG abnormalities seen in general

title: Seizures and epilepsy syndromes
href: IM/Neurology/Epilepsy and seizures/Seizures and epilepsy syndromes.html
content: # Seizures and Epilepsy Syndromes ## Background - Seizure - *transient occurence of signs or symptoms due to abnormal excessive or synchronous neuronal activity in the brain* - ~ 5-10% of the population will have at least ONCE seizure, most commonly in early childhood or late adulthood - Epilepsy - *condition in which a person has a risk of recurrent seizures due to a chronic, underlying process* - Incidence: ~ 0.3-0.5% of the population - Prevalence: 5-30/1000 people ## Seizure classification International League Against Epilepsy (2017) classified seizures 1. Focal onset - onset limited to on

content: # Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis ## Aetiologies - [Cavernous sinus thrombosis](Cavernous%20sinus%20thrombosis.md) - Hypercoagulable states - Thrombophilia - Pregnancy (And puerperium) - Oral contraceptive use - Dehydration ## Management - Anticoagulation - [Antithrombin III inhibitor](../../../BCS/Pharmacology/Targets/Antithrombin%20III/Antithrombin%20III%20inhibitor.md) has lower hospital mortality than [[../../../BCS/Pharmacology/Targets/Antithrombin III/Antithrombin III inhibitor](../../../BCS/Pharmacology/Targets/Antithrombin%20III/Antithrombin%20III%20inhibitor.md) - Continue anticoagul

title: Headache
content: # Headache ## Background Headache classification system (International Headache Society) characterises headache into: 1. Primary headaches - the disorder itself 1. Tension headache 2. Migraines 3. Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (e.g. cluster headaches) 2. Secondary headaches - caused by an exogenous disorder - common and rarely worrisome - trauma - whiplash, epidural/subdural - vascular - ischemic stroke, subarachnoid, intracerebral haemorrhage, aneurysm, arteritis - nonvascular - abnormal intracranial pressure, tumour, seizure - substance use - infection - meningitis - metabolic disorders

title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension
content: # Pseudotumor Cerebri (Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension, IIH) ## Background - Associated with Obesity, Female gender (90%), Pregnancy - Causes - Obstructions in cerebral venous sinuses - Persistently raised ICP - Requires the absence of any vascular or space occupying lesions - Associated with - Hypervitaminosis A - Use of [Tetracycline antibiotics](../../../BCS/Pharmacology/Classes/Antibiotics.md#Tetracycline%20antibiotics) - Retinoic acid use - Kidney failure - Endocrine disorders ([Hypoparathyroidism](../../Endocrinology/Hypoparathyroidism.md), [Addison disease](../../Endocrinology/Addi

title: Low CSF volume headache
href: IM/Neurology/Other neurological issues/Low CSF volume headache.html
content: # Low CSF volume headache ## Aetiology 1. Most common - post-LP headache - Begins within 48h, can be delayed up to 12 days - Incidence 10-30% 2. Other causes - Epidural injection - Vigorous Valsalva maneouvre (e.g. heavy lifting, straining, coughing, clearing eustacian tubes in airplane, multiple orgasms) - spotaneous CSF leaks ## Clinical features - Headache occuring from one day to the next - Generally not present on awakening - worsens throughout day - improves on supine/recumbency - worsens when sitting upright/standing ## Diagnosis 1. CSF opening pressure - Can be low (0-50mm) however hea

title: Reversible Cerebral Vascoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)
href: IM/Neurology/Other neurological issues/Reversible Cerebral Vascoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS).html
content: # Reversible Cerebral Vascoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS) ## Background - Recurrent thunderclap headaches - Second most common cause of thunderclap headaches - Can be triggered by exertion, valsalva maneouvre, emotion, bathing ## Pathophysiology - Multifocal constriction of intracranial vessels ## Aetiologies 1. Vasoactive drug exposure - Sympatheticomimetics (Amphetamines, Pseudoephedrine, Phenylpropanolamine) - Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, MAOi) - Troptan or ergot alkaloids - Nicotine - Illicit drugs (cocaine, ecstasy, MDMA) - Cannabis 2. Pregnancy and puerperium - Preeclampsia, eclampsia, HEL

title: Cerebrovascular disease
content: # Cerebrovascular disease --- # Stroke ## Background - Epidemiology - Leading cause of serious disability amongst adults - Definition - WHO: Sudden onset focal neurological deficits associated with dysfunction in the brain, retina or spinal cord due to occlusion or rupture of a cerebral or spinal artery - Types - [Ischemic Stroke](Ischemic%20Stroke.md) - occlusion of artery, most common - [Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)](Transient%20Ischemic%20Attack%20(TIA).md) - [Haemorrhagic stroke](Haemorrhagic%20stroke.md) - less common, higher immediate mortality - [Intracerebral Haemorhage (ICH)](Intra

title: Haemorrhagic stroke
content: # Haemorrhagic stroke ## Background - Classified into - [Intracerebral Haemorhage (ICH)](Intracerebral%20Haemorhage%20(ICH).md) - [Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)](Subarachnoid%20haemorrhage%20(SAH).md)

title: Intracerebral Haemorhage (ICH)
content: # Intracerebral Haemorhage (ICH) ## Background ## Pathophysiology ### Hypertensive ICH - Most common cause affecting deep structures of the brain (e.g. basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum) ### Lobar ICH - Can be caused by hypertension - In >55 year old without hypertension, may be due to cerebral amyloid angiopathy - amyloid protein (similar to in A.D.) → deposition in cerebral arterioles near cortical surface → weakened arterial wall → more prone to rupture ### Morality - Most common: Hematoma expands → Increased ICP → Death ## Clinical features - Presents similarly to ischemic stroke -

title: Intracranial Haemorrhage
content: # Intracranial Haemorrhage ## Background - A form of stroke - Classified based on location of haemorrhage into - [[Intracerebral Haemorhage (ICH)](Intracerebral%20Haemorhage%20(ICH).md) - haemorrhage into brain parenchyma - [[Subdural Hematoma](Subdural%20Hematoma.md) - [[Epidural Hematoma](Epidural%20Hematoma.md) - [Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)](Subarachnoid%20haemorrhage%20(SAH).md) ## Clinical features - Likely to present with headaches (compared to [[Vascular Neurological Disorders/Ischemic Stroke](Ischemic%20Stroke.md)) ## Diagnosis and investigation 1. CT Non-contrast brain ## Manageme

title: Ischemic Stroke
content: # Ischemic stroke ## Background ## Aetiology and Pathophysiology 1. Vessels 1. Large vessel atherosclerosis - most common - Vessels become sclerotid - Extracranial: Carotid artery, vertebral artery - Intracranial: Carotid, middle cerebral, vertebral-basilar junction, midbasilar arteries - Plaque rupture → artery-to-artery embolism - Vascular dissection 1. Small vessel disease 2. Blood - hypercoagulable states 3. Cardiac 1. Arrhythmias - AF (Cardioembolic) - Atrial fibrillation (most common) or ventricular thrombus (e.g. post-MI or reduced EF) or PFO or severe valvular disease (E.g. rheumatic d

title: Stroke syndromes
content: # Stroke syndromes ## Anterior circulation strokes 1. Middle cerebral artery stroke - General - Contralateral hemiplegia - Hemisensory loss - Homonymous hemianopia (symmetric bilateral loss of vision in half of visual fields), eye deviation to side of lesion - Can result in considerable cerebral swelling in first 72h - Rostral brainstem functional disturbance -> comatose - Superior division in dominant hemisphere - Broca's aphasia (expressive) - Contralateral paralysis and loss of sensation in arm, face, +/- leg - Inferior division occlusion in dominant hemisphere - Receptive (Wernicke) aphasi

title: Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH)
content: # Subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) ## Background - SAH is the most common cause of thunderclap headache, seen in 25% of patients. - Pre-test probability increased by - Age >= 40 years - Onset during exercise - Witnssesed loss of consciousness - Concomitnat neck pain ## Aetiology - Saccular (berry) aneurysm - most common cause - Intracranial arterial dissection - Mycotic aneurysm rupture - [Reversible Cerebral Vascoconstriction Syndrome (RCVS)](../Other%20neurological%20issues/Reversible%20Cerebral%20Vascoconstriction%20Syndrome%20(RCVS).md) - Dural sinus thrombosis - Vascular malformations - Cer

title: Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA)
content: # Transient Ischemic Attack (TIA) ## Background - Definition - Previous definition: Focal neurological impairment with symptoms lasting < 24h - Current definition: Stroke-like neurological impairment with nil evidence of infarct on MRI - Patients with TIA are at high risk of stroke within first 48h after symptom onset ## Clinical Features - Hemiparesis - Monocular or visual field loss - Dysarthria, aphasia - Sensory loss ## Differentials - Migraine - Paresthesia - Isolated dizziness or vertigo - Seizure - can rule out with EEG - Vertigo - Memory loss ## Investigations 1. History and examinatio

title: Breast cancer
content: # Breast cancer ## Background - In the united states, 1 in 8 women develop breast cancer - most common cancer in women; 2nd most common cause of cancer mortality - Risk factors which increase risk of breast cancer are those which increase estrogen exposure - Increased age (increased exposure time) - median onset is 61 years old - **BRCA1/2 mutation** - BRCA1: 50-70% breast, 40-50% ovarian - BRCA2: 45-70% breast, 15-20% ovarian - increased male breast cancer risk - increased pancreatic and prostate cancer risk in BRCA1/2 - BRCA1/2 only explains 5-10% of breast cancers - MOST breast cancers are

title: Colorectal cancer
content: # Colorectal cancer ## Background - Risk factors - Decrease risk - screening, exercise, Vitamin D, aspirin/NSAIDs, post-menopausal estrogen, calcium - most decreased risk is from large observational studies rather than RCTs - Increased risk - family history, IBD, diabetes, obesity, red meat, western diet, EtOH, smoking - Familial/hereditary risk factors - Familial syndromes (~5% of CRCs) - familial adenomatous polyposis - FAP gene mutation - 100s-1000s of polyps by mid-teens, need full colectomy by 20s - hereditary nonpolyposis cancer syndrome - Lynch syndrome - mutated mismatch repair gene -

title: Esophageal cancer
content: # Esophageal cancer ## Background - Risk factors - Upper oesphageal tend to be SCC - not as common in 1st world countries - SCC rates decreasing since smoking cessation on a population level - adenocarcinoma risk factors tend to be associated with acid, obesity - increased incidence of adenocarcinoma over time (with mild improvements in 5 year survival) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231025171341.png) ## Pathogenesis of Adenocarcinoma of oesophagus ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231025171601.png) - once you develop adenocarcinoma, high percentage will develop mets (85%+) ## Treatme

title: Gastric Cancer
content: # Gastric cancer ## Epidemiology - incidence has decreased over time - Risk factors - nutritional - low fat/protein consumption, salted meat/fish, high nitrate consumption - enviornmental - poor food preparation/lack of refrigeration, poor drinking water, occupational (rubber, coal), smoking (1.6x), low SES - Medical - prior gastric surgery, gastric atrophy/gastritis - H.pylori infection (2x) - essentially do not get gastric cancer with no h.pylori - ~ 5% risk of gastric cancer if H.pylori positive - Hereditary - E.cardherin mutation families ## Investigations - HER2 positivity ## Treatment -

title: HMS oncology
content: Case 6 - Tobacco associated with - head and neck - pancreas - bladder - lung - esophagus - kidney - ionisaing radiation (focussed radiation) - thyroid - hodgkin's - breast - lung - leukemia - ifectious - Hep B and C → hepatocellular cancer - no hep C vaccine - EBV → post-transplant lymphoma and nasopharyngeal cancer (specific type) - HPV → cervical and anal cancer NOT uterine Case 8 - 32M in good health for routine physical. Mother passed colon cancer age 37. 39 brother just diagnosed with colon cancer. PE NAD, FOBT NAD, labs NAD - Next step - genetic counselling with screening to take place

title: Lung Cancer
content: ## Epidemiology - Leading cause of **cancer death** around the world - almost equal to sum of next 4 causes of cancer added together - smoking is the biggest risk factor - 85% LCa occurs in smokers - increases with pack year hx - 15-30x increase risk of lung cancer compared to nonsmokers - quitting NEVER normalises risk of lung cancer - more than half are metastatic at diagnosis (55%) - 22% will have locally advanced disease (lymph node, mediastinum, supraclavicular spread) - 15% have immediately resectable - Staging is the MOST IMPORTANT predictor of lung cancer - localised - 55% 5-year surv

title: Prostate cancer
content: # Prostate cancer ## Background - most common male cancer (1 in 7), 2nd most common cause of death in US - reducing incidence recently - due to reduced PCA testing - risk factors - age - fhx - race - africam aemerican - genetic disoposition - BRCA, lynch - Spectrum of disease - low risk, organ confined - will never kill - castration resistant prostate cancer - non treatable - aim is to balance treatment with risk of death ## Screening - rationale - to early identify - tests: PSA and DRE - PLCO trial (150000 USA) - no difference, but 50% of patients had contamination ?? - european study - 21% o

title: Spinal Cord Compression
content: Spinal cord compression - Principle of management - initiate treatment early to prevent neurological deficits - once neurological deficits occur, often irreversible - usually from epidural compression from vertebral body mets - most common tumour: lung, breast, prostate, myeloma, lymphoma, renal cell - thoracic spine most common location (60%) - **note mid back is not a common location for degenerative disc disease but it is a common area for cord compression** - epidural compression - intramedullary metastases less common (WITHIN the spinal cord) - normally EPIDURAL compression - SSx - back p

title: DETECT Junior
content: # DETECT Junior 1. Detecting deterioration - How do they look from the end of the bed - ABCDEFG - Involve caregivers in assessment - Identify early and late warning signs 2. Evaluation - Check trend in observations (24-48h) - Yellow zone triggers - 3x yellow zone triggers is a red zone trigger - red zone triggers - Considering the diagnosis and 'at risk factors' - are they getting the right treatment, are they getting better 3. Treatment - Prioritise treatment using ABCDEFG algorithm - Increased frequency of observations? - Altered calling criteria? Work within scope of practice 4. Escalation

title: Adolescent health
content: # Adolescent health ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402170235.png) Developmental tasks of adolescence (affected by) - Establish a realistic body image (media M & F) - Establish a self identity that is realistic, positive & stable - Achieve appropriate independence from parents and other adults (prolongation of dependence on family) - Integrate into the peer group (isolation) - comfortable with one’s own sexuality and intimate relationships (difference & diversity) - Acquire skills for a future vocation (access to education) - Develop a morality/value system (rapid social change) Epi - Adolescen

title: Breast milk
content: # Breast milk, substitutes and introduction of solids ## Breast milk, breast milk substitutes and introduction of solids Preterm breastfed infants **require iron supplements from 4–8 weeks of age**. Breastfed infants - **improved neurodevelopment & ↓infection, diabetes, necrotizing enterocolitis and gastro-oesophageal reflux** specific immune factors such as IgA, maternal lymphocytes and macrophages, and other non-specific protective factors such as lactoferrin and lysozyme. ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402171340.png) All formulas have CMP – modified through heat to resemble human milk + lac

title: Risk taking behaviour in adolescents
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/Development/Risk taking behaviour in adolescents.html
content: # Risk taking behaviour in adolescents ## The brain and risk taking behaviours Brain imaging in adolescents - Brain imaging to look at adolescent brains (e.g. structural MRIs) - size, grey:white matter - functional MRIs - neuronal processes within brain by looking at increased regions of blood flow Brain development in adolescents - Increase in brain size and white matter, # pathways, # synapses - size peak in adolescents followed by neuronal connection pruning - adolescents experience dramatic neuronal overgrowth and pruning - Cortical regions peaks in M/F at 12/11; temporal peak 16.2/16.8, p

title: Sleep and chronic illness
content: # Sleep and chronic illness ## Sleep - Wakefulness: alpha waves (sawtooth appearance and frequency 8-13Hz). - Sleep onset: theta waves, mixed frequency - Deep sleep: delta waves (0.-2Hz) – wakes up groggy and confused - NREM sleep: **SWS activity** - REM sleep: dreaming, **muscle paralysis (OSA)**, genital changes Average 16 year old requires **8 ½ hours per night** Sleep onset delay - tendency to fall asleep later at night and wake later in the morning - On school days **mean bedtime for 16yo is 10:30pm (8.30pm for 9 year olds)** - **40% ↓ SWS from 10-20 years → Less restorative sleep** - RE

title: Sudden infant death syndrome
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/General Medicine/Sudden infant death syndrome.html
content: # Sudden Infant Death Syndrome ## Causes and prevention RF: - 1 and 4 months old, M>F, fall, winter and early spring months. - babies who sleep on **stomach or their side** rather than their back - **overheating while sleeping** - **too soft a sleeping surface**, with **fluffy blankets or toys** - 3x: mothers who **smoke during pregnancy** - 2x: exposure to **passive smoke** from smoking by mothers, fathers - mothers < 20y - babies born to mothers who had **little, late, or no prenatal care** - **premature or low birth weight babies** - **having a sibling who died of SIDS** Triple risk model 1

title: Noonan Syndrome
content: # Noonan Syndrome - a broad, webbed neck; **↑bleeding tendency; and a high incidence of congenital heart disease**, FTT, short stature, feeding difficulties, **sternal deformity, renal malformation**, pubertal delay**, cryptorchidism, developmental or behavioral problems**, vision problems, hearing loss, and lymphedema. Familial recurrence: AD but most cases (60%) are due to de novo mutations. Diagnosis: clinical features Molecular genetic testing can confirm diagnosis in 70% of cases ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220402175331.png)

title: Abdominal Pain
content: # Abdominal Pain ## Background - 20% all children present with abdominal pain - 5% require hospitalisation, intervention - 10% recurrent abdominal pain ## Approach to Abdominal Pain ### History - Age - Pain Hx - problem for non-verbalising patients - Gynaechological and pubertal history - e.g. Ovulation can present - Past history - ?Medication ?Drug use - Social history - Family history ### Examination - General appearance, posture in bed - Vital signs (Tachycardia for infection, inflammation, pain) - Abdominal examination (movement of abdominal wall - reduced movement in generalised peritonit

title: Coeliac disease
content: # Coeliac disease ## Background - Incidence 1:100 - FTT (FTT) - Diarrhoea - Abdominal pain - Vomiting - Constipation - Abdominal distension - Irritability Testing for Coeliac recommended: - Iron deficiency anaemia (Fe resistant or unexplained) - IgA deficiency (NB: screening test then difficult to interpret) - **Down, Turners, Williams, DM1 – not catch22** - **FDR** Screening and Diagnosis - **IgA antibody to TTG** - 92-100% sensitivity; 91-100% specificity - If high index of suspicion **but low TTG? IgA deficient ** IgG TTG - Endomysial antibody (EMA) ## Treatment - Only treatment: Gluten fr

title: Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia
content: # Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia: - Incidence of 1:2-5000 - **Mortality of approx. 50% - incl. in utero deaths** - **Associated anomalies in 50%** - Associated Syndromes – g. trisomy **13, 18 and 21** - 2 main types of CDH 1. **posterolateral >90**% - failure of **pleuroperitoneal membrane to completely close – R closes first  CDH more common on L** 2. **anterior medial Morgagni** – **older children, 2%** - **Prenatal Pulmonary hypoplasia**  **postnatal pulmonary hypertension** - Viscera: occupy space of **developing lung** - Antenatal diagnosis – **US at 24 weeks gestation** - Postnatal pr

title: Cyclical vomiting syndrome (Sandifiers syndrome)
content: # Cyclical vomiting syndrome - **Etiology**: unknown; a [family history](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/h-w2cClPa/3/article/En08Eg#Z0ccd21fcf57fab20ffb12c037f4d9af3) with [migraines](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/h-w2cClPa/3/article/hi0crf#Z29591a1bbf99342d264764dbfd22f0c4) is common - **[Epidemiology](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/h-w2cClPa/3/article/1j02zf#Z4c4f63797573af64476acb67d715be49)**: peak [incidence](https://next.amboss.com/us/questions/h-w2cClPa/3/article/1j02zf#Z98f70c066a3f2c11ae8d5ca8e9fe13dd) in children 3–7 years of age - **Presentation** - Recurrent attac

title: Exomphalos
content: # Exomphalos/Omphalocoele: - **failure of viscera to return to the abdominal cavity following physiological herniation in the pre-natal period  membrane covering – if too big  epithelise, brace  attempt closure 10-12y** - Aetiology: **persistence of primitive body stalk**, failure of migration of lateral body folds and body wall closure - Usually detected on **antenatal US** - Herniated viscera: **bowel and often the liver** – **covered with amniotic membrane** - **>50% have an associated anomaly**g. CNS, CVS, GU, GIT – cardiac and renal - Associated syndromes e.g. **Beckwith-Weidemann**, *

title: Gastroschisis
content: # Gastroschisis bowel herniation via a small defect in the anterior abdominal wall **right of the umbilicus** - Incidence of 1:4000 live births - Aetiological theories – young motherhood, recreational D&A use in pregnancy - **Failure of mesoderm to form in body wall**, rupture over umbilical amnion, herniation of bowel, abnormal involution of R umbilical vein, disruption of R vitelline artery, normal abdo wall folding - Usually detected on **antenatal US** – loops of bowel free floating in amniotic fluid - **Associated anomalies are uncommon**: **intestinal atresia** and **undescended testes**

title: Hirschsprung Disease
content: ## Hirschsprung Disease aka Congenital Aganglionic Megacolon absence of the myenteric and submucosal plexuses in continuity **from the anus (rectum always affected) to a variable distance proximally (ENS migrate from proximal to distal – varying transition circumference)** - Incidence 1:5000 - Aetiology unknown – trisomy 21, MEN type 2, congenital central hypoventilation, neurofibromatosis - **50-90% present in the neonatal period** – **failure to pass meconium in 1st 48h**, abdominal distension, feeding intolerance, bilious vomiting - Older PC: constipation, FTT, abdo pain/distension – in tri

title: Infantile colic
content: # Infantile colic / Infant Irritability ## Background - Common in the first few weeks - Cause poorly understood - 'colicky' abdominal pain (Wax and wane) however explanation for why it is colicky is controversial - tend to disappear by the 4th month of age, ## Clinical features 1. Attacks of screaming 2. Draws up legs 3. Unable to be comforted 4. Nil vomiting 5. Normal bowel movements 6. Infant otherwise thriving ## Diagnosis and investigations - Nil specific exam findings - Nl l evidence of surgical problems (e.g. [Hernias (Paediatrics)](Hernias%20(Paediatrics).md)) ## Treatment - Supportive

title: Oesophageal atresia
content: # Esophageal atresia Newborn with type C esophageal atresia X-ray (AP view with contrast): contrast agent filling a blind-ended esophageal pouch (green overlay) that ends at the level of the third thoracic vertebra. Inside the pouch, a nasogastric tube can be seen (dashed lines). There is abundant gas in the gastrointestinal tract, indicating patent tracheoesophageal fistula. ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402173700.png)

title: Allergies
content: # Allergy ## **Allergy: terminology and tests** ### **Terminology in paediatric allergy** - **** Allergy: a disordered reaction by the immune system to a non-toxic antigen. - **** Atopy: the predisposition to produce allergic responses. - **** Allergic disease: includes eczema, food allergies, asthma, and allergic rhinitis. Atopic diseases are common—worldwide 1 in 8 children have asthma, 1 in 13 eczema, 1 in 12 food allergies, and 1 in 8 allergic rhinitis. - **** Allergic reactions: either IgE-mediated (type I hypersensitivity reactions) or from a mixture of other inappropriate hypersensitivi

title: Fever of unknown origin in children
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/Immunology and microbiology/Fever of unknown origin in children.html
content: # Fever of unknown origin in children ### Pathogenesis of fever 1. Foreign material phagocytosis 2. Peripheral synthesis of IL-1, IL-6, TNF, IFa, pyogenic cytokines 3. Cytokines enter anterior hypothalamus 4. Induction of Prostaglandin synthesis (PGE2) → raising of hypothalamic set-point 5. Body recognises current temperature too low → elevation of body temperature to set-point 1. increased metabolic rate 2. increased muscle tone and activity 3. decreased heat loss through decreased skin perfusion 6. pyrogenic cytokines → synthesis of acute phase proteins by liver 1. decreased serum Fe, Zn 2.

title: Acute and chronic neck lumps
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/Surgery/Acute and chronic neck lumps.html
content: # Acute and chronic neck lumps ### Anatomy **Submandibular space** - subdivided by the **mylohyoid muscle into the sublingual space superiorly and submaxillary space inferiorly** - Once an infection is present, it may **spread freely through tissue planes because of communicating spaces → bilateral** - Infection can also spread to pharyngomaxillary and retropharyngeal spaces **Torticollis** – **injury during labour** → tumour → shortened and becomes tight – physio, occasionally have to divide SCM head ### Normal lump - Hyoid bone - Thyroid cartilage - Transverse process of C1 - Submandibular g

title: Branchial cleft remnant and cyst
href: IM/Paediatric Medicine/Surgery/Branchial cleft remnant and cyst.html
content: # Branchial cleft remnant/cyst ## General features - Most common lateral congenital neck mass (most typically anterolateral neck) - Found in young kids up to 10 years of age typically ### Pathophysiology - Congenital abnrmoality, diagnosed prior to adulthood - Failure of the pharyngeal cleft to involute ## Clinical features - Cyst, sinus or fistula - can become infected - can become more prominent after RTI, occasional discharge with mucous - hard/firm/SC lesions along the **SCM**, cartilage, benign, **excision for cosmetic reasons** ## Management - Asymptomatic - conservative management - Sur

title: Cystic hygroma
content: # Cystic hygroma - Types - Microcystic - Macrocystic – better if **only 1 rather than 1000 small cysts – results are good** - Investigations - **transilluminate, fluctuant** – malformation in lymphatic system → **expanding mass** – incision – 2 types ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402170002.png)

title: Penile pathology
content: # Penile pathology ### Is there a problem - Parental anxiety - Penile size - penile stretched length and girth on growth chart - Appearance of penis following circumcision - cosmetic, unlikely a functional issue - Foreskin retraction and when this should/could occur - Should be left alone, and forced retraction is not advised - 30-40% have fully retractile foreskin by 4 years - Tends not to be retractile until puberty - Foreskin erythema - misinterpreted as infection, rather than urine reaction (dermatitis of foreskin) - addressed by frequent nappy changes and improved hygiene - Retained smegm

title: Retropharyngeal abscess
content: # Retropharyngeal abscess ### General features - Typically polymicrobial flora with causative organisms including GPCs, GNBs and anaerobes such as - Streptococcus pyogenes - Staphylococcus aureus - Prevotella melaninogenicus - Fusobacterium spp. ### Clinical features - Mouth and throat pain - Trismus (limited mouth opening) - Hot potato voice - Inability to swallow saliva and stridor suggest imminent airway compromise - Fever, tachycardia, and progression to septic shock - Bull neck appearance - Tripod position and respiratory distress - Tongue appears displaced superiorly and anteriorly, and

content: # Sebaceous cyst - General features - (collection of fluid) – dermal lesion - Sebaceous areas – head, neck, back, shoulders, genitals - Investigation - attached when move the skin - **Punctum in the middle** ![](figs/Pasted%20image%2020220402165521.png)

title: Pain medicine
content: # Pain medicine ## [Pain assessment](Pain%20medicine/Pain%20assessment.md) Pain is a multifaceted disorder characterised by both physical subjective experiences and its emotional response. Consideration must be given towards the functional limitations resulting from pain. ## Pain management The goal of pain management is to improve function and quality of life. Pain management should focus on non-pharmacological therapy with consideration of the biopsychosocial models of health. See: - [Nonpharmacological options for pain management](Pain%20medicine/Nonpharmacological%20options%20for%20pain%20

title: Essential Pain Management
content: # Essential Pain Management - Pain can be acute or chronic - childbirth, trauma, cancer as causes of pain - Think about 1. Recognising - relationship between psychological and physical factors of pain - Definition - **An unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with, or resembling that associated with, actual or potential tissue damage.** (International Assocation for the Study of Pain) - affected by biopsychosocial factors - experience of pain should be respected - adverse effects on functional, social and psychological wellbeing 1. Assessing 2. Treatment - non-pharmacological s

title: Management of cancer pain
content: # Management of cancer pain ## Background - up to 90% of advanced cancer patients have pain - 33% of cancer pain remainds untreated/undertreated - pain made of - physical pain - psychosocial and spiritual issues/suffering ## Management - Multidisciplinary teams - nurses, psychologists, chaplains, social workers - Nonpharmacological interventions - CBT - coping techniques - physical thereapy - functional improvement - radiation therapy - directed at underlying lesion - Pharmacological pain management - Paracetamol - NSAIDs - Glucocorticoids - topical therapies - neuropathic agents - antidepress

title: Pharmacological options for pain management
href: IM/Pain medicine/Pharmacological options for pain management.html
content: # Pharmacological options for pain management ## Non-opioid therapy options - First line - Paracetamol - NSAIDs - Topical NSAIDs (e.g. diclofenac) have few systemic side effects - Muscle relaxants - short course may be considered for acute pain - Nonopioid adjuvants for neuropathic pain syndromes - [Gabapentanoids](../../BCS/Pharmacology/Classes/Gabapentanoids.md) (gabapentin, pregabain) - [Serotonin-Norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors](../../BCS/Pharmacology/Classes/Serotonin-Norepinephrine%20reuptake%20inhibitors.md) - [Antiepileptic drugs (AEDs)](../../BCS/Pharmacology/Classes/Antiepileptic%

content: # Symptom management in pallative care ## Background - Effective symptom management crucial to palliative care interventions --- ## Dyspnoea ### Background - Most prominent and distressing symptom with underlying illness - Goal - address underlying cause (e.g. pleural effusion, infection, anaemia, COPD, heart failure) and treat hypoxaemia ### Management - Hypoxaemia → supplemental O2 - Refractory dyspnoea → morphine - *reduces sensation of dyspnoea, do not cause respiratory depression or hasten death when appropriately selected and dosed* --- ## Oropharyngeal secretions ### Background - Audibl

title: Ankylosing spondylitis
content: # Ankylosing spondylitis ## Background - Chronic inflammatory disorder affecting the axial skeleton - Up to 20% of those who are HLA B27 positive will develop the condition ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Back pain affecting young males, typically HLA-B27 (spondylnegative) associated. Presents with loss of lumbar lordosis, chest pain, hyper-extended neck and hip/knee flexion, sacroilitis. XR shows bamboo spine and dyndesmophytes from ligamental calcification. - Affected articulations develop bony or fibrous changes - Typical spinal features include loss of the lumbar lordosis and progressi

title: Myositis and inflammatory myopathies
href: IM/Rheumatology/Connective Tissue Disorders/Myositis and inflammatory myopathies.html
content: # Myositis ## Background - Autoimmune disorders - common features is immune mediated muscle injury - presents with muscle weakness and elevated muscle enzymes (CK/aldolase) - Includes - [[Connective Tissue Disorders/Dermatomyositis](Polymyositis%20and%20Dermatomyositis.md) - [Polymyositis and Dermatomyositis](Polymyositis%20and%20Dermatomyositis.md) - Overlap myositis - [Inclusion body myositis](Inclusion%20body%20myositis.md) - Necrotising autoimmune myositis ## Differentials for inflammatory myopathies - Inflammatory myopathy - Drug-induced myopathy - Steroids - Statins - Colchicine - Hydrox

title: Polymyositis and Dermatomyositis
content: # Polymyositis and dermatomyositis ## Clinical features - DM/PM - PM (>90%): Symmetrical proximal muscle weakness - DM (70-90%): Symmetrical proximal muscle weakness - 25-50%: mylagia and muscle tenderness - 50% have a preceding rash - Musclar weakness located in - deltoid, hip flexor, neck flexor - diaphragmatic/intercostal muscles (→ can result in hypoventilation and T2RF) - upper oesophageal (Striated) muscle weakness → dysphagia, aspiration - Myocarditis → increased risk of MI - ILD in 10% - may be subclinical - Involvement of other organ systems - Gastrointestinal - pharyngeal muscle - dy

title: Scleroderma
content: # Scleroderma (Systemic sclerosis) ## Background - systemic disease affecting skin, lung, heart, GIT, kidney - incidence 19/1000000 ## Pathology - Vascular and endothelial dysfunction - autoimmunity - fibroblastic activation and proliferation Two general forms 1. Localised scleroderma 1. morphea 2. linear scleroderma - en coup de sabre - cephalic subtype variant of localised scleroderma 1. Systemic sclerosis 1. diffuse 2. limited (CREST) - Calcinosis - Raynaud's - Esophageal dysmotility - Sclerodactyly - Telangiectasias 1. Sine scleroderma - internal organ involvement without skin involvement

title: Sjogrens syndrome
content: # Sjogrens syndrome ## Background - Autoimmune disorder characterised by salivary and lacrimal gland dysfunction - decreased tear and saliva production ## Pathology 1. Primary - prevalence: 2-10/10,000 2. Secondary - secondary to autoimmune disorders incl [Systemic Lupus Erythematosis (SLE)](../Vasculitis/Systemic%20Lupus%20Erythematosis%20(SLE).md) - secondary RA - systemic sclerosis (Scleroderma) Pathophysiology - proliferation and infiltration of lymphocytes in exocrine glands - autoantibody production and role of B-cells in pathophys are unclear ## Clinical features - Keratoconjunctivitis

title: Gout
content: # Gouty Arthritis ## Background - Metabolic heterogenous disease - Abnormal urate deposits in tissues - deposition occurs with supersaturated uric acid levels - Mechanism of hyperuricaemia - overproduction of uric acid - underexcretion of uric acid - Populations at risk - Pacific islanders (e.g. Filipinos, Samoans) - Familial risk - heritable component - Men (90%) over 30 - Postmenopausal women - Precpipitants - EtOH excess (particularly beer) - Hospitalised patient - Changes in medications affecting urate metabolism - Fasting prior to medical procedures - Chronic lead intoxication (saturnine

title: Osteoarthritis
content: # Osteoarthritis ## Background - Primary from old age, Secondary from repeat injuries; mainly affects the weight bearing joints ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Radiographic changes - Joint space narrowing - Osteophytes (bony spurs) - Subchondral sclerosis - Subcondral cysts ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220925005957.png) ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Mx: 1. Pain relief (NSAIDS → opioids; can consider intra-articular injections of CS to reduce inflammation) 1. NSAIDs more effective than panadol for osteoarthritis - should concomitantly start with PPI if ris

title: Rheumatoid arthritis
content: # Rheumatoid arthritis ## Background - systemic disease affecting joints - untreated or undertreated → pain, disability, risk of death - extra-articular effects - lung, increased CVD risk → premature mortality of 7-10 years driven by these effects - inflammation → symptoms → joint damage → cardiac risk - early recognition and intervention improves functional outcomes - F:M = 3:1, onset 30-60 years - 0.5-1% of worldowide population ## Pathology - HLA-DR1/4 associated disease, worsened by smoking, with chronic joint inflammation. Presents with early morning stiffness, and degenerative change on

title: Vasculitis
content: # Vasculitis ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231102115629.png) ## When to consider vasculitis - Multisystem disease - unexplained constitutional ssx - skin lesions (palpable purpura) - Ischemic vascular changes (gangrene, claudication, Raynaud's, livedo) - Glomerulonephritis - RCC casts - mononeuritis multiplex - Myalgia, arthralgia/arthritis - Abdominal (intestinal angina) or testicular pain ## Small vessels (Venules, Arterioles) - [Drug induced vasculitis (Leukocytoclasic Vasculitis)](Vasculitis/Drug%20induced%20vasculitis%20(Leukocytoclasic%20Vasculitis).md) and serum sickness - PTU -

title: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Churg Strauss)
href: IM/Rheumatology/Vasculitis/ANCA vasculitidies/Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Churg Strauss).html
content: # Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Churg Strauss) ## Background ## Pathology ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231026015155.png) ## Clinical features - Asthma - Eosinophilia - Pulmonary infiltrates (non-fixed) - Paranasal sinus abnormality - Nerve - mononeuropathy or polyneuropathy - Extravascular eosinophils (on biopsy) 1. Phase 1: Adult onset asthma - most require steroids for control 2. Phase 2: Eosinophilic infiltrates in lung, GIT. Lung infiltrates typically peripheral, patchy and assymetrical 3. Phase 3: Constitutional Sx and systemic disease, peripheral neuropathy

title: Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegeners Granulomatosis)
content: # Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegeners Granulomatosis) ## Background ## Pathology ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231026015138.png) ## Clinical features - Upper respiratory symptoms - sinusitis - epistaxis - hearing loss - Otitis media - note that adults RARELY get otitis media; if an adult gets recurrent otitis media without immunodeficiency syndrome, they need to be further investigated - oral ulcer - Lower respiratory tract involvement - bronchitis - hemoptysis due to capillaritis, diffuse alveolar haemorrhage - cavitating or noncavitating nodules - Pulmonary/renal syndrome

title: Microscopic Polyangiitis
content: # Microscopic Polyangiitis ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - mononeuritis multiplex - Pulmonary - alveolar haemorrhage - can be hard to differentiate from [Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (Wegeners Granulomatosis)](Granulomatosis%20with%20Polyangiitis%20(Wegeners%20Granulomatosis).md) ## Diagnostic criteria - MPO ANCA positive ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Antisynthetase syndrome
content: # Antisynthetase syndrome ## Background - associated with antisynthetase antibodies (classically anti-Jo-1) ## Pathology - anti-aminoacyl-transfer ribonucleic acid synthetase (anti-tRNA synthetase) ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240305130422.png) ## Clinical features - Fever - Raynaud's - Inflammatory arthritis - Mechanics hands - coarsening of lateral aspects of fingers, nail fold changes, ILD - Mechanics hands (hyperkeratotic lesions and skin cracking) - constitutional symptoms - myositis - Raynaud's - non-erosive arthritis - ILD - 30-50% of DM/PM ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image

title: Giant Cell Arteritis
content: # Temporal arteritis (Giant cell arteritis) ARA criteria - Age >= 50 - Absence of exclusion criteria (eye, skin, kidney, peripheral nerve, lung, LN disease, digital gangrene) - 3 of 11 points - new onset localised headache - sudden onset visual loss - PMR (2) - jaw claudication - abnormal temporal artery (2) - unexplained fever/anaemia - ESR 50 - compatible pathology (2) Investigations - Ultrasound - halo sign - from circumferential wall thickening and vascular wall edema ![](../../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020241221172707.png) ## Background - Inflammatory disorder of arteries frequently i

title: Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN)
content: # Polyarteritis Nodosa ## Background - Epidemiology - Middle aged male ## Pathology - medium vessel vasculitis - mainly affects visceral arteries, especially at bifurcations by forming 'aneurysms' - affects organs - spleen, bowel, kidney; doesnt tend to cause a GN (too big) - associated with hepatitis B - ADA2 deficiency (autosomal recessive) - single gene mutation vasculitis - tends to affect skin/CNS more ## Clinical features - Abdominal pain - Skin ulcers - necrotic skin lesions - Neuropathy - wrist drop can be seen in mononeuritis multiplex - hard to recover without quick treatment - if ne

title: Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR)
content: # Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR) ## Background - Age > 50 - one of the most common systemic rheumatic conditions - Lifetime risk - Women - 2.43%, men 1.66% - can occur in almost 50% of [Giant Cell Arteritis](Large%20vessel%20vasculitidies/Giant%20Cell%20Arteritis.md) - 10% of PMR develops [Giant Cell Arteritis](Large%20vessel%20vasculitidies/Giant%20Cell%20Arteritis.md) ## Pathology ## Clinical features - 1/12 history of prolonged morning stiffness and aching in 3+ areas - shoulders - hips girdle - thighs - neck - normal muscle strength (but may be pain limited) - associated with depression, fat

title: Raynaud's Phenomena
content: # Raynaud's Phenomena ## Background ## Pathology 1. Primary Raynaud's - common in young women (<30y) - often have family history - ANA usually negative (note population positive ANA is pretty high, so can be overlap) 1. Secondary Raynaud's - Onset in age > 35 - More severe - Digital ulcers, pitting scars in fingers - Abnormal capillary microscopy - Presenece of autoantibodies - need to look - Associated with systemic illness including autoimmune disease (e.g. [Scleroderma](../Connective%20Tissue%20Disorders/Scleroderma.md)) - Associated with - Connective tissue disease: [Scleroderma](../Connec

title: Chancroid
content: # Chancroid ## Clinical features - Ulcer - Painful, non-indurated ulcer - Localized to prepuce and frenulum in men, vulva or Cervix in women, and at perineum - Serpiginous border - Friable base - Lymph - Painful, unilateral, inguinal Lymphadenitis (50% of cases) - Buboes - Develops from swollen Lymph Nodes - May rupture if become fluctuant ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220721191248.png) ## Diagnosis 1. PCR testing 2. Gram stain - for Haemophilus ducrei

title: Chlamydia Trachomatis
content: # Chlamydia Trachomatis ## Background - most commonly reported bacterial STI in USA - Screening of all sexually active women > 25 years recommended, especially with STI risk factors - High risk men (e.g. MSM, STI clinics) should be screened ## Clinical features - Oropharyngeal and recal infection possible - Asymptomatic and mild pulmoanry histoplasmosis - resolves with treatment ## Diagnosis - Nucleic acid amplificaiton test (NAAT) - First catch urine (Men and women) - nearly identical sensitivty and specificity to swabs - Endocervical swab (Women) - Urethral swab (Men) ## Management 1. Antibi

title: Genital Herpes (Herpes Genitalis)
content: # Genital Herpes ## Clinical features - Symptoms - pain, burning, pruritis - occasional associated urethritis - accompanying tender lymphadenitis, constitutional symptoms - associated aspectic meningitis - Viral shedding can occur in absence of visible lesions - Vessicle - Multiple vesicular lesions on foreskin, labia, vagina or anus - intact or ruptured - Onset after prodromal Paresthesias - Ulcer - Painful shallow ulcers result when vessicles rupture - erythematous base - Lymph - Accompanied by lymhadenopathy with primary (first) infection ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020220721190405.pn

title: Genital Ulcer
content: # Genital Ulcer ## Background - Open soft-tissue lesion on vagina, penis, perineal and anorectal surface - Risk factors - Previous STIs - Current or former prison inmate - Low SES - Sex workers - IVDU - Multiple sexual partners ## Aetiology Most Common - Genital Herpes (60-70% of Genital Ulcers, may be HSV 1 or 2) Less common - [Syphilis](Syphilis.md) (10-20% of Genital Ulcers) - Chancroid (Haemophilus ducreyi) - Fixed Drug Eruption Rare Lymphogranuloma venereum (Chlamydia trachomatis L1, L2 and L3) - Granuloma Inguinale (Donovanosis, Klebsiella granulomatis) - Bacterial Infection - Fungal inf

title: Neisseria Gonorrhea
content: # Gonorrhea ## Background - 20-24 year adults at highest risk for N. gonorrhoeae ## Clinical features - Localised - Cervicitis, urethritis, pharyngitis, rectal infection - Disseminated - arthritis-dermatitis syndrome (Reiters syndrome) - Asymptomatic - more likely in women - thus screening recommended if >25 or STI risk factors ## Diagnosis 1. Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) - first-catch urine - endocervical, urethral, rectal, pharyngeal swabs - should be tested at least yearly in MSM - antibiotic susceptibility testing necessary if suspected dissemination 2. Cultures - Blood - Joint f

title: Sexually Trasmitted Diseases
content: # Sexually Transmitted Diseases ## Background - Epidemiology - Most commonly gonnorhea, syphilis, HPV-associated condyloma, chlamydia, HSV, trichomonas, chancroid, granuloma inguinale, scabes, louse infestation, bacterial vaginosis - oral-anal contact - shigellosis, Hepatitis A, B, C, amebiasis, giardiasis, cryptosporidiosis, salmonellosis, campylobacteriosis ## Genital ulceration - Common causes - HSV - Primary syphilis - Chancroid - Less common - Phymogranuloma venereum - Granuloma inguinale (secondary to Klebsiella granulomatis) - EBV/HIV lesions - Non-infectious - Behcet disease - Neoplasm

title: Syphilis
content: # Syphilis ## Background - Caused by Treponema Palidum (a spirochete) - Stages - Primary (Chancre) - painess - Secondary - Latent ## Clinical features - **Chancre** (ulcer in Primary Syphilis) - Single, painless, well-demarcated ulcer - Clean base - Regular, raised, Indurated border - Distribution in anogenital region (may also affect **oropharynx**) - Glans (35%) > Prepuce (19%) > Frenulum (10%) - Regional lymphadenopathy may be present - Tend to heal within 3-6 weeks - Secondary syphilis - Rash - palmar rash / soles - papules in intertriginous regions fuse to form condyloma lata - mucous pat

title: Guidelines
content: # Guidelines Clinical guidelines inform decision making - however they continuously get updated and at times it is hard to follow up on recommendations. Each update can either complete change how we approach clinical practice - it is for this reason we remain up to date. This page serges to contain my own summaries of existing guidelines, in Australia and overseas. ## Neurology - Stroke guidelines - [ANZ Stroke foundation guidelines](ANZ%20Stroke%20foundation%20guidelines.md) ## Gastroenterology - Pancreatitis - [NICE Pancreatitis guidelines 2020](NICE%20Pancreatitis%20guidelines%202020.md)
