title: Thrombolytics
content: # Thrombolytics (IV) ## Absolute contraindications - Acute intracranial haemorrhage - Extensive frank hypodensity on CT scan (> 1/3 of middle cerebral artery territory, or equivalent) - i.e. recent infarct not in reperfusion window - Active non-compressive systemic bleeding - Systemic coagulopathy (should not delay thrombolysis unless CLINICAL suspicion) - Plt < 100, 000 - INR > 1.7 including warfarin use - unfractionated heparin wthin 48h with elevated APTT - therapeutic LMWH within 24h with abnormal anti-factor Xa - DOAC use withi n48h unless reveral agent is available - Infective endocardit

title: Amiodarone
content: # Amiodarone ## Chemical structure - 2 atoms of iodine - 37% by weight - structural analogue to thyroxine - Drug properties - high iodine content (37% by weight), 7mg iodine in 200mg tablet (RDI 150mcg/adult) - H/L 100 days - can take up to 1.5 years for drug to be fully cleared ## Pharmacodynamics - Class I, II, III, IV actions - I - some degree of Sodium blockade - effect increases over time - II - noncompetitive antiadrenergic drug - starts immediately with use and stabilise over time - can cause initial dizziness - **III - mainly Class III - effect increases with chronic use - IV - calciu

title: Noradrenaline
content: # Noradrenaline ## Uses - Typical uses - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Sympathomimetic - agonist on alpha1, alpha2, beta1 - little/no effect on beta2 or dopamine receptors - Effect - positive inotrope - alpha effect - peripheral vasoconstriction - positive chronotrope ## Pharmacokinetics - A - Rapid onset 1-2 minutes - D - M - COMT and MAO - E - fast elimination - half life 3 minutes ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - Black box warning?? - Considerations ## Indications - Acute hypotension requiring. vasoconstrictor ## Contraindications - Relative contraindication

title: NICE Pancreatitis guidelines 2020
content: # NICE Pancreatitis guidelines (NG104) 2020 ## Background ## Meta-data Access date: URL: ## Preamble - For patients with severe acute pacreatitis, a full recovery may take at least 3 times as long as their hospital stay - Hospital admissions for several months is relatively common - Local complications of acute pancreatitis may take up to several weeks before we can identify if intervention is needed - indeed, delayed intervention may be safer - 15-20% of adults with severe acute pancreatitis die in hospital - On discharge, GPs should be notified on - how a patient should take their pancreatic

title: Immunotherapy related Endocrinopathies
content: # Immunotherapy related Endocrinopathies RACP dotpoint - Broad pharmacological principles and complications of chemotherapy, endocrine therapy, immunotherapy, molecular targed therapy and radiotherapy ## Immune checkpoint inhibitors - Removes break on immune system - allows immune recognition and destruction of cancer cells - also result in self-targets (off target immune responses) - monoclonal antibodies targeting specific cell surface molecules which act as checkpoints on the immune system - Anti-CTLA4 - ipilimumab - Anti-PD1 - nivolumab, pembrolizumab, cemiplimab - Anti-PDL1 - atezolizumab

title: Immunotherapies in Haematology
content: # Immunotherapies in Haematology BPT learning points - Broad pharmacological principles and complications of immunotherapy and molecularly targeted therapy ## Immunotherapies in haematological cancers - Advantages and use cases - immune responsiveness - allogeneic stem cell transplant and donor lymphocyte infusion - immune cells from donor can set up immune response - collecting donor lymphcytes and infusing in recipient can ramp up immune response and mop up outstanding malignant cells - malignancies are located close to sites of immune monitoring - e.g. lymph nodes, bone marrow, blood - immu

title: Graft vs Host Disease (GvHD)
content: # Graft vs Host Disease (GvHD) ## Background - multisystemic disorder which occur commonly after [Allogenic hematopoietic sten cell transplant (alloHSCT)](Allogenic%20hematopoietic%20sten%20cell%20transplant%20(alloHSCT).md) - Epidemiology - incidence if unknown, but reported 9-50% - common in matched unrelated donors and in haploidentical related donors - Risk factors - Major: - Degree of HLA disparity - HLA mismatch or unrelated donor - GvHD prophylaxis regimen - Minor - donor and recipient sex disparity (F to M) - intensity of transplant conditioning regimen - Lesser - increasing host age,

title: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML)
content: # Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) ## Background - normal hematopoiesis is how blood cells are produced - it isorganised in hierarchy - begins with hematopoetic stem cells - long lived, quiescent, self-renew, but not many - Epidemiology - disease of older people, median age ~ 70 years ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240116182037.png) - AML is a heterogeneous disease - important subtype is [Acute Promyelocytic Lymphoma (APML)](Acute%20Promyelocytic%20Lymphoma%20(APML).md) ## Pathology - genetic mutations in hematopoietic precursors - affects growth and differentiation - accumuluation of last n

title: Renal Replacement Therapy in ICU
href: Critical Care/Topics/Renal Replacement Therapy in ICU.html
content: # Renal replacement therapy ## Background - slow to resolve AKIs may result in toxin/electrolyte/metabolite buildup which can result in life threatening complications - fluid overload → pulmonary odema and cardiorenal syndrome - electrolyte buildup → arrhythmias - toxin buildup → encephalopathy and other end organ injury ## Physical principles 1. Removing fluid/water (ultrafiltration) 1. transmembrane pressure generation (i.e. pushing blood against a membrane until transmembrane pressure overcomes oncotic pressure) 2. osmosis (i.e. increasing dialysate pressure) to drive water across semiperme

content: # Monogenic Diabetes ## Background ## Pathology - Neonatal diabets - Mature onset diabetes of the young (MODY) ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis --- # Mature onset diabetes of the young (MODY) ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - [Type I Diabetes](Type%20I%20Diabetes.md) atypical features - ­absence of antibodies - evidence of endogenous insulin production beyond the honeymoon period (i.e., 3-5 years after the onset of diabetes) - measurable C-peptide in the presence of hyperglycaemia - low insulin requirement - ketoacidosis d

title: Slowly Evolving Immune-Mediated Diabetes (SEIMD)
content: # Slowly Evolving Immune-Mediated Diabetes (SEIMD) ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Differences between SEIMD and T1DM: - ­Obesity - ­Features of metabolic syndrome - ­Retaining greater beta cell function - ­Express a single autoantibody (usually the islet antibody) - ­Carry the transcription factor 7-like 2 gene polymorphism ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - C-peptide as a guide for therapy - ­<0.3mmol/L: multiple daily insulin injections - ­0.3-0.7 mmol/L: can treat like T2DM, consider insulin to manage beta cell failure - ­>0.7mmol/L: treat like T2DM,

title: Diabetic nephropathy
content: # Diabetic nephropathy ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Higher risk of hypoglycaemia - can lead to MI, syncope, seizures, CVA, death ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Management of Diabetes - HbA1c target 53-64 (~ 7-8%) - Note HbA1c often inaccurate due to anaemia - can consider testing fructosamine (2 week overview of BGLs) - Adjusting insulin - Kidneys responsible for 30-80% of insulin removal - no insulin adjustment required for CKD regardless of type - be mindful of possible slower clearance of insulin and risk of [Insulin stacking](Clinical%20approa

title: Oral antihyperglycaemic agents
content: ## Metformin - First line for all patients - Pros - mildly weight beneficial - cheap - low hypo risk - Adverse effects - nausea, diarrhoea - GI side effects less likely if XR version - lactic acidosis - rare on its own - B12 deficiency - Dosing - 500mg BD and increase to 1g BD within 1 week as an inpatient - dose reduce if renal function - eGFR < 60, max 1g/day - ­GFR <30 contraindicated - ­GFR 30-60 max dose is 1g/day - ­GFR >60 max dose is 2g/day - Expect HbA1c reduction 1% ## Sulfonylureas - Previously second line - Benefit - Cheap - Adverse effect - hypoglycaemia - can be very potent in pa

title: Euglycaemic ketoacidosis
content: # Euglycaemic ketoacidosis ## Background ## Pathology - Examples: dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin - Not recommended for use in T1DM - Withhold 3-5 days prior to surgeries/procedures and in the context of fasting states or illnesses - Start these medications or recommence when patients are well and eating normally ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management Management: 10% dextrose often helps clear ketones faster, and is a good option in the elderly/renal failure/heart failure ## Prognosis Increase the risk of DKA in T2DM by 7-fold

title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic Syndrome (HHS)
content: # Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic Syndrome (HHS) ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features Usually evolves over a few days ­Hypovolaemia ­Absent ketosis/acidosis ­Generally, severely dehydrated and unwell ­Often have a reduced level of consciousness/confusion ­Calculate the serum osmolality: 2Na (calculated) + urea + formal BGL ­Osmolality >320 is considered generally diagnostic ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ­Check BGL, ketones, UEC, CMP, VBG, Osmolality, formal BGL ­Start IV fluids ­Cease any OHAs ## Prognosis

title: Diabetic ketoalkalosis
content: # Diabetic ketoalkalosis ## Background ## Pathology Profuse vomiting (often in DKA) leads to a metabolic alkalosis ­Occurs due to hydrogen ion loss from the GIT and contraction alkalosis from volume depletion ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ­High bicarbonate ­Hyperaldosteronism -> induces hypokalaemia ­Often there is co-existing DKA (need to calculate an anion gap as the acidosis can be masked) ## Investigations ## Management ­Treatment is the same as for [Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)](Diabetic%20Ketoacidosis%20(DKA).md) ­Require a lot of fluid resuscitation ## Prognosis

title: Engraftment syndrome
content: # Engraftment syndrome ## Definition - noninfectious complication of autologous SCT and allogenic SCT - Epidemiology - 8-50% of autoSCT - 10-77% of alloSCT - assocaited with acute GvHD - typically occurs within 4 days of granulocyte recovery ## Pathology - Neutrophil recovery after HSCT results in engraftment syndrome - proinflammatory cytokine release occurs prior to neutrophil engraftment - endothelial injury and activated granulocytes in setting of proinflammatory cytokines - results in increased systemic capillary permeability ## Clinical features - non-infectious fever - most common sympt

title: Botched milk - ICU teaching
content: ## Presentation - 61M with worsening diplopia, bulbar symptoms, weakness - no significant comorbidies or precipitating illnesses - onset 8/1, awoke with diplopia - ataxia and dysarthria / coordination and balance issues when walking with dog - noticed speech and word-finding dififculities → ED presentation in evening - no associated headache, back pain, neurological symptoms - no fevers, diarrhoea (?GBS), respiratory symptoms - PMHx - Hypertension - low dose statin and antihypertensive - dyslipidaemia - no diabetes history - Social History - worked in business, born in Australia - no recent tr

title: HIV
content: # HIV ## Background - Prevalence - 0.02% of worldwide HIV in Australia - 30% prevalence in some areas of Africa - Transmission - MSM > heterosexual - almost no transmission via IVDU anymore - Types - HIV-1 most common in western world - HIV-2 more common in western central africa - less virulent, lower viral load, lower rates of vertical transmission, slower progression - Strains - R5 - most frequently transmitted, less aggressive, infect macrophages and T-cells, and use CXCR5 for CD4+ entry - X4 - infects only T-cell and T-cell lines, more aggressive, use CXCR4 for CD4+ cell entry ## Patholog

title: Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS)
content: # Guillain-Barre Syndrome (GBS) ## Background - Acute immune-mediated polyneuropathy - incidence - 1-2/100,000 person years - M > F, increased with age ## Pathology - Antecedent event → molecular mimicry with immune response - peripheral nerve damage occurs - Triggers - typically occur in preceding 4 weeks - campylobacter jejuni, CMV, influenza A and B, HIV, Zikavirus, COVID19 ## Clinical features - Progressive and symmetric muscle weakness (ascending) - most common cause of acute flaccid paralysis - Absent or depressed deep tendon reflexes - Sensory symptoms and dysautonomia - Progress over 2

title: Clostridium Botulinum
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by disease/Bacterial infections/Clostridium Botulinum.html
content: # Clostridium Botulinum ## Background - Clinical syndrome - botulism - botulism can rarely be caused by other clostridium species (C.baratii, B.noyvii, C.butyricum) - Epidemiology - widespread in environment - Incidence - once every 2 years in Australia - Transmission - Food consumption - infant, adult - Infants - untreated honey - adults - low income countries - e.g. pickled foods and long storage - Inoculation - wounds, IVDU, iatrogenic - Bioterrorism ## Pathology - Binds presynaptic motor neuron NMJ irreversibly - inhibits ACh release - 7 immunological toxin types: A-G - A, B, E, F - human

title: Bulbar Palsy
content: ### What is bulbar palsy? [Bulbar palsy](https://www.osmosis.org/learn/Anatomy_clinical_correlates:_Glossopharyngeal_(CN_IX),_vagus_(X),_spinal_accessory_(CN_XI)_and_hypoglossal_(CN_XII)_nerves) refers to a set of signs and symptoms linked to the impaired function of the lower [cranial nerves](https://www.osmosis.org/learn/Cranial_nerves), typically caused by damage to their lower motor neurons or to the lower [cranial nerve](https://www.osmosis.org/learn/Cranial_nerves) itself. The impacted cranial nerves are a set of nerves that arise straight from the [brainstem](https://www.osmosis.org/lea

title: Sedation whilst intubated
content: # Sedation whilst intubated in ICU ## Sedation - Purpose: tube tolerance ('aim tube tolerance') - Propofol - 25mL/hr → (1% → 250mg/hr) - dosage can change based on context - theatre GA can be up to 100-200mL/hr - central respiratory depression, hypotension - propofol infusion syndrome - more common in critically unwell children, long term infusion, high rate infusion - metabolic derangement → hyperkalemia, acute kidney injury, arrhythmias, organ failure - acute refractory bradycardia progressing to asystole and one or more of: 1. metabolic acidosis 2. rhabdomyolysis 3. hyperlipidaemia 4. enlar

title: Post-operative physiology

title: Susac syndrome

title: Diabetes management in geriatrics
content: - BGL should be >6 - do not start treatment until fasting BGL > 7 - Peak BGL should be <= 11 - HbA1c goal 7-7.5% - if life expectancy < 5 years, HbA1c 8-9% acceptable - Treat BP and hypercholesterolaemia

title: Urinary incontinence
content: # Urinary incontinence ## Background - 4-5% of population - more common in elderly females - Risk factors - advanced age - previous pregnancy/childbirth - high BMI - hysterectomy - FHx ## Definitions 1. Overactive bladder / urge incontinence - detrusor overactivity - urge to urinate followed by uncontrollable leakage 1. Stress inconitnence - small leaking when increased intra-abdominal pressure 1. Mixed incontinence - mix of above 1. Overflow incontinence - bladder outlet obstruction (E.g. prostate enlargement) 1. Functional incontinence - secondary to comorbid physical condition - dementia, s

title: Fluid Resuscitation
content: # Fluid resuscitation ## Types of fluids 1. Crystalloids - predominantly ionic solute 2. Colloids - solution/suspension of organic, non-ionized solute with MW > 30,000D ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231215091710.png) ## Resuscitation vs Maintenance fluids - Resuscitation fluids - USE: restore ECV in absolute or relative fluid deficiency - only to be done when >=1 marker of hypovolemia: 1. Oliguria 2. Hypotension 3. Tachycardia 4. Poor central/peripheral perfusion 5. lactatemia (suggestive of end-organ hypoperfusion) - Isotonic fluids - crystalloid or colloid ONLY - Maintenance fluids -

title: Targeted Temperature Management
content: # Targeted Temperature Management (TTM) ## Background - TTM - deliberate hypothermia with a range of 32-34C in patients who remain unconscious post ROSC - Therapeutic hypothermia - aim of 33-35C - induced normothermia - aim 37C in hyperthermic patients ## Physiology - Animal model (empirical) studies - reduces secondary injury - Hypothermia: 1. Reduced neuronal excitotoxicity 2. reduced apoptosis and inflammation 3. reduced FR production - can lead to DNA, mitochondrial damage and apoptosis 4. maintains blood flow, metabolic and BBB integrity 5. improves neurogenesis, gliogenesis, angiogenesis

title: TTM1
content: # TTM1 - Targeted Temperature Management at 33C versus 36C after Cardiac Arrest ## PICO - Population - unconscious survivors of OHH cardiac arrest - 36 ICUs in Europe and Australia - 18 years or older, GCS < 8 - >20 minutes of spontaneous circulation after ROSC - Exclusion - > 240 minutes from ROSC - unwitnessed arrest with asystole as initial rhythm - suspected or known acute intracranial haemorrhage or stroke - body temperature < 30C - Intervention - 33C TTM - 36 hours - ice cold fluids, ice packs, intravascular or surface temperature management - Comparator - 36C TTM - Outcome - Primary - a

title: Status Epilepticus
content: # Status Epilepticus ## 0-5 minutes - ABCs - IV access - Bloods to be sent - FBC EUC CMP LFT and antiepileptic drug levels - BSL ## 5-15 minute seizures 1. IV Midazolam 5mg followed by 5 minute wait, up to 15mg 1. No IV access - IM midazolam 10mg at a time ## >15 minutes 1. IV levetiracetam load - 60mg/kg up to 4.5g in 100mL 0.9% NaCl over 10 minutes - Contraindications (absolute) - Contraindications (relative) - mood or behaviour disorder 1. Phenytoin 20mg/kg IV up to 2g over 30 minutes (needs cardiac monitor) - **reduce rate to 25mg/min in elderly or cardiac disease** - Contraindications (ab

title: Hemophagocytic Lymphohistiocytosis (HLH)
content: # Hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis ## Background - Aggressive and life threatening - Occurs typically 0-18mo, but also in children/adults - Causes - Familial (genetic)/primary - seen in infants and young children - Sporadic/secondary/acquired - adults with infection, malignancy, rheumatologic disease - can mimic SIRS response - Triggered by events which disrupt homeostasis of immune system - infection common trigger ## Classifications - HLH Syndrome - condition of pathologic immune activation associated with genetic defects of lymphocyte cytotoxicity - HLH disease - HLH syndrome with distinc

title: Chronotropes
content: # Chronotropes ## Pharmacodynamics - b1 activity increases chronotropy AND inotropy - noradrenaline, adrenaline and dobutamine all act on b1 - b1 effect induces chronotropy Beta 1 MOA - Ga/sPCR → adenylyl cyclase activation → (ATP → cAMP) → PKA activation → L-type Ca2 channel phosphoylation → increased IC Ca2+ - RAAS activation Beta 2 MOA - GPCR → AC → cAMP → PKA → MLCK (myosin light chain kinase) inactivation - smooth muscle relaxation in bronchi, peripheries, GIT and uterine - hepatic glycogenolysis - glucagon release ## Isoprenaline / isoprenterenol - Pharmacodynamics - B1R agonist - heart

title: Non-pacemaker cardiomyocytes
content: # Non-pacemaker cardiomyocytes ## Background ## Definition ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231225013136.png) - Phase 0 - Rapid depolarisation - Sodium channel activation and input - L-type Ca2+ channels also open - Phase 1 - Early repolarisation - Voltage gated sodium channels are now closed - Potassium channel (Kv) activation and efflux - Na/Ca exchanger also opens - Phase 2 - plateau - Calcium channel (L-type, T-type) opens and concurrent K+ efflux (via slow delayed K+ rectifier) and Ca2+ entry - Phase 3 - repolarisation - Calcium channel closure - K+ efflux continues via three chann

title: Pacemaker cardiomyocytes
content: # Pacemaker cardiomyocytes ## Background ## Definition ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231225013125.png) - Phase 0 - rapid depolarisation - VG Na+ channels inactivated (permanently - i.e. there is no Na channel role in pacemaker cells) - mediated by L-type calcium channels - Upstroke of membrane potential due to opening of Ca2+ channels. As the resting potential of pacemakers is lower than of normal cells, the Na+ channel is permanently inactivated. - conduction velocity is slow (not a rapid AP) → results in the slowing of transmission at the AV node, between atrium and ventricles - Ph

title: Procainamide
content: # Procainamide ## Uses - Typical uses - ventricular arrhythmia - Used in pre-excited atrial fibrillation (e.g. WPW patient developing AF) - slows down accessory pathway conduction - Cardioversion of AF or VT - Termination of AVRT - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Class 1a (immediate acting sodium channel blocker) arrhythmic - increases effective refractory period - reduces impulse conduction in atria - reduces excitability ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - 20-50mg until arrhythmia suppressed, then 1-4mg/min afterwards - Metabolism - Hepatic metabolism with active metabolite (NAPA) - Eliminatio

title: Lidocaine (Lignocaine)
content: # Lidocaine (Lignocaine) ## Uses - Typical uses - Local anaesthetic - Anti-arrhythmic - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Class 1b antiarrhythmic - fast Na channel blocker - Effect - reversible neural blockade - shortened AP and refractory period ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - 1mg/kg loading then 20-50mcg/kg/minute - Absorption - Effect onset 45-90 seconds - Metabolism - Hepatic metabolism - metabolites can lower seizure threshold - Elimination - 100 minute halflife - reduced clearance in hepatic/cardiac failure ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - inihibition of inhibit

title: Flecainide
content: # Flecainide ## Uses - Typical uses - Symptomatic paroxysmal AF - suppression of foci and re-entrant arrhythmias - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Class 1c antiarrhythmic (sodium channel) - Effect - decreased dromotropy - ectopic suppression ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - 2mg/kg over 10 minutes then 1.5mg/kg/hr after - Metabolism - Hepatic - Elimination - T1/2 = 18h (50% excreted unchanged in urine) ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - negative inotropy - hepatotoxicity - similar CNS changes to [Lidocaine (Lignocaine)](Lidocaine%20(Lignocaine).md) - Black box warning??

title: Metoprolol
content: # Metoprolol ## Uses - Typical uses - Heart failure - Atrial arrhythmias - Hypertension - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Beta blockade (Class II) - decreased chronotropy - decreased inotrophy - decreased myocardial O2 demand - Absorption - peak effect in 20 minutes - Distribution - Metabolism - Hepatic metabolism - 60% first pass - Elimination - T1/2 = 3-8h - excreted in urine ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - IV 1-2mg aliquots up to 20mg - PO 12.5-100mg BD ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - bronchospasm - Fatigue - bradycardia - hypotension - Black box warning?? - Con

title: Esmolol
content: # Esmolol ## Uses - Typical uses - acute supraventricular dysrhythmias - hypertension - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - beta blockade (class II antiarrhythmic) - decreased dromotropy - ectopic suppression ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - 50-150mcg/kg/min - Absorption - peak effect 5 minutes - Metabolism - by plasma esterases - Elimination - T1/2 = 9 minutes (50% excreted in urine unchanged) ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - bronchospasm - fatigue - bradycardia - hypertension - Black box warning?? - Considerations ## Indications ## Contraindications - Relative contrain

title: Verapamil
content: # Verapamil ## Uses - Typical uses - hypertension - supraventricular tachyarrhythmia - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - Class IV (L-type Ca2+ Channel inhibitor) - decreases automaticity and conduction velocity - increased refractory period - decreased SVR and coronary vasodilation ## Pharmacokinetics - Dose - 5-10mg IV - PO 240-480mg in divided doses - Absorption - peak effect in 10 minutes - Distribution - Metabolism - hepatic - Elimination - T1/2 = 6h (mostly urinary elimination - renal adjustment needed) ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - negative inotropy - flus

title: Heparin Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)
content: # Heparin Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) ## Background - Occurs secondary to exposure to heparin (UFH) or LMWH - present in ICU patients - thrombocytopenia is common (in 50%) - Risk factors - Heparin exposure volume (therapeutic vs. prophylactic) - PF4 release (surgical/trauma release > medical) ## Classification 1. HIT-1 - benign, non-immune condition - 10-30% of patients within 4 days post heparin exposure 1. HIT-2 (conventional 'HITTS') - Immune-mediated syndrome from antibody to PF4-heparin complex - Occurs in 1-5% with UFH and <1% in LMWH ## Pathology - Platelet factor 4 is released on pl

content: # Cephalosporins ## Background - No activity against LAME - listeria - atypicals (mycoplasma/chlamydia) - MRSA - Enterococci ## Cephalosporin generationes ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231227103324.png) 1. 1st gen - better against gram positive 1. Cefalexin 2. Cefazolin 3. Cefadroxil, Cefazedone 2. 2nd gen 1. Cefaclor 2. Cefuroxime 3. Cefotetan, Cefprozil 3. 3rd gen- better against gram negative 1. Ceftriaxone 2. Ceftazidime 3. Cefotaxime 4. Cefixime, Cefdinir, Cefodizine 4. 4th gen - pseudomonas cover 1. Cefepime 2. Cefquinome 5. 5th gen - MRSA cover - Ceftaroline - Ceftobiprole, Ce

title: Cerebral Salt-wasting syndrome
content: # Cerebral Salt-wasting syndrome ## Background - Most common presentation - hyponatremia after SAH - SNa will drop whilst UNa increases after a few days - patient becomes hypovolemic hyponatremic (Due to loss of fluid - Also reported in - CNS surgery including pituitary, vestibular schwannoma, calvarial remodelling and after head injury, intracranial malignancy, CNS infection ## Pathology - Trauma or tumours in/around brain (e.g. SAH) - BNP release into circulation via disrupted BBB - acts on collecting ducts of tubules - inhibits Na resorption - decreases release of renin - SNS injury cannot

title: Limbic encephalitis
content: ## Limbic encephalitis categories 1. Infectious encephalitis - invasion of limbic area of brain by bacteria/virus - HSV preferentially target virus - Other viruses - HSV-1/2 - VZV - HHV-6 (immunocompromised) - Enterovirus - West Nile Virus - Bacterial - Syphilis - Tuberculosis - Bartonella Henselae 1. Autoimmune encephalitis 1. Paraneoplastic Limbic Encephalitis (PLE) - 15% - small cell lung cancer, testicular tumors, thymoma, breast cancer, Hodgkin lymphoma - thymoma or SCLC most common 1. Nonparaneoplastic limbic encephalitis (NPLE) - more common than PLE - Antibodies: Hu (may also involve c

title: Primary Hemostasis
content: # Primary hemostasis ## Background ## Endothelium prevents primary hemostasis physiologically - Normal endothelium inhibits platelet adhesion and aggregation by releasing factors and mediators - Anti-aggregation factors - PGI2 (Prostacyclin) - allows vasodilation - tPA (tissue plasminogen activator) - lyses fibrin (released in small amounts with small tissue injury) - EDRF (Nitrous oxide) - short lived vasodilator - Inactivation factors - ADPase - breaks down ADP from platelets which are released during primary hemostasis - Thrombomodulin - thrombin receptor (?) - Heparan sulfate - increases a

title: Glanzmann's Thrombasthenia
content: # Glanzmann's Thrombasthenia ## Background - defect in GP-IIb-IIIa ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Mucocutaneous bleeding - increased bleeding time - similat ro vWD - normal platelet count and coagulation studies - no response to platelet aggregation studies with ADP as ADP stimulates GP IIb/IIIa ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Secondary Hemostasis
content: ## Secondary Hemostasis ### Cascade hypothesis helps explain bleeding - Coagulation proteins circulate as inactive zymogens - when activated, they become serine protease - this needs to happen in presence of phospholipid surface (platelets), calcium and other cofactors - However the cascade hypothesis is limited - 'contact' factors in the intrinsic pathway doesnt impact bleeding in vivo - FIIa/TF activation cannot accomodate for a factor VIII/IX deficiency - hemophiliacs will continue to bleed - FXI deficiency causes milder bleeding than a hemophilia which cannot be accounted by this pathway !

title: Antifibrinolytics
content: # Antifibrinolytics ## Tranexamic acid (TXA) - synthetic derivative of lysin → binds the lysin binding sites (x5) on plasminogen - Results in - inhibited plasmin formation - displaced plasminogen from fibrin surface - *i.e. it cucks plasminogen so it cannot bind to fibrin* ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231230122032.png)

title: NETosis
content: # NETosis - Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) bind bacteria and platelets - neutrophils which are activated translocate their granular enzymes and effectively break down their own nucleus - *this is like a suicide-homicide* - it kills itself to kill everything around it - the mix of granular enzymes/nucleus is released as a 'net' which kills bacteria nearby - this mechanism also traps platelets and causes thrombi ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231230122512.png)

title: Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP)
content: # Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) ## Background - Autoimmune condition of antibody binding to platelets - splenic clearance of platelets from circulation - additionally lack appropriate platelet compensation so cannot recover ## Classification - Primary (idiopathic) - most adult patients - Secondary - Autoimmune associated - SLE - Lymphoproliferative disease - e.g. Lymphoma - Medications - infections - e.g. Hepatitis C, HIV, H.pylori ## Pathology - Antibody targets - GPIIb/IIIa, GP-Ib/IX ## Clinical features - Mucocutaneous bleeding (depending on platelet count - would typically require < 20* 10

title: Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA)
content: # Thrombotic Microangiopathy (TMA) ## Background - Consist of [Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)](#Thrombotic%20Thrombocytopenic%20Purpura%20(TTP)) and [Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome (HUS)](#Hemolytic%20Uremic%20Syndrome%20(HUS)) - Can be medication induced - cyclosporine, quinine, ticlopidine, clopidogrel, mitomycin C, bleomycin - can be ADAMTS-13 inhibition/underactivity induced (see below) - Also see [Drug-induced Thrombotic Microangiopathy (DITMA)](Drug-induced%20Thrombotic%20Microangiopathy%20(DITMA).md) ## Pathology - Platelet aggregation and fibrin thrombi formation in microvasculat

title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
content: # Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation ## Background - uncontrolled local or systemic activation of coagulation - coagulation factor depletion and fibrinogen depletion - platelet activation and consumption → thrombocytopenia - associated with underlying disorder - Sepsis - lipopolysaccharide activates coagulation - cancer - trauma - burns - pregnancy associated complications - releases tissue factor - aortic aneurysms - cavernous hemangiomas - snake bite - exogenous toxin precipitates DIC ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Bleeding at multiple sites (e.g. IDC, incision) - widespead bleeding

title: Vaccine induced thrombosis and thrombocytopenia (VITT)
content: # Vaccine induced thrombosis and thrombocytopenia (VITT) ## Background - adverse effect in certain adenoviral vector COVID-19 vaccines - AZ/Vaxzevria (ChAdOx1) - Johnson and Johnson - Types - VIPIT - Vaccine induced prothrombotic immune thrombocytopenia - TTS - [Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP)](Thrombotic%20Microangiopathy%20(TMA).md#Thrombotic%20Thrombocytopenic%20Purpura%20(TTP)) syndrome - Epidemiology - though may be skewed because of original studies - more common in younger females than males - risk is higher when age < 60 years ## Pathology ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%202

title: Iron deficiency anaemia
content: # Iron deficiency anaemia ## Background - Most common cause of anaemia worldwide - Iron is stored in RBCs, ferritin or hemosiderin (macrophages) - 10% of dietary iron (10-15mg/day) is absorbed in stomach, duodenum and upper jejunum - see [Iron Regulation](../../BCS/Physiology/Iron%20Regulation.md) - menstrual blood loss accounts for a large amount of iron loss in women - the most common cause of microcytic anaemia in the **developing world** is **secondary to hookworm infection (resulting in GI bleeds)** ## Pathology - Iron losses - bleeding (incl menstrual bleed) - chronic GI blood loss - chr

title: Iron Regulation
content: # Iron Regulation ## Background - 10% of daily iron (10-15mg/day) is absorbed via stomach, duodenum and upper jejunum ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231230180043.png) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240115214405.png) ## Dietary iron sources - Dietary iron includes - Heme-bound (organic) - efficient absorption (10-20%) - found in meat - this is transported into the enterocyte by HCP1 (Heme carrier protein 1) - unbound iron (inorganic) - inefficient absorption (1-5%) due to interference by tannins, phosphates, etc. - found in vegetables - needs to be reduced from ferric to ferrous sta

content: # Plummer-Vinson Syndrome ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Iron deficiency anaemia and oesophageal webs ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Anaemia of chronic disease
content: # Anaemia of chronic disease ## Background ## Pathology - Anaemia of inflammation - chronic inflammatory states (IBD, rhematological issues, infections, malignancy) - **Hepcidin** elevated by increased IL-6 - reduces iron uptake in gut and iron transfer from macrophages to bone marrow - patient is technically iron replete but erythropoiesis is **iron restricted** - this also tends not to be EPO responsive as the problem is not stimulation - hemolysins are elaborated and EBC survival is shortened - Serum iron is low in anaemia of inflammation - Anaemia of organ failure (e.g. CKD, liver failure,

title: Hemolytic anaemia
content: # Hemolytic anaemia ## Background ## Red cell destruction - RBCs are removed from the circulation at the end of their lifespan (about 120 days) - destroyed within the reticuloendothelial system (spleen, liver and bone marrow) ## Intravascular hemolysis - e.g. DIC, HUS/TTP - Plasma hemoglobin concentration increases as it is released into the vessel as RBCs lyse - positive for methemalbumin - complex of albumin and heme - schistocytes can be seen from shearing of albumin against vessel - Heme released → Haptoglobins bind to free hemoglobin → depleted haptoglobin - excess hemoglobin is filtered

title: Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH)
content: # Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria (PNH) ## Background - Epidemiology - 3-5% risk of developing leukemia - affects 1-2/million in population - young adults (median age 35-40 years) with occasional cases in childhood or adolescence - 30% will evolve FROM aplastic anaemia ## Pathology - Normal RBC surface regulators - these three are absent in PNH - Decay accelerating factor (CD55) - CD55 on surface membrane inhibits C3 convertase from forming C3b and breaking down the RBC membrane - Membrane inhibitor of reactive lysis (CD59) - CD59 on surface membrane prevents C3b from forming the membrane

title: Receptors responsible for toxidromes
content: # Receptors responsible for toxidromes ## Background ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231231114926.png) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231231114932.png) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020231231114953.png) ![](../../figures/Neurotransmitter.pdf) ## Definition ## Relevant links

title: Sympathomimetic toxidrome
content: # Sympathomimetic toxidrome ## Description - Delusions, Paranoia - Tachycardia, Hypertension → hypotension in severe toxidrome - Hyperthermia, Sweating, Piloerection - Mydriasis (big pupils) - hyperreflexia - seizures ## Drugs - Cocaine - MDMA - Ritalin - Amphetamines - OTC decongestants

title: Anticholinergic toxidrome
content: # Anticholinergic toxidrome ## Description Think of it as, what happens if parasympathetic things are blocked? Atropine poisoning - antagonist at muscarinic receptors for ACh Almost always anti-psychotics Hot as a hare: increased body temperature. Blind as a bat: mydriasis (dilated pupils) Lack of parasympathetic stimulation, so sympathetic dilation unopposed Dry as a bone: dry mouth, dry eyes, decreased sweat. Red as a beet: flushed face. Mad as a hatter: delirium. A little less febrile, hypertensive, than the other toxidromes, but more delirious Substances with anticholinergic properties com

title: Cholinergic toxidrome
content: # Cholinergic toxidrome ## Description Cholinergic - muscarinic (all the usual effects) and nicotinic (more complex MOA) receptors - Brady (or tachy from nicotinic) - Bronchospasms - Diaphoresis - Delirium - Urinary incontinence - Diarrhoea - Delayed neuromuscular paralysis (from nicotinic blockade) Treat organophosphate toxicity with atropine - 1.6g, then double every 5 minutes, up to 15g bolus, and may need infusions Up to a dose of 50g is not too unusual Pyridoxine can theoretically change enzyme conformation so the cholinergic molecules drop off But no evidence that the mortality improves

title: Paracetamol overdose
content: # Paracetamol overdose ## Background - common cause of liver failure - most common co-ingestant in an overdose - Risk factors for increased toxicity - Enzyme induction (CYP2E1 induced by EtOH, anticonvulsants) - glutathione depletion (starvation, acute illness, chronic poisoning) - Protective factors - decreased risk - OCP - hepatotoxic at >75 mg/kg (so about 3.75g for a 50kg woman) ## Pathology - Paracetamol metabolised by CYP2E1 to form reactive intermediate NAPQI - CYP2E1 can be induced by chronic EtOH consumption which results in formation of MORE NAPQI - can also occur via CYP3A4, CYP1A2

title: Serotonin Syndrome
content: # Serotonin Syndrome ## Background ## Pathology SNRI and SSRI that cause serotonin syndrome are usually venlafaxine and citalopram nowadays - >500mg citalopram is toxic - Dose toxicity well established - Can also cause QT prolongation - Venlafaxine can also cause sodium blocking toxicity - Can cause cardiac arrhythmias Can use charcoal in both these agents Tramadol + SNRI/SSRI can cause serotonin syndrome Usually the trigger is tramadol, which functions as an opioid, and a serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor Otherwise rare for other single agents to cause it - Fluoxetine relatively

title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
content: # Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome ## Background - risk factors - medication factors: sudden increase in dosage, starting a new drug - patient factors: medical illness, dehydration, exhaustion, poor nutrition, external heat load, male, young adults ## Pathology The most widely accepted mechanism by which antipsychotics cause neuroleptic malignant syndrome is that of dopamine D2 receptor antagonism. In this model, central D2 receptor blockade in the hypothalamus, nigrostriatal pathways, and spinal cord leads to increased muscle rigidity and tremor via extrapyramidal pathways. Hypothalamic D2 rece

title: Toxicology Assessment
content: # Toxicology Assessment ## The toxicology assessment 1. Risk assessment - Examination and physical findings, vitals - Drug doses 2. Decontamination - Activated charcoal useful for everything except heavy metals, alcohol, acid or alkaline ingestions - 50g in adults, or 1g/kg in paeds - Note aspiration risks - Minimal support on afterhours and night shifts - IR - usually give within 2-3 hrs - SR, given within 6-8hrs 3. Enhance elimination - Urinary alkalinsation - Dialysis - ECMO 4. Antidotes 5. Investigations 1. Paracetamol levels 2. beta HCG levels 3. AXR - good for metal tablets, especially u

title: Decontamination methods
content: # Decontamination methods ## Activated charcoal Activated Charcoal - Decreases bioavailability pores increase surface area - weak electrostatic forces (desorbs as well as adsorbs) - aqueous with repeat dose to avoid fluid shifts - doesn't work for metals, strong acids or alkalis - avoid if perforation ## Gastric Lavage - Gastric Lavage - Not part of routine management — rarely used - Within one hour of ingestion (unless anticholinergic) - for drugs with significant risk of harm - tablet has to pass up the lumen - caution with verapamil, diltiazem and propranolol (consider atropine first) prote

title: Amniotic fluid embolism
content: Amniotic fluid embolism This is a catastrophic obstetric complication where immediate recognition and active management can be life saving. AFE is the leading cause of direct maternal mortality despite low incidence of 5.4 per 100,000 with 15% case mortality rate in Aust/NZ study Presentation occurs post-partum in >50% cases however can be at any stage of labour and even with ECV Hypotension, haemorrhage and coagulopathy in >70% cases Diagnosis: clinical - acute hypotension or cardiac arrest, acute hypoxia and coagulopathy in the absence of any other potential explanation post mortem - presenc

title: Psoriatic arthritis

title: Chemotherapeutics
content: # Chemotherapeutics ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240109161207.png) ## Phases of the cell cycle and Cell Cycle-specific (CCS) drug MOA 1. G0: Cell arrest (quiescent) - *in this stage, cells are resting and performing normal duties without replicating* - in this stage, cells are less prone to chemotherapy agents as **chemotherapeutics aim to disrupt the replication cycle** however **anything that DIRECTLY DAMAGES DNA will kill in G0, even at rest** - **Alkylating agents** - Oxazaphosphorines - Cyclophosphamide: Attacks guanine N7 - Ifosfamide - Nitrogen mustards - Chlorambucil - Melph

title: Antimetabolite agents
content: # Antimetabolite agents ## Background - To understand antimetabolite agents, you need to understand the processes which underlie the replication of DNA - Antimetabolites **inhibit the use of a metabolite** by either - incoporating of chemically altered nucleotides - masquerade as a purine - masquerade as a pyrimidine - depleting supply of deoxynucleotides needed for DNA replication and cell proliferation - inhibiting folate use - inhibiting ribonucleotide reductase - These agents act in the S-phase, where there is **rapid synthesis of nucleotides** ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%202024010

title: Folate biochemistry
content: # Folate biochemistry ## Background - Folate necessary for DNA synthesis - Folate sources include - green leafy vegetables, legumes, cereals, yeast - liver ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240109174840.png) ## Folate role in DNA synthesis 1. Tetrahydrofolate (THF) receives methylene group (1-carbon transfer reaction) from serine to produce 5,10-CH2-THF - -CH2- transferred by thymidylate synthase for dUMP → dTMP reaction - dTMP used in purine synthesis -

title: Creatine Kinase
content: ## Background - isoenzymes differ in subunit composition - derived from different alleles of the same gene - creatinine kinase have 3 isoenzymes - CK-MM → skeletal muscle predominant - used in diagnosis of Duchenne muscular dystrophy - CK-MB → cardiac muscle - Used in diagnosis of myocardial infarct - CK-BB → brain, smooth muscle, lungs - Depends on - age, sex, muscle mass ## Function of CK - Combines creatine with ATP to produce creatine phosphate (high energy storage compound present in tissue) - catalyses the breakdown of creatine phosphate into ATP by creatine kinase ## Normal reference ra

title: Valproate induced hyperammonaemia
content: # Valproate induced hyperammonaemia ## Background - VPA used as an anti-epileptic - Risk factors - Age < 24 months - concomitant neurologic or metabolic disorders - multiple AEDs used ## Pathology - VPA is metabolised through three pathways 1. Primary glucouronidation 2. beta oxidation - carnitine essential for this step - where carnitine is depleted, sigma-oxidation is undertaken 1. sigma-oxidation - when this takes place, 4-en-VPA levels are elevated (2-propyl-4-pentenoic acid) - 4-en-VPA inhibits carbamoyl phosphate synthetas I (CPS) and impairs the urea cycle → this leads to hyperammonaemi

title: NK Cell lymphoma
content: # NK Cell lymphoma ## Background - aggressive malignancy of [NK cell](../../BCS/Immunology/NK%20cell.md) origin - minority from T-cell lineage - Two classic forms - extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma (nasal type) - 80% from nose/upper aerodigestive tract - 20% in non-nasal areas (skin, GIT, testis, salivary gland) - aggressive NK cell leukemia - rarely ## Pathology - Genes involved - [JAK-STAT pathway](../../BCS/Physiology/Cellular%20physiology/JAK-STAT%20pathway.md) - RNA assembly - epigenetic regulation - tumour suppressor - Lymphoma cells express the typical immunophenotype of CD2+, surface CD3–

title: Cytokine storm
content: # Cytokine storm ## Background - First used to describe the engraftment syndrome of acute [Graft vs Host Disease (GvHD)](../Haematology/Graft%20vs%20Host%20Disease%20(GvHD).md) - differs from [Cytokine release syndrome (CRS)](Cytokine%20release%20syndrome%20(CRS).md) - first used to describe the side effects following an muromonab-CD3 infusion - Historial infection driven cytokine storms - Plague/black death → alveolar macrophages releasing excessive cytokines - H1N1 1918 pandemic - marked pulmonary inflammation - Historial targeted therapies to cytokine storm abrogation - [IL-6 antibody](../.

title: Antigen markers
content: # Antigen markers ## Ki-67 (MKI67) - Encoded by KGI67 gene - associated with cellular proliferation and ribosomal RNA transcription - used as a cell marker for proliferation - Uses - Breast cancer - high Ki67 suggests greater benefit from adjuvant chemotherapy

title: NK cell
content: ## NK Cell - shared ontogent to T-cells, arise from the common lymphoid progenitor - ID2 and E4BP4 commits to NK-cell lineage - NOTCH and RUNX commits to T-cell lineage - NK cells are at the blurred interface of innate and adaptive immune systems - rapid proliferation (innate) but memory function (adaptive) - NK cells predominantly develop in non-nodal sites (e.g. liver, bone marrow) - express cytotoxic molecules - granzyme B, perforin - express CD2, CD3e, CD16, CD56, CD94, KIRs - KIRs bind HLA class I molecules as ligands → will tranduce inhibitory signal → protect normal cells expressing HL

title: JAK-STAT pathway
content: The Janus kinase/signal transducers and activators of transcription (JAK/STAT) pathway is important for hematopoiesis and immune development. In extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, STAT3 of the JAK/STAT cascade is the most frequently mutated genes followed by TP53, JAK3, JAK1, and SOCS1.[[12]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559207/#)[[13]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559207/#)[[14]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559207/#) STAT3 mutations promote STAT3 phosphorylation and transcriptional activity of STAT3 in the absence of cytokines. title: Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS)
content: # Myelodysplastic Syndromes (MDS) ## Background - stem cell disorder in older people - increased risk from previous ctx/rtx ## Pathology - MDS precursor states (i.e. NOT MDS YET) - CHIP (clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential) - with age, mutations in hematopoietic precursor cells leading to clonal populations of blood cessels - normal blood counts = CHIP - most commonly mutated in DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1 - extremely common in MDS and AML - anologous to MGUS (myeloma) or MBL (CLL) in that they precede but not all patients with this will develop MDS - approx 1% preogression per year ![](..

title: Malaria
content: # Malaria ## Background - Epidemiology - Endemic areas - young children 6-59mo), pregnant women, travellers ## Pathology ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240119001826.png) ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240119120719.png) The malaria parasite life cycle involves two hosts. During a blood meal, a malaria-infected female Anopheles mosquito inoculates sporozoites into the human host . Sporozoites infect liver cells and mature into schizonts , which rupture and release merozoites . (Of note, in P. vivax and P. ovale a dormant stage [hypnozoites] can persist in the liver (if untreate

title: Melanoma
content: # Melanoma ## Background - Epidemiology - 3rd most common cancer after prostate and breast cancer ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240119113458.png) - Risk factors - Cutaneous melanoma - Phenotype: Fair skin, red, blonde or fair hair, skin type that burns easily - UV exposure: Sun exposure, skin damage due to sunburn, sunbed use - Dysplastic naevi - Age - Immunosuppression - Acral melanoma - Mucosal melanoma - Uveal melanoma ## Pathology ### Molecular drivers of melanoma | Mutations | Cutaneous melanoma| Mucosal melanoma|Uveal melanoma| |---|---|---|---| |NRAS|X|-|-| |BRAF|X|-|-| |NF1|X|X|

title: LAG3 monoclonal antibody
content: Relatlimab • Lymphocyte-activation gene 3 (LAG3) is a cell surface molecule that is expressed on immune cells, including T cells, and negatively regulates T-cell proliferation and effector T-cell function. • Relatlimab- monoclonal antibody to LAG-3

title: T-cells
content: # T-cells ## Background - Maturation occurs in the [Thymus](Thymus.md) (primary lymphoid tissue) - immature t-cells reach cortex first from bone marrow - cortex testing must occur prior to reaching medulla - Thymus looks for 2 things in the T-cells 1. Communication of foreign antigen- cortex testing - T-cell receptor is added in the thymus - TCR needs to be able to respond to MHC-antigen complex - MHC-I - recognised by CD8+ (cytotoxic) T-cells; MHC-I on all nucleated cells - MHC-II recognised by CD4+ (helper) T-cells; found on all APC cells - needs to understand the MHC is an own component - e

title: Thymus
content: - thymus named as such because it looks like 'thyme leaves' - made of cortex and medulla In health, the cells you find in the thymus are - T-regs → some are made in thymus - CD4+ T-cells are all T-cells - Macrophages → for removal of T-cells which fail positive and negative selection - dendritic cells - thymic epithelial cells present tissue restricted antigens to T-cells via APCs such as dendrites

title: mTOR inhibitor
content: sirolimus everolimus inhibitors mTOR so stops cells from entering cell cycle immunosuppressors and tumour suppressors

title: CDK4 and CDK6
content: # CDK4 and CDK6 ## Background - CDKs drive cell division - cyclin D1 binds CDK4 and CDK6 - this is driven by extracellular proproliferative or inhibitory signals - excessive cyclin D-CDK4/6 activity results in uncontrolled tumour cell proliferation - Cyclin D1 - PRAD1 oncogene expresses cyclin D1 in parathyroid adenoma - BCL1 oncogene in B-cell leukemia - Cyclin D-CDK4/6 regulation occurs by cell cycle inhibitors - INK inhibitors - p15, p16, p18, p19 - inhibit cycline D - KIP/CIP inhibitors (p27, p57, p21) - inhibit CDK2 complexes and help assemble cycline D-CDK4/6 - Inhibition of CDK4/6 has b

title: CDK4 and CDK6 inhibitors
content: Palbiciclib Ribociclib Abemaciclib useful in HR positive BCa

title: Gene Therapy
content: # Gene Therapy ## Background ## Definition - Treatment of a genetic disease by transferring genetic material (DNA or RNA) into cells of patients - mRNA vaccines not a very good example of gene therapy as it doesn't target genetic disease - Several different gene therapy stratgies - Gene augmentation therapy (1970s) - problem is underlying genetic variant with loss of function - reduced production of functional protein - previously through introduction of exogenous protein - e.g. in inborn errors of metabolism - introduction of functional copy of gene (e.g. with viral vector) - Gene silencing (

title: Monoclonal antibody
content: # Monoclonal antibody ## Background - A B-cell clone is fused to a myeloma cell (to make it immortal) - this produces antibodies - these antibodies have high affinity for a an epitope of an antigen (e.g. a protein expressed by a parasite) - Due to high specifcity - fewer off-target effects than small molecule drugs - can be produced in bioreactors ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240128145818.png) ## Definition ## Relevant links

title: JAK inhibitor
content: # JAK inhibitor - broadest efficacy signal in rheumatology since corticosteroids - [JAK-STAT pathway](../../BCS/Physiology/Cellular%20physiology/JAK-STAT%20pathway.md) is key mediator of cellular signalling - JAK1 and JAK2 deficiencies not compatible with life - responsible for loads of pathways ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240203104936.png) TNF vs. tofacitinib safety study - Pfizer and FDA - TNFi + MTX vs. Tfac 10bD MTX vs. tofac 5BD + MTX - increased PE in 10mg BD tofacitinib + MTX group → transitioned to 5mgBD + MTX arm - Eligible: mod-severe RA, age >= 50, >=1 CV RF, inadequate MTX

title: Status Epilepticus
content: # Status Epilepticus ## Definition - continuous seizure activity for >= 5 minutes without return of consciousness - recurrent (>=2) seizures without intervening period of neurological recovery ## Background - rapid seizure control is important because: - limits seizure-induced neuronal injury (animal studies; also associated with better outcomes in humans) - benzodiazepine effectiveness decreases over time (likely to changes in GABA subunits with ongoing seizure activity) - prevent ‘duration-dependent kindling’ and cytokine-mediated effects (experimental models) ## Differential - pseudoseizur

title: Hypertension in pregnancy
content: ## Hemodynamic changes in pregnancy - Increased preload - increased blood PV - Reduced afterload - decreased in SVR - decreased AT-II - increased estrogen/progesterone - Increased HR - Overall → increased cardiac output ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240203184029.png) - year-long map of MAP in small observational study - associated with increased hormone production in early pregnancy ## Classification of hypertension 1. Pre-existing hypertension - HTN before 20+0 weeks gestational age 2. Gestational hypertension - HTN after 20+0 weeks gestation 3. Pre-eclampsia - HTN, generally after 20+

title: Pre-eclampsia
content: # Pre-eclampsia - emergency requiring close monitoring ## Postulates for management 1. Blood pressure control 1. related to utero-placental dysfunction - i.e. placenta needs more nutrients than it is getting → oxidative stress and ROS 2. blood pressure is also a manifestation of pre-clampsia - does not CAUSE it, but should be controlled 2. Fetal condition monitoring 1. CFHR monitor 3. Eclampsia prophylaxis 1. Magnesium sulfate 4. Timing of delivery 1. Discussion with OB colleagues based on gestational age and patient chronic conditions 5. Postpartum care 1. close monitoring of BP up to 6 weeks

title: VTE in pregnancy
content: # VTE in pregnancy ## Background - difficult to diagnose due to pregnancy related changes and clinical signs of VTE - Signs occur normally in pregnancy - elevated HR (PE) - LL swelling (DVT) - dyspnoea (PE) - VTE epidemiology - 1-2 cases per 1000 - peripartum - 5-10-fold risk - 6-week postpartum - 15-35 fold higher risk compared to non-pregnancy ## Sign/symptoms - unilateral leg swelling - typically left leg more common due to compression of left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery ## Investigations ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240203185453.png) - Compression ultrasound/

title: Thyroid disease in pregnancy
href: IM/Obstetrics and Gynaecology/Thyroid disease in pregnancy.html
content: - pre-existing thyroid disease - more T4 needed when pregnant (30-35%) - double dose on sat/sun if taking daily - revert back to normal after delivery - target TSH <= 2.5 - Hyperthyroidism - carbimazole switch to PTU in 1st trimester - stop treatment and wait+watch thyroid function provided biochemically euthyroid - hypothyroidism - primary - subclinical - treatment targets - euthyroid but positive TPO antibodies - 10% are TPO positive, role in prgenancy unclear TSH decreases (estrogen increases thyroid binding globulin → increased thyroid hormone production) - TSH goes down in first trimester

title: ECG interpretation
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240205181243.png) - 53M long standing HTN and atypical chest pain - Sinus: QRS preceded by p-waves → regular sinus rhythm - sV1 + rV5/6 > 35mm → LVH - ST depressions in V4-V6, I, aVL - asymmetric TWI in V5-V6 and TWI in II, III, aVF - LVH with strain pattern - LVH criteria - Solokolov - SV1/2 + RV6 > 35 - Cornell - SV3 + rAVL > 22 (different b/w male and female) - ST and TW changes in LVH with strain - ST depression (1-2mm) in V5,V6 - TWI is symmetric - x > Y - ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240205181655.png) - indicative of hypertensive heart disease

title: Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS)
content: ## Background - [Multiple Myeloma (MM)](Multiple%20Myeloma%20(MM).md) always preceded by Monoclonal Gammopathy of Undetermined Significance (MGUS) - Small case study 241 (Mayo clinic) had 11% progression to MM, [Waldenstroms Macroglobulinaemia (WM)](Waldenstroms%20Macroglobulinaemia%20(WM).md) and [Light chain amyloidosis (LA)](Light%20chain%20amyloidosis%20(LA).md) - MGUS associated with autonomic neuropathy, osteoporosis and fractures independent of other disorders - MGUS progress - Risk of MGUS progression - M-protein > 15g/L, non-IgG MGUS (e.g .IGA), SFLC ratio < 0.25 or >1.65 - Number of

title: Plasma cell dyscrasia
content: ## Subtypes - Plasma cell myeloma = [Multiple Myeloma (MM)](Multiple%20Myeloma%20(MM).md) - variants of plasma cell myeloma - Smouldering myeloma - Non-secretory myeloma - Plasma cell leukemia - Plasmacytoma (solitary/extraosseus) → single lump of myeloma - Monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease - [Light chain amyloidosis (LA)](Light%20chain%20amyloidosis%20(LA).md) - Light/heavy chain deposition disease - Plasma cell neoplasms with paraneoplastic syndromes - [POEMS](POEMS.md) - Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, Monoclonal plasma cell disorder, Skin changes - TEMPI syndrome -

title: POEMS
content: # POEMS ## Diagnosis - Mandatory criteria - Polyradiculoneuropathy - Plasma cell disorder - Additional major criteria - Castleman disease - Sclerotic bone lesion - VEGF elevation - Minor criteria - Organomegaly - Extravascular volume overload - Endocrinopathy - Skin changes - Papilledema - Thrombocytosis/polycythemia - Other SSx - clubbing, weight loss, hyperhidrosis, PH, Restrictive LD, thrombotic diatheses, diarrhoea, low vitamin B12 values

title: Hyperviscosity syndrome
content: # Hyperviscosity syndrome ## Background ## Pathology - Too much protein → seen in - IgM pentameric → most viscosity effect - if total IgM > 50 - [Waldenstroms Macroglobulinaemia (WM)](Waldenstroms%20Macroglobulinaemia%20(WM).md) - IgA dimeric - if total IgA > 70 - IgA [Multiple Myeloma (MM)](Multiple%20Myeloma%20(MM).md) - IgG monomeric - if total IgG > 100 - [Multiple Myeloma (MM)](Multiple%20Myeloma%20(MM).md) - Too many cells ## Clinical features - headache - visual changes - mucosal haemorrhage - high output CCF ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - Fundoscopy with flame haemorrhage a

title: Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation (ASCT)
content: # Autologous Stem Cell Transpltation High dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue 1. Stem cell collection with apheresis and G-CSF to mobilise 2. Stem cells crytopreserved with control rate freezeing and DMSO 3. Store cells with liquid nitrogen 4. Give patients high dose chemotherapy 1. MM → melfolan 2. Lymphoma - BEAM 5. Thaw cells and give back to patient 6. Care patients who have been myeloablated 1. profoundly neutropenic, pancytopenic, anaemia 2. Significant multiorgan toxicity ## Indications - Standard of care indications - Multiple myeloma → but not curative, no survival bene

title: Hematopoietic stem cell transplant
content: # Hematopoietic stem cell transplant ## Background - [Autologous Stem Cell Transplantation (ASCT)](Autologous%20Stem%20Cell%20Transplantation%20(ASCT).md) - stem cells transplanted from yourself, collected prior to intense chemotherapy - Benefits: - lower risk of life-threatening c omplications - no risk of [Graft vs Host Disease (GvHD)](Graft%20vs%20Host%20Disease%20(GvHD).md) - no need for immunosuppressive therapy to prevent GvHD and graft rejection - more rapid immune reconstitution → lower risk of opportunistic infections - rarely failure of graft - treatment mortality < 5% in most studie

title: Reactive Arthritis
content: # Reactive Arthritis ## Background - sterile arthritis following remote infection - ## Pathology - Genitourinary - Chlamydia trachomatis - GIT - Shigella - Salmonella - Campylobacter - Yersinia - Other - E.coli - C. pneumoniae (K.?) ## Clinical features - Preceding infection by usually 1-4 weeks - urethritis/STI, gastroenteritis - Arthritis - Assymetric, oligoarticular, lower limb - enthesitis - Dactylitis - Sacroilitis - Classical triad (Reiter's syndrome) - arthritis/urethritis/conjunctivitis - Uncommon - Extra-artoicular manifestations - Skin: Keratoderma blenorrhagica, circinate balanitis,

title: IBD Spondyloarthritis
content: # IBD Spondyloarthritis ## Background - 10-20% of IBD patients - more common with - other extraintestinal features - e.g. uveitis - complications of bowel disease - large bowel involvement (e.g. Crohn's) - Tw osubtypes - Axial - may be asymptomatic in 20%; PsA type involvement - Peripherial - assymetric oligoarthritis in lower limbs ## Pathology ## Clinical features - peripheral arthritis - acute oligoarticular and lower limb - deformities/erosions rare - can be self-limiting, related to IBD flares - rarely - progressive, and independent of IBD activity - other SpA features .eg. enthesitis - A

title: DMARDs
content: Disease modifying anti-rheuatic drugs ## Conventional Synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs) - methotrexate - RA: 25mg OW; S/C if >15mg/wk due to variable oral absorption - <5% have to stop due to adverse effects - ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240207122534.png) - MOA: - ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240207122555.png) - anchor drug for DMARD regimen - reduces cardiovascular risk - ## Methotrexate toxicities - Gastrointestinal discomfort (most common) - liver enzyme abnormalities (role of fibroscan) - can lead to cirrhosis in rare cases - hematological - neutropenia - opportunistic infection

title: Drug Hypersensitivity and Anaphylaxis
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240207125107.png) ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240207125112.png) Assessing allergic drug hypersensitivity 1. title: Axial spondyloarthritis
content: # Axial spondyloarthritis ## Background - inflammatory disorders which involve predominantly the axial skeleton, possibly with peripheral joint involvement and characteristic extra-articular features - Are defined according to: 1. Axial Spondyloarthritis (Ax SpA) 1. Predominantly affecting axial skeleton – spine, sacro-iliac joints 2. With radiographic sacro-iliitis → [Ankylosing spondylitis](Ankylosing%20spondylitis.md) 3. Without radiographic sacro-iliitis → [Non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis](Non-radiographic%20axial%20spondyloarthritis.md) 2. Peripheral Spondyloarthritis 1. Predomin

title: Wide Complex Tachycardias (WCT)
content: # Wide complex tachycardia ## Definition - QRS >= 110ms (not 3 small squares!) - activation of ventricles NOT by functional AV conduction system --- ## VT vs SVT 1. A - axis - Northwest axis (-90 to -180deg) is highly specific for VT (90%) - dominant R wave in aVR suggests NW axis 1. B- Broad complexes - QRS > 200ms has Sp 85-90% for VT - QRS < 200ms has overlap between VT and SVT with abberance 1. C- Concordance - Precordial concordance - i.e. all RS or all QR complexes - all positive or all negative 1. D- Dissociation (AV dissociation) - P and QRS complexes at different rates - Capture beats

title: ICD modes
content: # ICD modes ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240209124329.png) - MADIT-RIT study by Moss et al (NEJM 2012) - Conventional arm shock criteria - 2.5s of >-170bpm - 1x of >=200bpm - High-rate shock criteria - i.e., shock only if semisustained - 2.5s of >=200 bpm - Duration delay shock criteria - i.e. minimise shock - 60s of >=170bpm - 12s of >=200bpm - 2.5s of >= 250bpm - Outcomes of study - High-rate shock criteria is superior to conventional therapy (and even delayed therapy) - 55% reduced chance of death and no significant increase in syncope events Therefore settings for IACD should be (b

title: Thyroid physiology
content: # Thyroid physiology 1. TRH from paraventricular nucleus → stimulates pituitary thyrotropin 1. subject to negative T3 feedback 2. T3 is active thyroid hormone 3. T4 is converted to T3 by deiodinase enzyme 4. somatostatin sensors in pituitary negatively regulate thyrotropin 2. thyroglobin produced by thyrosite → secreted into blood 3. iodide transported actively into thyrocyte by Na+-I--symporter 1. TSH-TSHR binding assists this 4. iodide tranported to follicular lumen by Pendrin 5. TPO in lumen will 1. oxidise iodide 2. bound to tyrosine residues 3. coupling of iodinated tyrosines will form T4

title: Drug related thyroid conditions
content: # Drug related thyroid conditions 1. Pituitary gland 1. drugs excrete action by thyrotropin suppression 2. hypophysitis 3. reduced T4 → T3 conversion 2. Action on thyroid - autoimmunity - iodine induced hyperthyroidism - thyroiditis - reduced T3/T4 release - peripheral action - increased/reduced binding protein - Increased - reduced free thyroid hormone; may not be an issue if eurthyroid because increased thyroid hormone synthesis can increase - Oestrogen/SERM - Methodone, heroin - Mitotane, 5FU - decrease BP - androgen, glucocorticoid, niacin - if on thyroid replacement, may need to reduce do

title: Anti-CD52
content: # Anti-CD52 ## Uses - Typical uses - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - humanised anti-CD52 MAb ## Pharmacokinetics ## Risks, Benefits and considerations - Benefits - Risks - Black box warning?? - Considerations - Side effects - Autoimune disease - most common side effect, can happen YEARS after treatment - depletes autoreactive lymphocytes rapidly → immune suppression induced - immune reconstitution afterwards → B-cell recovery → loss of immunological tolerance to self antigen → overproduction of autoimmune cells and autoantibodies → B-cell mediated autoimmunity - similar mechanism in anti-

title: Sick Euthyroid
content: # Sick Euthyroid ## Background ## Pathology - rapid inhibition of Type 1 deiodinase enzyme → reduction in T3 levels rapidly - T3 level drop → rT3 level increase - unclear clinical siginficance of rT3 - in severe illness → T4 and fT4 levels reduce - Recovery phase - TSH can be transiently elevated, but <10 → TSH > 10 suggests primary hypothyroidism - if fT3 raised → should not be expecting this in sick euthyroid → primary hyperthyroidism - fT3/fT4 low, TSH < 10 → severe NTI/recovery phase, but central hypothyroidism may need excluding ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigation

title: OMI-NOMI paradigm
content: - Shift in diagnostic occlusive MI - How the studies are written - angiogram occluded → ECG is 'OMI' - angiogram open → but could have been occluded DURING ECG but not occluded during angiogram - 33% of the time open - 20% of time is fully open with TIMI3 flow (completely open) - ECG does not measure stenosis - it measures PERFUSION - e.g. ECG at time of chest pain - subtle hyperacute T-waves. In angiogram suite, the artery is open. How do we tell if the artery was closed or not at time of ECG? - Use a peak troponin cutoff (as a reflection of infarct size) - cTrI 10ng/mL cutoff to distinguish

title: Actinic (Solar) Keratosis
content: # Actinic (Solar) Keratosis ## Background - common premalignant skin lesions developing as a result of chronic sun exposure ## Pathology ## Clinical features - small, crusty or scaly, lesions - may be pink, red, brown or the same colour as the skin - typically on sun-exposed areas e.g. temples of head - multiple lesions may be present ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240213005128.png) ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management 1. Secondary risk prevention - sun avoidance, sun cream 1. Management of skin disorder - fluorouracil cream: typically a 2 to 3 week course. The skin wil

title: Acute Intermittent Porphyria
content: # Acute Intermittent Porphyria ## Background - rare autosomal dominant condition - Precipitants - barbituates, sulfonamides, chloroquin, griseofulvin, diphenyl hydantoin - acute infections - EtOH - Onset - adolescence - F > M ## Pathology - defective porphobilinogen deaminase (needed for haem biosynthesis) - toxic accumulation of delta aminolaevulinic acid and porphobilinogen - increased PBG and d-ALA in urine → 'port wine' colour to urine on standing ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240213010833.png) ## Clinical features - abdominal and neuropsychiatric symptoms - 20-40 year olds - more c

title: Venous ulcer
content: # Venous ulcer ## Background ## Pathology ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214074728.png) - normal veins ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214074738.png) - venous backflow due to valvular incompetence - backflow of blood into tissue and extravasation into tissues - poor O2 transfer to skin and toxin removal → skin metabolism affected → ulceration ## Clinical features - inverted chambpagne bottle due to dermatofibrosis - brown pigmentation from hemisiderosis - irregular margin, shallow nature, and gater area above malloelus, below knee ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214074

Compression - specialised garmets

title: Arterial ulcer
content: # Arterial ulcer ## Background ## Pathology - can occur with mixed venous-arterial disease - 10-15% of leg ulcers are of mixed aetiology ## Clinical features ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214075302.png) - sharp punched out ulcers - at or distal to ankle (but can be more proximal) - Deep - painful - Other evidence of reduced peripheral perfusion - poor capillary return - shiny, hairless skin - ABPI < 0.7 (typically) ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Lower threshold for antibiotics - ischemic environment predisposes to infection - Debride - Primary dressings - Se

title: Mixed venous arterial ulcer
content: # Mixed venous arterial ulcer ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Mixed A/V ulcers will need compression - generally safe if ABPI > 0.7 ## Prognosis

title: Diabetic (neuroischemic) foot ulcers
content: # Diabetic (neuroischemic) foot ulcers ## Background ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Usually distal or plantar foot/ties - Can be found on distal leg/ankle region - especially if pressure (e.g. top of footwear) - combination of ischemia (small vessels - pulses still palpable) and reduced sensation - Charcot's foot deformity increases risk of ulcers - prone to infection - greatest risk factor for lower limb amputation ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214075652.png) - plantar surface of foot - often beneath corns - location of friction and compression ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investi

title: Skin tear
content: # Skin tear ## Background - most common wound for elderly patients and inpatients ## Pathology ## Clinical features - Category I: Linear with no to 10% skin loss - Category II: Flap present - Category III: Flat missing, total tissue loss ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214075824.png) ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Prevention - vital! - moisturise skin - avoid trauma - Treatment - Oppose edges - can use steristrips, etc. - Non-adhesive dressings - indicate where flap is approximated on the dressing - ensures dressing is removed in direction of flap ## Prognosis

title: Zoster rash
content: # Zoster rash ## Background - 1/3 of people will have HSV - 50% of people > 80 will have HSV ## Pathology ## Clinical features - vesicular 'grape-like' lesion clustered on erythematous base - unilateral based on sensory dermatomes - Thoracic dermatime 50-70% - Cervical or ophthalmic trigeminal (~ 10-20%) - Crust in 7-10 days with full healing taking up to 1 month ### Ophthalmic zoster - scarring - loss of vision - predisposing risk factor for stroke ## Diagnostic criteria - Clinical diagnosis - can aspirate vesicles and HSV PCR ## Investigations ## Management - Antiviral - Treat secondary bact

title: Vasculitis Ulcer
content: # Vasculitis Ulcer ## Background - underlying autoimmune disease - e.g. Crohn's, RA ## Pathology ## Clinical features - associated rash - palpable purura - reddish edge - biopsy may not be diagnostic - especially if not responding to venous ulcer treatment ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214080217.png) - purplish-red edge ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - immunosuppression - steroids - initially topically - tacrolimus - can apply topically - refractory - antiTNF mAbx - inflixamab - adalimumab ## Prognosis

title: Pyoderma Gangrenosum
content: # Pyoderma Gangrenosum ## Background ## Pathology - neutrophilic dermatosis - dense neutrophil infiltration in affected tissue on biopsy - Causes - idiopathic in 50% - [Inflammatory Bowel Disease](../Gastroenterology/Inflammatory%20Bowel%20Disease.md)in 10-15% - ulcerative colitis - Crohn's - rheumatological - [Rheumatoid arthritis](../Rheumatology/Rheumatoid%20arthritis.md) - [Systemic Lupus Erythematosis (SLE)](../Rheumatology/Vasculitis/Systemic%20Lupus%20Erythematosis%20(SLE).md) - haematological - myeloproliferative disorders - [Lymphoma](../Haematology/Lymphoma.md) - myeloid leukaemias -

title: Warfarin
content: # Warfarin ## Uses - Typical uses - Off-label uses ## Pharmacodynamics - inhibits epoxide reductase preventing the reduction of vitamin K to its active hydroquinone form - this in turn acts as a cofactor in the carboxylation of clotting factor II, VII, IX and X (mnemonic = 1972) and protein C. ## Pharmacokinetics Factors that may potentiate warfarin - liver disease - P450 enzyme inhibitors, e.g.: amiodarone, ciprofloxacin - cranberry juice - drugs which displace warfarin from plasma albumin, e.g. NSAIDs - inhibit platelet function: NSAIDs Monitoring - patients are monitored using the INR (inte

title: Calciphylaxis
content: # Calciphylaxis ## Background - chronic renal disease - can occur de novo - not related to excess Ca2+ but can be found ## Pathology ## Clinical features ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214081311.png) - painful +++ - extensive necrotic ulcers and lesions - satellite lesions ## Diagnostic criteria - clinical diagnosis ## Investigations - Renal function - Calcium level ## Management - Sodium thiosulfate - used to treat nephrocalcinosis but case reports suggestive - reduces pain in 2 weeks - healing in 6 weeks - IV 10g 3/wk for 12 weeks - topica sodium thiosulfate may also be effective ##

title: Mycobacterium ulcerans (Buruli Ulcer)
content: # Mycobacterium ulcerans ## Background - transmitted by mosquito bite - vector Possum in Australia ## Pathology ## Clinical features ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214081455.png) - younger patients - no evidence of chronic venous insufficiency - necrotic scar will lift and present with a mildly painful ulcer ## Diagnostic criteria - Biopsy - PCR of biopsy for mycobacterium ## Investigations ## Management - Rifampicin/Clarithromycin for 2 weeks - Simple dressing - Sorbact can be used ## Prognosis - Ulcer deteriorates over 2-4 weeks - can become many times larger - lactoferrin release b

title: Pressure injury
content: # Pressure injury ## Background ## Pathology - lesion caused by unrelieved pressure resulting in damage of underlying tissue - 95% of all pressure injuries are preventable - MOA - Pressure - direct pressure, especially over bony prominence > 30mmHg will cause ischemia in surrounding tissue due to compression of capillary bed supplying the skin - causes hypoxia and ischemia - can occur in bed, trolley or in chair - continued moisture exposure → maceration → skin cannot function and becomes damaged - macerated skin → stick to bed linen - source: urinary incontinence, fecal incontinence, perspira

title: Direct acting antivirals
content: # Direct acting antivirals ## Uses - Typical uses - Hepatitis C - Off-label uses - Oral combination drugs with a duration of treatment of 8-12 weeks ## Pharmacodynamics ![](../../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240214190556.png) - targets in viral life cycle - cell entry, viral RNA translated to polyprotein - cleaved during/after translation by proteases by NS3/4A proteases - synthesis of new viral RNA occurs in complex with NS3/NS4/NS4A/NS4B/ Ns5A/NS5B - NS5A regulates replication, assembly of viral particle - NS5B - polymerase essential for viral replication - nucleoside/nucleotide analogue in

title: Aflatoxin
content: # Aflatoxin ## Background - found in corns and grains - likely bigger issue in non-western parts of world ## Pathology - Produced by aspergillus flavus and aspergillujs parasiticus - AFB-1 considered most toxic - Liver major site of aflatoxin detoxification (by CYP450) - Long term exposure → genetic multations and epigentic alterations → HCC formation ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240215082634.png) ## Clinical features ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Osteoporosis
content: # Osteoporosis ## Background - Bone disease affecting about 1 million Australians - 200 million people suffer from osteoporosis worldwide - 1 in 3 women over 50, 1 in 5 men will have lifetime osteoporotic fracture* - Classically associated with post –menopausal state, ageing and genetic factors - However secondary osteoporosis can occur due to medical disorders, medications, lifestyle factors - liver disease, kidney disease, hyperparathyroidism, hyperthyroidism - steroids, aromatase inhibitors (Breast cancer), androgen deprivation therapy (prostate cancer) - excess EtOH, smoking ## Pathology -

title: Acne Rosacea

title: Multiple Sclerosis

title: Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH)

title: Myaesthenia gravis
content: # Myaesthenia gravis ## Background - Autoimmune disorder - F : M = 2:1 ## Pathology - Associations - Thymoma - Autoimmune disorders - pernicious anaemia, autoimmune thyroiditis, RA, SLE - Thymic hyperplasia in 50-70% ## Clinical features - Fatiguability and progressive weakness - increased towards end of day ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations - Examination - Ice test - Tensilon test - IV edrophonium reduces muscle weakness - no longer done due to risk of arrhythmias - Blood - Anti AchR - 85-90% positive - Anti MuSK - 40% positive when Anti-AchR negative - CK - NAD (rule out myositis for

title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS)
content: # Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) ## Background - Ovarian dysfunction affecting 5-20% women ## Pathology - Hyperinsulinaemia and high LH → metabolic syndrome ## Clinical features - Amenorrhea - Hirsutism, acne - obesity - acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance) - Subfertility/infertility - Menstrual disturbances - oligomenorrhoea/amenorrhoea - ## Diagnostic criteria Rotterdam criteria - 2 of the following 3 are present: - infrequent or no ovulation (usually manifested as infrequent or no menstruation) - clinical and/or biochemical signs of hyperandrogenism (such as hirsutism, acne, or eleva
