title: Minimal change disease
content: # Minimal change disease ## Background - nephrotic syndrome, accounting for 75% of cases in children and 25% in adults. - Causes - Idiopathic (majority) - Other (10-20%) - drugs: NSAIDs, rifampicin - Hodgkin's lymphoma, thymoma - infectious mononucleosis ## Pathology - T-cell and cytokine-mediated damage to the glomerular basement membrane → polyanion loss - the resultant reduction of electrostatic charge → increased glomerular permeability to serum albumin ## Clinical features - nephrotic syndrome - normotension - hypertension is rare - highly selective proteinuria - only intermediate-sized p

title: Primary hyperaldosteronism
content: # Primary hyperaldosteronism ## Background - most commonly caused by bilateral idiopathic adrenal hyperplasia (not Conn's syndrome) - Causes - bilateral idiopathic adrenal hyperplasia: the cause of around 60-70% of cases - adrenal adenoma: 20-30% of cases - unilateral hyperplasia - familial hyperaldosteronism - adrenal carcinoma ## Pathology ## Clinical features - **hypertension** - increasingly recognised but still underdiagnosed cause of hypertension - **hypokalaemia** - e.g. muscle weakness - this is a classical feature in exams but studies suggest this is seen in only 10-40% of patients, a

title: Post-transplantation Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD)
content: # Post-transplantation Lymphoproliferative Disorder (PTLD) ## Background - PTLD incidence is highest in the first year post-transplantation, which is the time when immunosuppression is the most intense ## Pathology - Epstein Barr Virus ## Clinical features - weight loss - anaemia - lymphadenopathy ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Reduce current immunosuppressive therapy - Chemotherapy ## Prognosis

title: Skin disorders of pregnancy
href: IM/Obstetrics and Gynaecology/Skin disorders of pregnancy.html
content: Atopic eruption of pregnancy - is the commonest skin disorder found in pregnancy - it typically presents as an eczematous, itchy red rash. - no specific treatment is needed Polymorphic eruption of pregnancy - pruritic condition associated with last trimester - lesions often first appear in abdominal striae - management depends on severity: emollients, mild potency topical steroids and oral steroids may be used ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240217232645.png) Pemphigoid gestationis - pruritic blistering lesions - often develop in peri-umbilical region, later spreading to the trunk, back,

title: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (Pseudotumor Cerebri)
content: # Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (Pseudotumor Cerebri) ## Background ## Pathology - Risk factors - obesity - female sex - pregnancy - drugs - combined oral contraceptive pill - steroids - tetracyclines - retinoids (isotretinoin, tretinoin) / vitamin A - lithium ## Clinical features - headache - blurred vision - papilloedema (usually present) - enlarged blind spot - sixth nerve palsy may be present ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management 1. Weight loss - Topiramate - weight loss + CA inhibition 2. Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor - acetazolamide 3. Repeat LP 4. Surgery - optic

title: Guttate psoriasis
content: # Guttate psoriasis ## Background - children and adolescents ## Pathology - precipitated by streptococcal infection 2-4 weeks prior ## Clinical features - tear drop ('gutta') lesions on trunk and limbs - pink, scaly patches or plaques - acute onset - days ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240217235341.png) ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management - Supportive - spontaneous resolution in 2-3 months - no evidence for antibiotics - Psoriasis management - topical agents - UVB phototherapy ## Prognosis

title: Clostridioides difficile colitis
href: IM/Infectious Diseases/ID by disease/Bacterial infections/Clostridioides difficile colitis.html
content: # Clostridioides difficile colitis ## Background - gram positive rod - exotoxin production leading to pseudomembranous colitis - ## Pathology - Risk factors - PPIs - Clindamycin use - 2nd and 3rd generation cephalosporines are leading cause - normal gut flora suppressed by broad spectrum antibiotics - anaerobic gram-positive, spore-forming, toxin-producing bacillus - transmission: via the faecal-oral route by ingestion of spores - releases two exotoxins (toxin A and toxin B) that act on intestinal epithelial cells and inflammatory cells resulting in colitis ## Clinical features - diarrhoea - a

title: Sulfonylurea overdose
content: # Sulfonylurea overdose ## Background - SU overdose (e.g. gliclazide) can lead to severe and prolonged hypoglycaemia - eliminiation half life 12-20 hours ## Investigations - C-peptide and insulin levels - BGLs ## Management - Glucose infusion - if fails - octreotide - somatostatin analogue which inhibits hormonal secretion includingi nsulin - IM glucagon - dependent on glycogen stores - activated charcoal if early enough ## Definition ## Relevant links

title: Fanconi syndrome
content: # Fanconi syndrome ## Background ## Pathology - generalised reabsorptive disorder of renal tubular transport in the proximal convoluted tubule - Causes - cystinosis (most common cause in children) - Sjogren's syndrome - multiple myeloma - nephrotic syndrome - Wilson's disease ## Clinical features - type 2 (proximal) renal tubular acidosis - polyuria - aminoaciduria - glycosuria - phosphaturia - osteomalacia ## Diagnostic criteria ## Investigations ## Management ## Prognosis

title: Thalassemia
content: # Thalassemia ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020240218163007.png) # Alpha thalassemia ## Background ## Pathology - impaired alpha chain production → excessive beta chains → hemolysis - 4 genes responsible for alpha chain production - 1 gene deletion = silent carrier → aa/a or aa/a- - common in NZ maori (14%), SE asians, Africans - Hb ~ 10g/L less than normal - MCV borderline at 75-85fL - rarely will see HbH bodies (beta tetramers); some gamma tetramers in 70% - No issues with reproduction - 2 gene deletion = alpha trait → a-/a- OR aa/-- - seen in maori/polynesians - Hb - mild anaemia - MCV

title: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS)
content: # Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) ## Background - venous or arterial thrombosis and/or adverse pregnancy outcomes in presence of persistent antiphopsohplipid antibodies - epidemiology - incidence 5/100000 per year - prevalence 45-50/100000 - 5% of population may be positive for one APS antibody but a small proportion ever clinically develop APS - more common in select groups - 3% of patients with CVA - 9.5% of patients with DVT - 6% of patients with recurrent pregnancy loss - Additional thrombosis risk factors - Obesity - Smoking - Surgey - Oestrogens - Pregnancy/puerperium - NSAIDs - long dis

title: Factor V Leiden (FVL)

title: Statin myopathy
content: # Statin myopathy ## Background - Large range of statin myopathy - Mylagia - Self-limiting statin-myopathy which recovers after statin withdrawal - myopathy persisting post-cessation (autoimmune process) - frank muscle necrosis - Rhabdomyolysis (typically onset <48h) - IIM trigger - linked with anti-HMGCR antibiotics - Contributing medications to worsening statin myopathy - Fibrates - gemfibrozil more than fenofibrate - cyclosporine - Azoles (antifungals) - macrolides (azithromycin, erythromycin, etc.) - HIV protease inhibitors - verapamil - amiodarone - nefazodone - Grapefruit juice

title: Immune mediated necrotising myopathy (IMNM)
content: # Immune mediated necrotising myopathy (IMNM) ## Background - Necrotising myopathy associated with - malignancy - active viral infection - drugs (statins) - genetic myopathies (muscular dystrophy) - IMNM - IMNM subtypes - Anti-SRP (signal recognition particle) autoantibodies - Anti-HMGCR (3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA reductase) autoantibodies - direct damage to HMG coA reductase receptors on muscles → CK release high (similar to statin effect) - will result in increased enhancement of semimembranosus because it directly attacks the muscle - Seronegative (20-30%) - probably don't know whats c

title: HAE
content: HAE causes recurrent episodes of angioedema in the upper respiratory, gastrointestinal tract or in subcutaneous tissues. Acute episodes of angioedema may be triggered by infection, stress, menstruation, surgery, dental work, trauma and some medicines (including oestrogen-containing contraceptives and ACE-inhibitors) or may have no clear trigger. HAE is a rare autosomal dominant condition in which C1 esterase inhibitor levels are reduced (HAE type I) or poorly functional (HAE type II). HAE is diagnosed by the finding of low C1 esterase inhibitor level or function. C4 level is also low during ep

title: Beta blockers
content: # Beta blockers - Cardioselective beta blockers - less likely to cause bronchoconstriction - better for respiratory disease - bisoprolol, metoprolol, atenolol - vasodilating beta blockers - binds to alpha1 as well and blocks peripheral vascoconstriction (i.e. causes vasodilation) - less metabolic side effects - better for T2DM - e.g. carvidelol - Water soluble beta blockers - less likely to cause. CNS effects (e.g. sleep difficulty) - excreted through kidneys; dose reduction in renal function. - e.g. atenolol, nadolol - lipid soluble beta blockers - liver metabolised, better for poor renal fun

content: ## Set the scene ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250416224946.png) - early - EBV, meningoencephalitis symptoms - Immune conditions, immune activation - Less severe/troublesome - CD4 < 200, opportunistic/AIDS-defining infections - Likelihood for developing AIDS - CD4+ < 250, viral load >55000, 87% will develop aids in 3 years - high viral load with normal CD4+ count has 1/3 chance in developng aids in 3 years - Major AIDS defining conditions - PJP - Kaposi's sarcoma - Oesophageal candidiasis - HIV Wasting disease - Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) - NHL - CMV - Cryptococcosis - HIV enceph

XX is a XX year old gentleman when (complexity)

content: ## Stem - ' Neck and proceed ' - ' Thyroid exam '' ## Thyroid examination - step back - anxious, tremor, tapping foot, thyroid stare - masses and scars - sip of water and examine thyroid - tongue protrusion - thyroglossal duct cyst follows with tongue protrusion, whereas a thyroid doesnt - posterior bimanual palpation of thyroid - hold one side down and palpate the other, then vice versa - swallow water whilst palpating - try to get under it - lymph node examination - percuss for thyroid extension (retrosternal dullness) - listen for thyroid bruit (increased thyroid vascularity in Graves disea

title: Endocrine - Diabetic foot exam
href: Divisional Clinical Examinations/Short case notes/Endocrine - Diabetic foot exam.html
content: '59M difficulties walking, examine lower limbs' - lower limb sensory neuropathy → foot deformity → think of charcot foot Gait assessment - walking on heels, tiptoes - Rombergs Inspection - position seated on bed - skin changes - nail bed - dry feet, cracked appearance - hair changes - skin lesions, healed ulcers, open wounds - chronic venous changes - cushingoid features - Examine soles and between toes - charcot foot, ulcer, callous - assisted footwear (DARCO), flattened arches - lower limb trophic changes, dry skin (fits with autonomic neuropathy) - palpate for pulses (DP, PT) - BSL, BP, HR

content: ## History - palpitations - exercise induced? - time course, relationship with medications - end-dose off effect in PD? - related to positional changes? Toileting? - use of mobility aids - FASF - very likely you are not independent, would not be living at home - how do they manage the stairs? - does the aid fit in the house - complications - Driving - Eyes - Glasses (multifocals increase falls risk), eye check (cataract operations reduce falls risk) - Bone health

title: HSCT notes
href: Divisional Clinical Examinations/Long case notes/HSCT notes.html
content: - Principle - high dose chemo/radiotherapy to remove the diseased bone marrow and create space for new marrow ('conditioning') - MM - melphalan (200mg/m^2) - NHL/HL - BEAM (VCNU, Etoposide, araC, melphalan) - stem cells given 24-48h to reconstitute a NEW bone marrow with donor stem cells - infusion of a CD34 cell count (pluripotent stem cell) - rescue from lethal side effects of chemoradiotherapy at the same time - autologous stem cell transplant - Indication: multiple myeloma, multiple sclerosis, systemic sclerosis, NHL, HL - autoSCT is MM SOC - obtain stem cells 1 month before beginning pro

title: Cardiology long case
href: Divisional Clinical Examinations/Long case notes/Cardiology long case.html
content: # Cardiology long case - Hypertension - Diagnosis - should do more advanced BP monitoring - 24h home BP monitoring superior - secondary hypertension workup - phaeochromocytoma - serotonin syndrome - coarctation - Treatment - Goal: SBP < 130/90mmHg - Lifestyle modification - 10kg weight loss, DASH diet, sodium reduction, physical activity, moderation of EtOH consumption, smoking cessation - Medical therapy: ACEi, CCB, Thiazides (for >65y) - avoid CCB in peripheral oedema - undiagnosed cough, dont start ACEi - but better for preventing LV hypertrophy - renal impairment, avoid diuretic - Newer th

title: Respiratory - Cystic fibrosis
href: Divisional Clinical Examinations/Long case notes/Respiratory - Cystic fibrosis.html
content: - Diagnosis - Birth or later (mec ileus, heel prick, other) - Gene (dF508, G511D), heter/homo, unknown - mode of diagnosis (heel price, sweat test) - Manifestations/complications - Respiratory - chronic suppurative airway disease (bronchitis, bronchiectasis) → ?bronchiectasis - admissions, ICU, ventilation - Microbiology and resistance patterns (pseudomonas, burkholderia, staph aureus, fungus, NTBM) - multiresistant pseud and staph wont prohibit transplant; burkholderia can induce mediastinal breakdown - pleural fungal disease is very bad - mycobacterium abscessus is worrying and may prevent

title: Case 1 - Elderly woman with weakness, nausea, lightheadedness
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 1 - Elderly woman with weakness, nausea, lightheadedness.html
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715222351.png) - Rate - 50-60s - Rhythm: Sinus (upright P-waves in I, II, III, aVF; inverted in aVR) - ST: depressed in V6 (sagging...) - T-waves: flattening in II, V2; biphasic in V3-V5 Diagnosis: Severe hypokalemia (reverse wellens waves) - potassium in 1s and low 2s resulting in this pattern → sagging of the T-wave - upward deflection isn't the T-wave, it is the U-wave! - limb leads aren't producing biphasic pattern - mainly T-wave flattening Wellens syndrome - T-wave abnormality from critical pLAD occlusion - however T-waves go UP before DOWN → this

title: Case 2 - 38M chest pain 95% pLAD
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715222656.png) Diagnosis: Wellens syndrome - pLAd occlusion - notice the Wellen's waves - T goes up then up (in V2-V4) - notice normal QT intervals

title: Case 3 - severe hypokalemia
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715222854.png) - notice the biphasic T-wave pattern, reverse of wellens

title: Case 6 - Digoxin toxicity with hypokalemia
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 6 - Digoxin toxicity with hypokalemia.html
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715222956.png) - biphasic T-wave pattern - down followed by upward secondary deflection (U-wave) - limb lead T-wave flattening pattern

title: Case 7 - 85M chest pain (high lateral MI)
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 7 - 85M chest pain (high lateral MI).html
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715223246.png) - Hx: initial call for chest pain - Rate - approx 70bpm - Rhythm - positive in I, II, III, aVF, negative in aVR - this is sinus rhythm - Axis - leftward axis - leftward axis can imply - LBBB, pacer, WPW, inferior MI (big Q-waves in inferior leads), LAFB, LVH, hyperK (ANY axis) or normal variant - note - no left bundle, no pacer spikes, no delta waves, no peaked T-waves of hyperkalemia, no evidence of LVH, no Q-waves - LAFB seen here - Intervals - PR - prolonged >200ms - 1st degree AV block - QRS - wide QRS > 120ms - DDx: BBB, pacer, WPW, v

title: Case 8 - 50M substernal pressure and left arm radiation and dyspnoea
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 8 - 50M substernal pressure and left arm radiation and dyspnoea.html
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225238.png) Differential: Acute pericarditis - no reciprocal ST depression, no ST depression in III or II, no tombstoning of ST elevation or horizontal elevation - PR segment depression in I, II, V4-V5 (downsloping) - TTE showed pericardial effusion Other findings of pericarditis - Spodick's sign - seen in lead 2 (see case 9)

title: Case 9 - Spodicks sign
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225453.png) - in 80% of pericarditis, there is a downsloping of the QRST-TP segment - due to the downsloping TP segment - entire complex appears to go down INCLUDING the TP segment ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225557.png)

title: Case 10 - STEMI
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225730.png) note no downsloping of QRTSP segment

content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225757.png) STEMI

title: Case 13 - STEMI
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225840.png) TP segments not downsloping

title: case 14 - STEMI
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225856.png) - misdiagnosed as pericarditis due to downsloping PR segment - PR downsloping NOT specific for pericarditis - reciprocal changes in I, avL - entirely flat baseline - TP is not sloping down

title: Case 15 - Pericarditis without Spodicks
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715225952.png) - 80% will have spodicks sign positive - 20% will NOT have downstoping TP segment -

title: Case 16 - ACS
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715230030.png) - downsloping PR segments III, II - however TP segments are flat - NOT downsloping -

content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250715230144.png) - look at lead II - slopes down and comes back up again

title: Case 19 - 17M persistent exertional dyspnoea
content: - nil prior medical history however intermittent palpitations - HR 170, BP 128/59, RR 16, SpO2 100%, afebrile ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719171845.png) - AF with preexcitation (WPW)

title: Case 20 - 33M intermittent chest pain on exercise
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 20 - 33M intermittent chest pain on exercise.html
content: - no cardiac hx, fhx, nil drugs - incredibly diaphoretic on examination ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719171948.png) - irregular T-waves in V2-V3 - 'reverse wellens' - V4-V6 TWI and I, aVL - intermittent chest pain, ischemic appearing T-waves → ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719172037.png) - 10 minutes later ECG ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719172051.png) - followed by VF after a shock, followed by: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719172116.png) - ST segment depression in V2-6 and lateral - morphology shows depression and **upsloping** ST segment depressio

title: Case 21 - 57M lightheaded, presyncope, dyspnoea, weakness
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 21 - 57M lightheaded, presyncope, dyspnoea, weakness.html
content: - PMHx: leukemia, DM, CHF in insulin, digoxin - last CTx 4 days earlier, last blood transfusion 3 weeks earlier - nil palpitations, angina - pale, HR 20s, borderline BP but was awake/alert - non focal neurology - otherwise cardiopulmonary Ex normal ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719172648.png) - low voltage in general - no pericardial effusion on bedside echo - PR prolongation + dropout of P-waves - LBBB appearance - QRS widening, deep S waves in V1-3, no lateral Q-waves → left bundleoid appearance (no leftward axis) - bizarre bradydysrhythmia → think hyperkalemia - normally - peaked

title: Case 22 - elderly man with dementia and weakness for 3 days
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 22 - elderly man with dementia and weakness for 3 days.html
content: - HR 28, BP 200/90 on digoxin ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719173342.png) - digoxin toxicity - U shaped 'salvador dali' wave - deep S-waves in V1-V3, not quite a leftward axis, no q-waves in lateral leads (left bundleoid) - distinct p-waves across the rhythm strip in V1 - not all conducted - PR intervals are unchanging - Mobitz II with a 3:1 conduction ratio (advanced AV block), bradydysrhythmia - K+ 6.9, SCr 2.9mg/dL, digoxin 1.9ng/mL - may be contributing by digoxin but not likely the primary cause - Rx: insulin/dextrose, salbutamol, IVF, furosemide, digibind

title: Case 23 - 83F weak, hypoglycaemia, aLOC
content: - HR 20s - treated with dextrose → improved MS but still weak ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719173925.png) - left bundle pattern - rhythm is slightly irregular - but this is coming in clumps of 2 → regularly irregular rhythm - distance between clumps are consistent - this is not AF - grouped beats are either **PACs or Mobitz** - to assess the presence of P-waves, should look at lead V1 - lead 1 probably has buried P-waves ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719174237.png) - mapped out P-waves - not CHB - PR intervals need to be RANDOMLY changing - gradual increase in PR interval

title: Case 24 - 78M weakness and lightheadedness
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719174549.png) - Mobitz I

title: Case 25 - young woman with palpitations
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 25 - young woman with palpitations.html
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719174631.png) - initially looks like a 2:1 block - PR segment is increasing - gradually increasing PR segment - mobitz I ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719174823.png)

title: Case 26 - 57M central angina and diaphoresis
href: ECG cases/ECGs/Case 26 - 57M central angina and diaphoresis.html
content: - 9:26pm ECG with 7/10 CP ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719181928.png) - possible ST depression out laterally (V5-6), T-wave flattening in aVL, aVF... - GTN and morphine given, pain persistent and repeated ECG 10 minutes later... ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719183441.png) - ongoing 7/10 chest pain - mild lateral ST segment depression, possible V3 ST depression - possible minimal ST elevation in III, aVF? - aVL looks like downsloped AT segment and TWI? aVL was flat in ECG I - chest pain continues ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719183818.png) - aVL worse - STd - II

title: Case 27 - 84F dyspnoea weakness constipation
content: - dyspnoea and weakness common presentation of ACS in elderly patients - dyspnoea most common angina equivalent in elderly patients - most present without chest pain - weakness more common at age 80-85 ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719184820.png) - wide complex tachycardia, regular - this cannot be VT - why? - needs to have rate of at LEAST 120bpm - some irregularity in V3 - do not expect irregularity unless capture beat/fusion beat - QRS complexes are very wide - >200ms - need to consider **toxic or metabolic issues** - LBBB QRSs dont tend to be that wide - impression - atrial fibri

title: Case 28 - hyperkalemia
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719190543.png) - RRWCT - regular really wide complex tachyarrhythmias - K+ 9.2 (could also be a TCA overdose) - given amiodarone → asystole and died - noted that in this case - some irregularity in QRS complexes - very wide QRS complexes TCA overdose can be misdiagnosed as VT → can result in asystole

title: Chung 1
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719192755.png) - ?normal - P - 72bpm, sinus rhythm, normal axis - p morphology normal - inverted P in r-wave is normal - qrs morphology normal, r-wave progression at V3 normal - ST segments normal, TWI in normal in III aVR This is a normal ECG p-waves positive in II, biphasic in V1 and negative in aVR NORMAL SINUS RHYTHM - normal p-wave axis, constant PR interval, constant P-wave config (above), HR 60-100 and constant PP or RR interval

title: Chung 2
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719193000.png) - ?occsional negative p-waves in lead II with short PR interval? - diagnosis: sinus arrhythmia, wandering pacemaker - seen by varying PP intervals - WAP not uncommon in healthy young individuals this is a wandering atrial pacemaker - seen by varying PR intervals and P-wave morphologies

title: Chung 3
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719193145.png) - notably TWI in leads V1-V3 with sinus arrhythmia - Diagnosis: juvenile T-wave pattern (JTWP) and high left ventricular voltage + sinus arrhythmia - inverted V1-V3 awith biphasic V4 is JTWP other normal variants of ECG - JWTP - early repolarising pattern (ERP) - athletic T-wave changes - flat/biphasic waves - short PR intervals - RAD in children and young adults - low voltage in obesity T wave inversion in V1-V3 - "juvenile pattern" with high LV voltages - youn healthy females, biphasic in V4

title: Chung 4
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719193816.png) - ST elevation in V3-V6 and I, II, aVF - inferolateral ST elevation, but also ST elevation in I? - **not ST elevations - actually J-point elevations in these leads** - sinus rhythm otherwise J-point elevation in V3-V6 - ST-T upward concavity - descending limb of T-wave is straighter - notched J-point in V4 - more prominent at a lower heart rate - ST/T-wave amplitude ratio < 0.25 suggests BER Associated with SCD 30 years down the line

title: Chung 5
content: ![](../../figures/Pasted%20image%2020250719194108.png) - II, III, aVF TW flattening - biphasic T-wave in V5 - ST elevations in V3-V4 with high J-point - however flat ST elevation - This is an athletic T-wave variant
